## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> HCI problems at KTU are typically **scenario-based, design-based, or comparative** rather than pure mathematics. The "derivations" below represent **structured analytical frameworks** that examiners award marks for. Each step is expanded to model the **KTU valuation key** pattern.

### 3.1 Derivation of Usability Metrics for a Sample Scenario

**Scenario (KTU-style problem):**
*"A team conducts a usability test on a mobile banking app. 20 users each attempt 5 transactions. The results are: 18, 19, 17, 20, and 16 users succeeded on tasks 1–5 respectively. Average task times were: 45s, 60s, 30s, 90s, and 75s. Total errors across all users: 47. Calculate (i) Effectiveness, (ii) Efficiency, (iii) Error Rate, and (iv) interpret the results."*

**Given:**
- Number of users $N = 20$
- Tasks attempted per user: $5$
- Success counts per task: $S_1 = 18$, $S_2 = 19$, $S_3 = 17$, $S_4 = 20$, $S_5 = 16$
- Average times per task: $T_1 = 45$ s, $T_2 = 60$ s, $T_3 = 30$ s, $T_4 = 90$ s, $T_5 = 75$ s
- Total errors: $E_{total} = 47$

**Step 1: Calculate total possible task completions.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Task Attempts} &= N \times \text{Tasks per User} \\
&= 20 \times 5 \\
&= 100
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* Each of the 20 users attempted 5 transactions, giving 100 total trial opportunities.

**Step 2: Calculate total successful task completions.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Successful Tasks} &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} S_i \\
&= 18 + 19 + 17 + 20 + 16 \\
&= 90
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* Sum the successes across all five tasks.

**Step 3: Compute Effectiveness $E$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{\text{Successful Tasks}}{\text{Total Attempts}} \\
&= \frac{90}{100} \\
&= 0.90 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 90\%
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* Effectiveness is the proportion of attempts that succeeded. A value of 90% is generally considered "good" in HCI usability standards (typical acceptance threshold $\geq 78\%$).

**Step 4: Compute average task time across all tasks.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{T} &= \frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} T_i \\
&= \frac{45 + 60 + 30 + 90 + 75}{5} \\
&= \frac{300}{5} \\
&= 60 \;\; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* Average time per task across all five task types.

**Step 5: Compute Efficiency $Eff$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Eff &= \frac{\text{Total Successful Tasks}}{\text{Total Time Spent}} \\
&= \frac{90}{20 \times 300} \;\; \text{(20 users, 300s total each)} \\
&= \frac{90}{6000} \\
&= 0.015 \;\; \text{tasks/second}
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* Approximately 1 successful task per 66.7 seconds.

**Step 6: Compute Error Rate $ER$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
ER &= \frac{E_{total}}{\text{Total Attempts}} \\
&= \frac{47}{100} \\
&= 0.47 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 47\%
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* Nearly half of all attempts involved at least one error — this is **unacceptably high** and indicates serious usability flaws.

**Step 7: Analytical interpretation (KTU-mandated concluding remark).**

> The app has high **effectiveness** (90%) but poor **efficiency** and alarming **error rate** (47%). The interface likely suffers from poor feedback mechanisms or confusing workflows. Recommendations: redesign error messages, add confirmation dialogs, and conduct iterative user testing per Nielsen's 5-user heuristic (see Section 4).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Interaction Styles

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU answer pattern** for any "Compare and contrast" or "Discuss the merits of..." question worth 7–14 marks.

| Criterion | Command Line (CLI) | Menu-Driven | Form-Fill | Direct Manipulation (WIMP) | Natural Language |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ease of Learning** | Poor — needs memorization | High | High | Moderate–High | High |
| **Speed of Use (Expert)** | Very Fast | Moderate | Slow | Fast | Moderate |
| **Error Proneness** | High (typos) | Low | Low | Low | Moderate (ambiguity) |
| **Flexibility** | Very High | Low | Low | High | Moderate |
| **Discoverability** | Poor | High | Moderate | High | Moderate |
| **Cognitive Load** | High | Low | Low | Low | Low |
| **Best Suited For** | Sysadmins, scripting | ATMs, kiosks | Data entry, web | Desktop apps | Chatbots, voice assistants |
| **Example** | Bash, PowerShell | Restaurant POS | Tax filing form | Windows, macOS | Siri, Alexa |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> When asked to "compare interaction styles," students often write generic descriptions without a structured comparison table. Examiners award full marks ONLY when the answer includes **at least 4 distinct comparison criteria** and a **concluding recommendation** about which style suits which context. Merely listing styles scores at most 4/14.

---

### 3.3 GOMS Model — Step-by-Step Analysis

**Scenario:** User wants to "Copy a paragraph in MS Word using only keyboard shortcuts."

| Step | Goal Operator | Method | Time (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Home key (go to beginning) | Press `Ctrl + Home` | 200 |
| 2 | Select to end of paragraph | Press `Shift + End` | 200 |
| 3 | Copy selection | Press `Ctrl + C` | 200 |
| 4 | Paste to new location | Press `Ctrl + V` | 200 |
| 5 | Cognitive overhead (recall + planning) | 4 mental operators × 1.35 s | 5400 |

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= \sum t_{\text{motor}} + \sum t_{\text{cognitive}} \\
&= (200 + 200 + 200 + 200) + 5400 \\
&= 800 + 5400 \\
&= 6200 \;\; \text{ms} \\
&= 6.2 \;\; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* GOMS predicts the expert user will complete the task in **6.2 seconds**, which closely matches empirical observations (5–7 seconds). This validates GOMS as a predictive model.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Human-Computer Interaction (HCI). List any four disciplines that contribute to HCI.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) is a multidisciplinary field focused on the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use, studying the phenomena surrounding how humans interact with computers to accomplish tasks efficiently, safely, and satisfactorily.

**Four Contributing Disciplines (1.5 marks — 0.4 each, first three get full credit, fourth is bonus):**

1. **Computer Science** — Provides the computational substrate (hardware, software, algorithms).
2. **Cognitive Psychology** — Studies human perception, memory, attention, and decision-making.
3. **Design/Ergonomics** — Focuses on user experience, aesthetics, and physical usability.
4. *(Optional)* **Sociology/Anthropology** — Examines social and cultural contexts of use.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Explain the term "usability" with any three usability goals.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Usability refers to the **extent to which a system, product, or service can be used by specified users to achieve specified goals with effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction** in a specified context of use (ISO 9241-11).

**Three Usability Goals (2 marks — 0.7 each):**

1. **Learnability:** The system should be easy to learn so that new users can rapidly begin performing tasks.
2. **Efficiency:** Once learned, the system should allow users to perform tasks quickly with high throughput.
3. **Satisfaction:** The system should be pleasant to use, fostering positive user attitudes and continued engagement.

*(Any three from: Learnability, Efficiency, Memorability, Errors, Satisfaction.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU Module Choice Format:** The KTU examination offers internal choice between two full 14-mark questions. Both options are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]*

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **major goals of HCI**. Why is HCI considered a multidisciplinary field? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **components of the human–computer interaction loop** with a neat diagram. How does feedback play a critical role? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
HCI aims to create systems that are usable, accessible, efficient, and satisfying. Its goals span multiple dimensions of quality.

**The Seven Major Goals (5 marks — 0.7 each):**

1. **Effectiveness:** The system must help users achieve their goals accurately and completely.
2. **Efficiency:** Tasks should be completed with minimum effort, time, and resource consumption.
3. **Safety:** Errors must be prevented, and catastrophic failures must be avoided, especially in critical systems (aviation, healthcare).
4. **Utility:** The system must provide the functions and features users genuinely need.
5. **Learnability:** New users should be able to start using the system quickly with minimal training.
6. **Memorability:** Casual users should be able to return to the system after a break and remember how to use it.
7. **Satisfaction:** The interaction should be enjoyable, fostering positive emotional responses and continued use.

**Why Multidisciplinary (1 mark):**
HCI draws from **computer science** (feasibility), **cognitive psychology** (mental models, attention), **design** (aesthetics), **ergonomics** (physical comfort), **linguistics** (language interfaces), and **sociology** (collaborative systems). No single discipline can fully explain or improve human-computer interaction, making multidisciplinary collaboration essential.

---

#### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks**

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(See Section 4.1 for the canonical loop diagram.)*

**Step-by-Step Explanation (4 marks — 1 mark each for four components):**

1. **Goal Formulation:** The user forms a high-level intention (e.g., "Send an email to John").
2. **Task Translation & Input Articulation:** The user translates the goal into specific actions (clicking "Compose," typing, clicking "Send").
3. **System Processing:** The computer receives the input, validates it, executes the underlying operations.
4. **Output Presentation:** The system displays feedback ("Email sent successfully!") via screen, audio, or haptic channels.
5. **Perception & Evaluation:** The user perceives the output, compares it with the goal, and decides whether the interaction was successful.

**Critical Role of Feedback (1 mark):**
Feedback is the **bridge between user perception and system state**. Without feedback, users cannot confirm whether their actions succeeded, leading to uncertainty, errors, and anxiety. Good feedback is **immediate, clear, relevant, and consistent** — for example, a green checkmark animation on a successful payment.

**Conclusion (optional, for depth):**
Donald Norman's **gulf of execution and evaluation** framework highlights that feedback helps users close the evaluation gulf — confirming whether their action matched their intention.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast any **four interaction styles** in HCI with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Fitts's Law** and **Hick's Law**. Explain their practical applications in interface design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Interaction styles define *how* users communicate with a system. Each style represents a different trade-off between learnability, efficiency, flexibility, and discoverability.

**Comparison Table (4 marks — 1 mark per style for description + 0.5 for example):**

*(Refer to the analytical matrix in Section 3.2 for the complete comparison. Below is the essential text version.)*

1. **Command Line Interface (CLI):** Text-based commands; high flexibility and speed for experts but poor discoverability. *Example:* Unix Bash, Windows Command Prompt.

2. **Menu-Driven Interface:** User selects from hierarchical lists; highly discoverable and easy to learn but rigid. *Example:* ATM menus, restaurant ordering kiosks.

3. **Form-Fill Interface:** Structured data entry; excellent for transactional tasks. *Example:* Online tax filing, job application portals.

4. **Direct Manipulation (WIMP):** Visual interaction with graphical objects (Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointers); intuitive, engaging, and supports rapid learning. *Example:* macOS Finder, Windows Explorer.

**Comparative Analysis (1.5 marks):**
CLI maximizes expert efficiency but sacrifices learnability. Menus and forms maximize learnability but restrict flexibility. Direct manipulation strikes the best balance for general-purpose computing. Natural language and multimodal styles represent the **frontier** of HCI evolution.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The choice of interaction style must align with **user expertise**, **task complexity**, and **environment of use**.

---

#### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks**

**Fitts's Law (3.5 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):**
Fitts's Law quantifies the relationship between the **time to move to a target area** and the **distance to** and **size of** that target.

**Equation (1 mark):**

$$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{A}{W} + 1\right)$$

Where:
- $MT$ = Movement Time
- $A$ = Distance to target
- $W$ = Width of target
- $a, b$ = Empirically derived constants
- The logarithmic term is called the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**.

**Explanation (1 mark):**
The further away a target is and the smaller it is, the longer it takes to acquire. Movement time increases logarithmically with difficulty.

**Practical Application (0.5 mark):**
- Make **frequently used buttons large** (e.g., "Submit" buttons in forms).
- Place **related targets close together**.
- Use **edge snap** features (taskbars at screen edges act as infinitely tall targets).
- Design **touch targets** of at least 44×44 px (Apple HIG) or 48×48 px (Material Design).

---

**Hick's Law (3 marks):**

**Statement (0.5 mark):**
Hick's Law states that the **time to make a decision** increases logarithmically with the **number of available choices**.

**Equation (0.5 mark):**

$$T = b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)$$

Where:
- $T$ = Reaction/decision time
- $n$ = Number of equally probable choices
- $b$ = Empirical constant (~150–200 ms)

**Explanation (1 mark):**
More choices = longer decision time. Doubling the options does NOT double the time, but adds a fixed logarithmic increment.

**Practical Application (1 mark):**
- **Limit menu items** to 5–7 top-level options (aligns with Miller's Law).
- **Group related choices** into submenus to reduce visible $n$.
- Use **progressive disclosure** — reveal advanced options only when needed.
- Avoid overwhelming landing pages (e.g., Amazon's mega-menus are engineered using Hick's research).

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Together, Fitts's and Hick's Laws form the **mathematical backbone of interaction design**, guiding decisions about target size, placement, and menu structure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units:** When stating Fitts's or Hick's results, always mention **bits** for Index of Difficulty and **seconds/ms** for time.
> 2. **Conflating the two laws:** Fitts's Law is about **motor movement** (clicking a target); Hick's Law is about **cognitive choice** (deciding between options). Mixing them up loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping examples:** Each law MUST be paired with at least one **real-world design application**. Abstract definitions without application = 60% marks at best.
> 4. **Omitting the diagram** in Question A(b): The interaction loop diagram is worth **2 marks** alone. Without it, full marks are mathematically impossible.
> 5. **No comparative criteria:** For "compare interaction styles," examiners allocate marks per criterion. Listing styles without comparing them earns only 2–3 out of 7.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **HCI = Multidisciplinary Bridge** between human cognition and computer logic; it is **NOT** just UI design.
- **Miller's Law:** Short-term memory holds **$7 \pm 2$ chunks** — limit chunkable UI elements accordingly.
- **Fitts's Law:** Bigger, closer targets = faster acquisition. **Apply to button design and touch targets.**
- **Hick's Law:** More choices = slower decisions. **Apply to menu depth and breadth.**
- **Interaction Loop:** Goal → Input → Processing → Output → Perception → Feedback (continuous).
- **Five Usability Goals:** Learnability, Efficiency, Memorability, Low Errors, Satisfaction (Nielsen's *N*-factors).
- **Three Pillars:** Computer Science + Cognitive Psychology + Design/Ergonomics.
- **Five Interaction Styles:** CLI, Menu, Form-Fill, Direct Manipulation (WIMP), Natural Language.
- **Norman’s Framework:** Gulf of Execution (user intent → action) and Gulf of Evaluation (system output → user understanding).
- **Multimodal Interfaces:** The future — combining voice, gesture, touch, and gaze.
- **Accessibility is non-negotiable:** Designs must comply with **WCAG 2.2** guidelines for inclusive access.
- **GOMS Model:** $T_{total} = \sum t_{\text{motor}} + \sum t_{\text{cognitive}}$ — predicts expert performance accurately.
- **Always pair formulas with applications** in KTU answers; examiners value **engineering judgment** over rote recall.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Human Characteristics - Human Consideration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Importance of Human Characteristics - Human Consideration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Human Characteristics** in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) refer to the complete spectrum of physical, perceptual, cognitive, psychological, and emotional attributes that describe a human user. These characteristics determine how efficiently, accurately, and comfortably a person can operate an interactive computing system.

**Human Consideration** is the HCI design philosophy and engineering practice of systematically accounting for, predicting, and accommodating the strengths, limitations, and behavioral patterns of real human users throughout the entire design lifecycle. It is the deliberate alignment of system design with empirical human factors data.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, Human Consideration is positioned as the **foundational pillar** of User-Centered Design (UCD). It is the "Why" behind every usability heuristic, every affordance, and every interaction paradigm taught in the course.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot:** *Human Consideration in HCI is the process of identifying, measuring, and applying knowledge about human sensory, motor, cognitive, and emotional characteristics to the design of interactive systems, ensuring compatibility between the user and the technology.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Taylored Suit" Principle

Imagine buying an off-the-rack shirt versus a custom-tailored suit.

- An **off-the-rack shirt** is built around an "average" mannequin (so-called "standard human"). It fits *nobody* perfectly — sleeves are slightly too long for some, the collar too tight for others, the cut too narrow for tall users.
- A **custom-tailored suit** begins with a tailor taking **30+ measurements** of *your* body — shoulder width, arm length, neck circumference, posture, dominant side. The cloth is cut to match *your* unique human form.

**Human Characteristics** = the 30+ measurements the tailor records.  
**Human Consideration** = the tailor's discipline of *using those measurements* while cutting the cloth.

In HCI, the interface is the "cloth," the user is the "body," and the designer is the "tailor." An interface that ignores human characteristics produces a system that is uncomfortable, error-prone, and rejected by users — just like an ill-fitting shirt.

## 1.3 Why Human Consideration is the Single Most Important Topic in HCI

Without Human Consideration, every other HCI concept collapses:

| HCI Concept | Fails Without Human Consideration Because... |
|--------------|---------------------------------------------|
| **Usability** | Cannot be measured against the wrong reference human |
| **Affordance** | Designer guesses what users perceive, instead of measuring |
| **Feedback** | Delivered at wrong cognitive/visual scale |
| **Accessibility** | Excludes users whose characteristics lie outside the "assumed norm" |
| **User Experience (UX)** | Becomes a subjective guess, not an evidence-based discipline |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** Human Consideration is *explicitly* listed as the prerequisite knowledge required for understanding interaction styles, design principles, and evaluation methodologies studied in Modules 3, 4, and 5. Mastering this topic is, in effect, securing 40–60% of the total course grade.

## 1.4 Key Physical & Cognitive Constants Every KTU Student Must Memorize

These are empirically measured human baselines that recur in KTU exam questions:

- **Working Memory Capacity:** **7 ± 2 chunks** (George Miller, 1956)
- **Shortest Noticeable Visual Latency (human flicker fusion):** ~**60 Hz** (16.7 ms/frame)
- **Simple Reaction Time (visual stimulus):** **200 ms** (range: 150–250 ms)
- **Choice Reaction Time:** **350 ms** per additional choice (Hick's Law)
- **Field of View (binocular, focused):** ~**60° horizontal**, ~**45° vertical**
- **Visual Acuity Standard (Snellen):** **6/6 (20/20)** — design baseline
- **Fitts' Law Index of Performance:** typically **4 to 10 bits/sec** for skilled pointing
- **Preferred Reading Distance:** **40–60 cm (16–24 inches)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Human Characteristics

Human characteristics are classified into **four major domains**. Every KTU question on this topic maps into exactly one of these four pillars.

### Pillar 1 — Physical & Anthropometric Characteristics
The measurable bodily dimensions of humans. These govern:
- Screen size, font size, button dimensions
- Keyboard layout (QWERTY vs. DVORAK trade-offs)
- Device weight, grip diameter, reach envelope
- Workstation height (chair, desk, monitor)

> [!NOTE]
> Designers must design for the **5th–95th percentile** of users, not the mean. The mean is unsuitable because human dimensions are not symmetrically distributed (e.g., height is right-skewed).

### Pillar 2 — Perceptual & Sensory Characteristics
How humans receive information from displays:
- **Vision:** dominant channel (~**80% of all sensory input**)
- **Audition:** critical for alerts, speech interfaces
- **Haptics (touch):** critical for touchscreens, keyboards, game controllers
- **Vestibular & proprioception:** governs motion sickness, AR/VR design

### Pillar 3 — Cognitive & Mental Characteristics
How humans process, store, and retrieve information:
- **Attention:** selective, limited, easily distracted
- **Memory:** sensory → short-term/working → long-term
- **Learning:** skill acquisition follows a power law of practice
- **Problem-solving & reasoning:** bounded rationality
- **Mental models:** user's internal conceptual model of the system

### Pillar 4 — Motor & Response Characteristics
How humans execute physical actions:
- Reaction time (simple vs. choice)
- Movement time (governed by Fitts' Law)
- Dexterity limits (Finger Fitt's index, minimum target size)
- Fatigue, Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI) thresholds

## 2.2 The Human Information Processor (HIP) Model

The classic **Stroop / Card, Moran & Newell** model treats the human as a three-stage information processor:

1. **Perceptual Processor** (senses) → encodes raw stimuli
2. **Cognitive Processor** (brain) → interprets, decides, plans
3. **Motor Processor** (effectors) → executes physical action

Each stage has **limited capacity and finite latency**. Designers must respect these bottlenecks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Law / Model | Formula | Meaning of Terms | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------|---------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Fitts' Law** (Movement Time) | $$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$ | $MT$ = movement time, $D$ = distance to target, $W$ = target width, $a,b$ = empirically fitted constants | Sizing buttons, menu items, scroll targets. Index of Difficulty $ID = \log_2(D/W + 1)$ in **bits**. |
| 2 | **Hick's Law** (Choice Reaction Time) | $$RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1)$$ | $N$ = number of equally probable choices, $a,b$ = constants | Limiting menu items, dropdown options, toolbar icons. |
| 3 | **Power Law of Practice** | $$T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-\alpha}$$ | $T_n$ = time on $n$-th trial, $T_1$ = first-trial time, $\alpha$ = learning rate ($\approx 0.2$–$0.6$) | Predicting expert user performance; GUI learnability. |
| 4 | **Memory Capacity (Miller)** | $C = 7 \pm 2$ chunks | $C$ = working memory capacity | Limit menu items, breadcrumbs, phone-number chunks, OTP digits. |
| 5 | **Stevens' Power Law (Perception)** | $$P = k \cdot I^{n}$$ | $P$ = perceived magnitude, $I$ = stimulus intensity, $k$ = constant, $n$ = modality exponent (e.g., light $\approx 0.33$, electric shock $\approx 3.5$) | Calibrating volume sliders, brightness, vibration intensity. |
| 6 | **Index of Difficulty (Fitts')** | $$ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \quad \text{[bits]}$$ | Same as above | Quantifies pointing-task complexity. |
| 7 | **Throughput (Fitts')** | $$TP = \frac{ID}{MT} \quad \text{[bits/sec]}$$ | Benchmark for motor efficiency | Comparing input devices (mouse vs. touch vs. eye-tracker). |
| 8 | **Optimal Target Size** | $$W_{\min} \approx \frac{D}{2^{k/a} - 1}$$ | Derived from Fitts' Law for given $MT$ budget | Mobile touch-target sizing (Apple HIG: 44×44 pt; Material: 48×48 dp). |
| 9 | **Effective Target Width** | $$W_e = W \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{ID}}\right)$$ | Shannon's formulation; accounts for accuracy | Refined Fitts' Law modeling. |
| 10 | **Welford's Model of Skill** | $RT \propto$ signal strength & channel noise | Signal Detection Theory | Designing error-resistant displays. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** In the exam, if a question gives you distance $D$ and target width $W$ and asks for "movement time" or "index of difficulty," the answer **must** use Fitts' Law — and you must include the **+1** inside the logarithm. Dropping the +1 is the #1 mistake students make.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Human Consideration |
|--------|------------------------------------|
| **Avionics Cockpit Design** | All alerts use at least 2 of {visual, auditory, tactile} channels, accommodating pilots under high G-forces and stress |
| **Mobile UI Design** | Touch targets sized via Fitts' Law; thumb-reach zones calculated from anthropometry (5th–95th percentile hand length) |
| **Automotive HMI** | Display brightness = Stevens' Power Law of Light Perception; latency < 100 ms to avoid driver disorientation |
| **Medical Devices** | Cognitive load limits (max 4 alarms simultaneously — Joint Commission guideline) |
| **Web Accessibility (WCAG 2.2)** | Text contrast ratios derived from human visual contrast sensitivity functions |
| **AR/VR** | Vergence-accommodation conflict mitigated using interpupillary distance (IPD) anthropometry, typically 54–74 mm |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Fitts' Law Movement Time for a Mobile Button

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A mobile app designer places a "Submit" button at distance $D = 80$ mm from the user's starting thumb position. The button is square with side $W = 10$ mm. Using Fitts' Law with $a = 50$ ms and $b = 150$ ms/bit, calculate: (i) the Index of Difficulty, (ii) the predicted Movement Time.

**Step 1 — Identify the given values**

$$
D = 80\ \text{mm}, \quad W = 10\ \text{mm}, \quad a = 50\ \text{ms}, \quad b = 150\ \text{ms/bit}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio D/W**

$$
\frac{D}{W} = \frac{80}{10} = 8
$$

This ratio is dimensionless. We add 1 inside the logarithm to prevent a log-of-zero when $D = 0$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Index of Difficulty (ID) in bits**

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(8 + 1\right) = \log_2(9)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm**

Since $\log_2(9) = \frac{\ln 9}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.1972}{0.6931} \approx 3.1699$:

$$
ID \approx 3.17\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Movement Time equation**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID
$$

$$
MT = 50\ \text{ms} + (150\ \text{ms/bit}) \cdot (3.1699\ \text{bits})
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply and add**

$$
MT = 50\ \text{ms} + 475.49\ \text{ms} = 525.49\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 7 — Final result with units and rounding**

$$
\boxed{MT \approx 525\ \text{ms}, \quad ID \approx 3.17\ \text{bits}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** It will take the user about **half a second** to move the thumb to the button. If the system is to feel "instant," the visual feedback (button highlight) must occur in **less than 100 ms** after the tap completes — otherwise the user feels the system is sluggish, even though the pointing itself is fast.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Hick's Law Applied to a Drop-Down Menu

> **Problem:** A navigation menu originally had $N = 64$ items, producing a slow choice reaction. The designer proposes a hierarchical split: a top-level of 8 categories, each containing 8 sub-items. Compare the original and proposed choice reaction times. Use $a = 200$ ms, $b = 100$ ms/bit.

**Step 1 — Original menu: N = 64**

$$
RT_1 = a + b \cdot \log_2(N + 1)
$$

$$
RT_1 = 200 + 100 \cdot \log_2(65)
$$

Evaluate $\log_2(65) = \frac{\ln 65}{\ln 2} = \frac{4.1744}{0.6931} \approx 6.0227$:

$$
RT_1 = 200 + 100 \cdot 6.0227 = 200 + 602.27 = 802.27\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Hierarchical menu: two consecutive choices of 8 each**

Total time is the **sum of two Hick's Law evaluations**, because the user makes two separate decisions in sequence:

$$
RT_2 = \left[a + b \cdot \log_2(8+1)\right] + \left[a + b \cdot \log_2(8+1)\right]
$$

$$
RT_2 = 2a + 2b \cdot \log_2(9)
$$

Evaluate $\log_2(9) \approx 3.1699$:

$$
RT_2 = 2(200) + 2(100)(3.1699) = 400 + 633.98 = 1033.98\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare**

$$
RT_2 - RT_1 = 1033.98 - 802.27 = 231.71\ \text{ms}\ \text{slower}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Hierarchy is \textbf{slower} in this case: } +231.7\ \text{ms (}\approx 29\%\text{ increase)} }
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Insight (Hick's Law HCI lesson)**

Hick's Law says *each decision costs time*. Hierarchical menus **add decisions**; they are only faster if the total decision-count drops significantly (e.g., splitting 64 into 4 categories × 4 sub-items × 4 sub-sub-items, but only if the user knows the exact path — the "recognition vs. recall" trade-off).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Human Characteristic → Design Response

| Human Characteristic | Limitation | Quantified Value | Design Response |
|----------------------|------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Working Memory** | Limited slots | $7 \pm 2$ chunks | Chunk information; use progressive disclosure |
| **Visual Acuity** | Drops with age (>40 yrs) | Loss of near focus | Larger fonts; high-contrast mode |
| **Reaction Time** | ~200 ms simple | 150–250 ms range | Provide loading feedback within 100 ms |
| **Color Discrimination** | ~8% males red-green deficient | Deuteranopia prevalence | Never use color as the only signal; use shape + label |
| **Finger Width** | 5th–95th percentile | 16–25 mm (adult index finger pad) | Minimum touch target 9 mm × 9 mm (ISO 9241), 44×44 pt (Apple) |
| **Auditory Threshold** | Hearing loss > 25 dB common in elderly | Frequency-dependent | Visual reinforcement of all audio alerts |
| **Attention Span** | Easily divided | ~7 sec on single task | Avoid interruptions; batch notifications |
| **Movement Time** | Increases with distance | $MT = a + b \log_2(D/W+1)$ | Place primary actions close to the user's resting finger |
| **Reading Speed** | ~200–250 wpm average | 250 wpm screen | Concise copy; use icons + short labels |
| **Fatigue** | RSI after sustained input | Threshold ~4–6 hrs of continuous typing | Auto-save, ergonomic break reminders |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**"Define Human Consideration in HCI. Why is it called the foundation of user-centered design?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Human Consideration in HCI is the systematic process of identifying, measuring, and applying knowledge about human sensory, motor, cognitive, and emotional characteristics to the design of interactive systems, ensuring compatibility between user and technology.

It is called the foundation of user-centered design because **(i)** it provides the empirical baselines (e.g., $7 \pm 2$ memory, Fitts' Law, 200 ms reaction time) against which usability is measured, **(ii)** it ensures the design serves *real* users rather than an abstract "average" or the designer themselves, and **(iii)** all downstream HCI activities — interaction styles, prototyping, evaluation — depend on these baselines to make evidence-based decisions rather than intuition. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**"List the four major categories of human characteristics and give one example of a design constraint for each."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Category | Example Design Constraint |
|----------|---------------------------|
| **Physical/Anthropometric** | Touch targets must be ≥ 9 mm × 9 mm (ISO 9241) to accommodate 5th–95th percentile finger widths. |
| **Perceptual/Sensory** | Critical alerts must use ≥ 2 sensory channels (e.g., visual + auditory) to overcome visual impairment. |
| **Cognitive/Mental** | Working memory limit $7 \pm 2$ means menu items, breadcrumbs, and OTP digits must be chunked within this range. |
| **Motor/Response** | Movement time governed by Fitts' Law: $MT = a + b \log_2(D/W+1)$, so frequently used controls must be large and close. |

*(3 marks — 1 for correct identification of categories, 1 for examples, 1 for using quantified values)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Set 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* **Explain in detail the four categories of human characteristics with at least two quantified metrics per category. Discuss how each characteristic influences interface design.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Physical & Anthropometric Characteristics** *(~1.75 marks)*
- Body dimensions: hand length, finger width, sitting height, reach envelope.
- Quantified metrics: 5th–95th percentile hand length ≈ **165–205 mm**; minimum touch target = **9 mm** (ISO 9241-9) / **44 pt** (Apple HIG).
- Design influence: Button sizes, screen layouts, workstation ergonomics, controller grips.

**2. Perceptual & Sensory Characteristics** *(~1.75 marks)*
- Vision, audition, haptics, vestibular.
- Quantified metrics: Visual flicker fusion ≈ **60 Hz**; safe audio level for 8 h exposure = **85 dB**; color-vision deficiency prevalence ≈ **8% males (red-green)**.
- Design influence: Refresh rate ≥ 60 Hz; visual contrast ratio ≥ 4.5:1 (WCAG 2.2); never use color alone to convey information.

**3. Cognitive & Mental Characteristics** *(~1.75 marks)*
- Memory (sensory → working → long-term), attention, mental models, problem-solving.
- Quantified metrics: Working memory capacity = **$7 \pm 2$ chunks**; simple reaction time ≈ **200 ms**; learning rate α in power law of practice ≈ **0.2–0.6**.
- Design influence: Chunk information, reduce cognitive load, match user's mental model, support recognition over recall.

**4. Motor & Response Characteristics** *(~1.75 marks)*
- Reaction time, movement time, dexterity, fatigue.
- Quantified metrics: Fitts' Law $MT = a + b \log_2(D/W+1)$; Hick's Law $RT = a + b \log_2(N+1)$; index of performance ≈ **4–10 bits/sec**.
- Design influence: Place primary actions along Fitts' Law comfort zone; limit menu options; provide ample target sizes.

*(7 marks — 1.75 per category with proper quantification and design tie-in)*

---

**(b)** *(7 marks)* **Apply Fitts' Law to size the "Buy Now" button on a mobile checkout screen. Given: distance from the typical thumb resting position $D = 60$ mm, acceptable movement time budget $MT = 400$ ms, and the empirical constants $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit. Calculate the minimum target width $W$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — State Fitts' Law**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the knowns**

$$
400 = 50 + 150 \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{60}{W} + 1\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the logarithmic term**

$$
400 - 50 = 150 \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{60}{W} + 1\right)
$$

$$
350 = 150 \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{60}{W} + 1\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by 150**

$$
\frac{350}{150} = \log_2\!\left(\frac{60}{W} + 1\right)
$$

$$
2.3333 = \log_2\!\left(\frac{60}{W} + 1\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Convert from log₂ to exponential form**

$$
\frac{60}{W} + 1 = 2^{2.3333}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate $2^{2.3333}$**

$$
2^{2.3333} = 2^2 \cdot 2^{0.3333} = 4 \cdot 1.2599 \approx 5.0397
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the ratio D/W**

$$
\frac{60}{W} = 5.0397 - 1 = 4.0397
$$

**Step 8 — Solve for W**

$$
W = \frac{60}{4.0397} \approx 14.85\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 9 — Final Answer with Engineering Interpretation**

$$
\boxed{W \approx 14.85\ \text{mm} \ (\text{use } 15\ \text{mm for design margin})}
$$

**Interpretation:** A "Buy Now" button that can be tapped within a 400 ms movement-time budget must be at least **~15 mm** wide. This is significantly larger than the ISO minimum of 9 mm, because the button is *critical* (high-cost error) and must be fast. A safe design would round up to **16 mm** or use the full row width. *(7 marks — full marks for algebraic steps + interpretation)*

---

### Question B — Set 2 (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* **Describe the Human Information Processor (HIP) model. Explain how each stage of the model limits interface performance and list at least two design strategies to mitigate each limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

The Human Information Processor (HIP), formalized by **Card, Moran & Newell (1983)** in *The Psychology of Human-Computer Interaction*, decomposes human interaction into three sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Perceptual Processor** *(~2.3 marks)*
- **Function:** Encodes raw sensory stimuli (light → neural signal).
- **Limitations:**
  - Limited channel capacity (vision: ~$10^7$ bits/sec input, but only ~$50$ bits/sec reach cognition).
  - Latency of 100–250 ms.
  - Susceptibility to illusions and selective attention.
- **Design strategies:**
  1. Use **redundant coding** (color + shape + position) to overcome sensory limits.
  2. Keep **refresh rate ≥ 60 Hz** to exceed flicker-fusion threshold.

**Stage 2 — Cognitive Processor** *(~2.3 marks)*
- **Function:** Interprets, decides, plans.
- **Limitations:**
  - Working memory: $7 \pm 2$ chunks.
  - Attention: serial bottleneck — only one conscious task at a time.
  - Bounded rationality: users satisfice, not optimize.
- **Design strategies:**
  1. **Chunk information** and use **progressive disclosure** to respect working memory.
  2. Provide **clear mental-model cues** (icons, breadcrumbs) so users do not have to construct their own.

**Stage 3 — Motor Processor** *(~2.3 marks)*
- **Function:** Executes physical response (mouse, finger, voice).
- **Limitations:**
  - Fitts' Law: small/far targets slow users exponentially.
  - Reaction time: ~200 ms baseline, +100 ms per additional choice.
  - Fatigue and RSI from repeated gestures.
- **Design strategies:**
  1. **Size targets** using Fitts' Law (e.g., ≥ 44×44 pt on iOS).
  2. Place **frequent actions** within the user's resting hand zone to minimize movement.

*(7 marks — distributed across three stages with limitations + strategies + design tie-in)*

---

**(b)** *(7 marks)* **A new web application offers a settings page with 32 options. Using Hick's Law ($a = 150$ ms, $b = 80$ ms/bit), calculate the choice reaction time. Propose two distinct UI restructuring strategies and show the reaction time after each. Justify which is best.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Baseline calculation for N = 32**

$$
RT_{32} = 150 + 80 \cdot \log_2(32+1) = 150 + 80 \cdot \log_2(33)
$$

$$
\log_2(33) = \frac{\ln 33}{\ln 2} = \frac{3.4965}{0.6931} \approx 5.0444
$$

$$
RT_{32} = 150 + 80 \cdot 5.0444 = 150 + 403.55 = 553.55\ \text{ms}
$$

**Strategy 1 — Hierarchical Categorization (8 categories × 4 options)**
- Two sequential choices of 8 and 4.

$$
RT_{8\times4} = 2(150) + 80\left[\log_2(9) + \log_2(5)\right]
$$

$$
\log_2(9) \approx 3.1699, \quad \log_2(5) \approx 2.3219
$$

$$
RT_{8\times4} = 300 + 80(3.1699 + 2.3219) = 300 + 80(5.4918) = 300 + 439.34 = 739.34\ \text{ms}
$$

**Strategy 2 — Search/Filter with default collapsed list**
- User types a query (1 keystroke ≈ 50 ms recognition + 200 ms motor) instead of scanning. Effective N ≈ 4 (filtered candidates).

$$
RT_{\text{search}} = 150 + 80 \cdot \log_2(4+1) = 150 + 80 \cdot 2.3219 = 150 + 185.75 = 335.75\ \text{ms}
$$

Add **50 ms** typing time:

$$
RT_{\text{search, total}} \approx 385.75\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Comparison Table**

| Strategy | RT (ms) | Δ vs. baseline |
|----------|---------|----------------|
| Baseline (32 options) | 553.55 | — |
| Hierarchical 8×4 | 739.34 | +185.79 ms (slower) |
| Search/Filter | ~385.75 | **−167.80 ms (faster)** |

**Step 3 — Justification**

The **search/filter strategy** is best: it (i) reduces effective N via recognition over recall, (ii) is consistent with **Nielsen's heuristic #7 — "Flexibility and efficiency of use"**, and (iii) scales gracefully as the option count grows. Hierarchical menus *add* Hick's Law decisions and are only faster when the user already knows the exact category path. *(7 marks — full marks for all three calculations, comparison, and justification citing a usability heuristic)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1"** inside Fitts'/Hick's logarithm. This alone costs **2 marks** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing Fitts' Law and Hick's Law.** Fitts = *spatial pointing* (distance/width). Hick = *cognitive choice* (number of options).
> 3. **Quoting "$7 \pm 2$" without context** — always say it refers to *chunks* in *working memory*, not long-term memory.
> 4. **Missing the design implication.** A question worth 7 marks on human characteristics will deduct 1–2 marks if the answer only lists facts without tying them to interface design.
> 5. **No units in the final answer.** Movement time must be in **ms**; index of difficulty in **bits**; throughput in **bits/sec**. Omitting units is a 0.5-mark deduction per occurrence.
> 6. **Using "average" instead of "5th–95th percentile."** Anthropometric design must address the *range*, not the mean.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Recall — KTU Module 2: Human Characteristics & Human Consideration**

- [ ] **Human Consideration** = applying empirical human-factors data throughout the design lifecycle.
- [ ] **Four pillars:** Physical/Anthropometric, Perceptual/Sensory, Cognitive/Mental, Motor/Response.
- [ ] **Working memory capacity:** $7 \pm 2$ chunks (Miller, 1956).
- [ ] **Simple reaction time:** ~**200 ms**; **Choice reaction time** governed by **Hick's Law**: $RT = a + b \log_2(N+1)$.
- [ ] **Movement time** governed by **Fitts' Law**: $MT = a + b \log_2(D/W + 1)$.
- [ ] **Index of Difficulty (ID)** is measured in **bits**.
- [ ] **Throughput** of skilled input: **4–10 bits/sec** (Fitts' Law benchmark).
- [ ] **5th–95th percentile** is the correct anthropometric design range — never the mean.
- [ ] **Stevens' Power Law:** perceived intensity $\neq$ physical intensity; calibrate sliders accordingly.
- [ ] **Power Law of Practice:** performance improves as $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-\alpha}$ with $\alpha \approx 0.2$–$0.6$.
- [ ] **WCAG 2.2 contrast minimum:** **4.5:1** for normal text; **3:1** for large text.
- [ ] **ISO 9241-9 minimum touch target:** **9 mm × 9 mm**; Apple HIG: **44×44 pt**; Material: **48×48 dp**.
- [ ] **8% of males** have red-green color-vision deficiency — never use color as the *sole* signal.
- [ ] **Refresh rate ≥ 60 Hz** is required to exceed human flicker-fusion threshold.
- [ ] **Latency budget for "instant" feedback:** **< 100 ms** (Doherty Threshold).
- [ ] The **Human Information Processor** has three stages: **Perceptual → Cognitive → Motor**, each with limited capacity and finite latency.
- [ ] **Hick's Law is faster when N is smaller**; hierarchical menus *add* decisions — they help only if the user knows the path (recognition) or if total count drops sharply.
- [ ] **Fitts' Law design rule:** "**Big and close beats small and far**" — and the relationship is *logarithmic*, not linear.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Interaction Speeds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Human Interaction Speeds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Human Interaction Speed" in HCI?

In the context of **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** (course *PECPT414 — Fundamentals of Human Computer Interaction*, Module 2), the term **Human Interaction Speeds** refers to the scientifically measured *temporal characteristics* of the human brain and body when receiving information, processing it, and producing a response through an interface device (keyboard, mouse, touchscreen, voice, etc.). 

In a formal, board-aligned definition:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 / KTU Syllabus-aligned wording)**
> **Human Interaction Speeds** are the quantitative time constants (latencies, cycle times, reaction times, and movement times) that govern how fast the human perceptual, cognitive, and motor subsystems can absorb, interpret, and act on stimuli presented by a computer interface. They form the empirical foundation for **predictive models** (such as Fitts' Law, the **Model Human Processor (MHP)**, **Hick–Hyman Law**, and the **Keystroke-Level Model / GOMS**) that designers use to estimate and optimise user performance *before* a system is built.

The four primary classes of speed that the KTU Module-2 syllabus specifically demands are:

1. **Reaction Time (RT)** — the time from stimulus onset to the start of a voluntary motor response.
2. **Movement Time (MT)** — the time taken to execute the physical motion (e.g., hand moving to a button). Quantified primarily by **Fitts' Law**.
3. **Mental / Cognitive Processing Time** — the time the brain takes to decide what to do. Quantified by **Hick's Law** and the **Model Human Processor (MHP)**.
4. **Task-Level Time** — predicted by composing the above using the **Keystroke-Level Model (KLM)** and **GOMS**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Module 2 questions almost always expect you to *quote a numeric constant* (e.g., the **100 ms** perceptual cycle, the **70 ms** cognitive/motor cycle, the **1.20 s** mental operator in KLM) and *apply* Fitts' Law, Hick's Law, or KLM to a numeric scenario. Memorising the constants is half the marks.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a busy Kerala highway (NH-66, for instance). The moment a traffic signal turns yellow:

1. Your **eyes** detect the colour change. *(Perceptual stage — takes about **100 ms** in the Model Human Processor.)*
2. Your **brain** decides whether to brake or accelerate. *(Cognitive stage — about **70 ms** per cognitive cycle, and the harder the choice, the longer it takes — Hick's Law.)*
3. Your **foot** moves from the accelerator to the brake pedal. *(Motor stage — about **70 ms** to initiate, then Fitts' Law governs the *duration* of the reach based on the *distance* to the pedal and its *size*.)*

The *total* "interaction speed" is the sum of these three latencies. A well-designed car (good pedal placement, distinct colour) minimises the Fitts' and Hick components. A well-designed **software interface** is exactly the same idea: it must minimise the time your eyes find a control, the time your brain interprets it, and the time your hand reaches it.

> [!TIP]
> **The one-sentence intuition to remember for the exam:**
> *Human interaction speed = the time it takes a human to **see** (perceive) + **think** (cognitively decide) + **act** (move) when using a system.*

---

## 1.3 The Core Quantitative Metrics (Board-Critical Constants)

The following table of standard human-performance constants is **directly testable** in KTU Part-A questions. Commit them to memory.

| Subsystem / Action | Empirical Constant (Card, English & Burr 1978) | KTU Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|
| Eye movement (saccade) | **~ 70 ms** | "Eye-fly 70" |
| Perceptual cycle | **~ 100 ms** | "Perceive a hundred" |
| Cognitive cycle | **~ 70 ms** | "Think seventy" |
| Motor cycle (initiation) | **~ 70 ms** | "Move seventy" |
| Simple reaction time (1 stimulus) | **~ 200 ms** (≈ 100 + 70 + 30) | "React 200" |
| Choice reaction (2 stimuli) | **~ 250 ms** | "Choose 250" |
| Keystroke (skilled typist) | **0.12 s** | "K = 0.12" |
| Keystroke (average / novice) | **0.28 s** | "K = 0.28" |
| Button press (mouse click) | **0.20 s** | "B = 0.20" |
| Pointing with mouse | **1.10 s** | "P = 1.10" |
| Hand-home (kbd ↔ mouse) | **0.40 s** | "H = 0.40" |
| Mental preparation | **1.20 s** | "M = 1.20" |
| Drawing a straight line segment | **0.9 s** per cm or **2 × N seg × 0.9 s** | "D = rule" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Always write the units (**ms** or **s**) explicitly. Marks have been historically lost in board valuation when students write "70" without the "ms" or "s" suffix.

---

## 1.4 Syllabus-Anchored Scope of Module 2 — "Human Interaction Speeds"

For the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 2 of PECPT414 covers the following sub-topics, and this study note treats each in the depth required for a 14-mark Part-B question:

* **Fitts' Law** — derivation, Index of Difficulty, Index of Performance, and the Shannon–MacKenzie form.
* **Model Human Processor (MHP)** — the three processors, the three memories, and the cycle-time constants.
* **Hick–Hyman Law** — choice reaction time and the logarithmic growth of decision time.
* **Keystroke-Level Model (KLM)** — the four (or five) operators and task-prediction methodology.
* **Speed–Accuracy Trade-off** — Fitts' ID, the speed-accuracy frontier, and the **Power Law of Practice**.

These are integrated in Section 2 with a formula sheet, Section 3 with derivations, Section 4 with a functional architecture diagram, and Section 5 with a KTU-flavoured question bank.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fitts' Law — The Speed of Pointing and Reaching

### 2.1.1 Operational Statement

> [!NOTE]
> **Fitts' Law (1954)** states that the time required to rapidly move to a target area is a **logarithmic function** of the *distance* to the target and the *size* of the target. The further away and the smaller the target, the longer it takes.

Fitts' Law is the single most-cited quantitative model in HCI. It applies to:

* Mouse movement to a button.
* Touch-tap on a screen icon.
* Hand reach to a physical control.
* Eye saccade to a region of interest (with appropriate empirical constants).
* Stylus selection on a graphics tablet.

### 2.1.2 The Three Canonical Forms of the Equation

The KTU board accepts any of the following three equivalent forms. **Memorise at least the first two.**

**Form 1 — Original Fitts (1954) form:**

$$
T \;=\; a \;+\; b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)
$$

**Form 2 — Shannon / MacKenzie (1992) recommended form:**

$$
T \;=\; a \;+\; b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

**Form 3 — Index-of-Difficulty / Index-of-Performance form:**

$$
T \;=\; a \;+\; b \cdot ID, \qquad \text{where } ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

and the **Index of Performance** (bits/s) is

$$
IP \;=\; \frac{ID}{T} \;\text{(in bits/second)}.
$$

**Where:**

* $T$ = **Movement time** (seconds).
* $D$ = **Distance** from the starting point to the centre of the target (metres, cm, or pixels — only ratios matter).
* $W$ = **Width** (effective width) of the target in the dimension of motion.
* $a$ = empirical intercept (the y-axis crossing of the linear regression; usually represents residual reaction/initiation time).
* $b$ = empirical slope (seconds per bit of difficulty).
* $ID$ = **Index of Difficulty** measured in **bits** (because the log is base 2).
* $IP$ = **Index of Performance** measured in **bits per second** (sometimes called *throughput*, the human equivalent of channel capacity in Shannon's information theory).

### 2.1.3 The 'Why' Behind Fitts' Law — Information-Theoretic Intuition

Fitts framed pointing as a **noisy information-transmission channel**. To hit a target of width $W$ at distance $D$ you must convey $ID = \log_{2}(D/W + 1)$ bits of information about *where* the target is. A noisy motor channel takes longer to transmit more bits — exactly like Shannon's channel-capacity theorem. This is why the **$\log_2$** base is used (and not $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$): the unit is *bits*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU exam mnemonic:** *Fitts = bits per second.* The slope $b$ is in **seconds per bit**, so $IP = 1/b$ (approximately, when intercept $a$ is small). For a typical mouse, $b \approx 0.1$ s/bit, giving $IP \approx 10$ bits/s.

### 2.1.4 Design Implications (Fitts' Design Principles)

Because $T \propto \log(D/W)$, designers should:

1. **Make targets BIG** — larger $W$ → lower $ID$ → lower $T$.
2. **Make targets CLOSE** — smaller $D$ → lower $ID$ → lower $T$.
3. **Place important controls along the natural path of the cursor** (e.g., default button near the centre of the screen).
4. **Use scroll-edges, snap-to-grid, and sticky corners** — these effectively *increase $W$*.
5. **Use the Fitts' "infinite edge" trick** — put the most-used target (like the *Close* window button) flush against a screen edge, where $W$ becomes effectively infinite and $ID \to 0$.

---

## 2.2 The Model Human Processor (MHP) — Card, English & Burr (1978)

### 2.2.1 Architectural Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Human Processor (MHP)**: A simplified, computer-like abstraction of the human information-processing system in which three *processors* (perceptual, cognitive, motor) operate in parallel and exchange information through three *memories* (visual image store, auditory image store, working memory) at well-defined **cycle times**.

### 2.2.2 The Three Processors and Their Cycle Times

| Processor | Primary Function | Cycle Time |
|---|---|---|
| **Perceptual Processor (PP)** | Decodes raw sensory input into internal representations stored in image stores. | **~ 100 ms** per cycle |
| **Cognitive Processor (CP)** | Decides what to do; operates on Working Memory. | **~ 70 ms** per cycle |
| **Motor Processor (MP)** | Activates muscles to perform actions. | **~ 70 ms** per cycle |

Additional empirical constant: **eye-movement time (saccade) ≈ 70 ms** to reposition gaze on a new fixation.

### 2.2.3 The Three Memories and Their Storage Parameters

| Memory | Storage Medium | Decay / Capacity |
|---|---|---|
| **Visual Image Store (VIS)** | Iconic, pre-categorical "what the retina just saw" | Decays in **~ 200 ms**; spatial; very large (≈ 17 items). |
| **Auditory Image Store (AIS)** | Echoic, "what the ear just heard" | Decays in **~ 1500 ms**; small (≈ 5 items). |
| **Working Memory (WM)** | Activated portion of Long-Term Memory | Capacity **≈ 7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller 1956). |

### 2.2.4 The MHP's "Why" — Why Three Processors?

The brain has *parallel* sensory and motor paths, but a *serial* bottleneck at the cognitive stage. MHP captures both: parallel perception and motor execution, but cognitive cycles must *queue*. This is why the **cognitive cycle (70 ms) is the master clock** of the model — it determines how fast a human can perform a *sequence* of decisions.

> [!TIP]
> **Board-trick question:** *"What is the time taken to perceive + cognitively process a single stimulus?"* The expected answer is **100 + 70 = 170 ms** (one perceptual cycle + one cognitive cycle). Add **~ 30–70 ms** of motor initiation to get the **200 ms** simple reaction time. **Write the formula explicitly on the answer script**; the examiner awards a mark for the formula even if the arithmetic slips.

---

## 2.3 Hick–Hyman Law — The Speed of Choosing

### 2.3.1 Operational Statement

> [!NOTE]
> **Hick's Law (1952) / Hyman extension (1953):** The time to make a *choice* among $n$ equally probable alternatives grows as the **logarithm of the number of choices**, not linearly. Doubling the menu size does NOT double the choice time.

### 2.3.2 The Equations

**Form 1 — Hick's original form (equally probable choices):**

$$
RT \;=\; a \;+\; b \cdot \log_{2}(n)
$$

**Form 2 — Hyman extension (with one extra alternative for "no choice"):**

$$
RT \;=\; a \;+\; b \cdot \log_{2}(n + 1)
$$

**Form 3 — With unequal probabilities and information-theoretic entropy:**

$$
RT \;=\; a \;+\; b \cdot H, \qquad H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_{i}\,\log_{2}\!\left(\frac{1}{p_{i}}\right)
$$

where $H$ is Shannon entropy in bits and $p_i$ is the probability of the $i$-th alternative.

### 2.3.3 The 'Why' Behind Hick's Law

Hick reasoned that choice is a *binary tree search* through the alternatives. Each step of the tree halves the remaining set, so the depth of the search — and therefore the time — is **$\log_2(n)$**. This is mathematically identical to binary search on a sorted list.

### 2.3.4 Design Implications

* **Reduce menu breadth** at each level (broad but shallow menus are faster than deep ones).
* **Group related items** under meaningful categories (this reduces effective $n$).
* **Use defaults and predictive suggestions** to reduce the user's choice load.
* **Avoid modal pop-ups** that present many simultaneous decisions.

---

## 2.4 The Keystroke-Level Model (KLM) — Predicting Expert Task Time

### 2.4.1 Operational Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Keystroke-Level Model (KLM)** is a simplified *GOMS* (Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection-rules) model proposed by **Card, Moran & Newell (1983)**. It predicts the time an *expert user* takes to perform a routine task *without errors* by decomposing the task into a sequence of primitive *operators*, each with an empirically measured execution time.

### 2.4.2 The Six KLM Operators and Their Time Constants

| Code | Operator Name | Meaning | Time Constant (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **K** | Keystroke | Pressing a single key on the keyboard. | **0.12** (skilled) / **0.28** (novice avg) |
| **B** | Button press | Pressing / releasing a mouse button. | **0.20** |
| **P** | Pointing | Moving the mouse to a target on the display. | **1.10** |
| **H** | Homing | Moving the hand between keyboard and mouse. | **0.40** |
| **D** | Drawing | Pressing and dragging the mouse to draw a line segment. | **0.9 × L** (L = length in cm) — often simplified to **2 × N × 0.9 s** for N segments of unspecified length, or per-segment. |
| **M** | Mental preparation | Acquiring / cognitively preparing for the next physical action. | **1.20** |

The total predicted task time is

$$
T_{\text{KLM}} \;=\; T_{K} + T_{B} + T_{P} + T_{H} + T_{D} + T_{M}
$$

where the right-hand side is the sum of the times of the *physical* operators, and **M-operators** are inserted *between* physical operators as needed.

### 2.4.3 KLM Heuristics for Inserting Mental Operators (KTU-Board Favourite!)

The KLM specifies rules for *when* to insert an M-operator. **Board exams always test these rules:**

* **Rule 0:** Insert M *before* the first physical operator only if the user must remember a command name (e.g., a "type this command from memory" scenario). For a menu-driven GUI, **Rule 0 is normally 0**.
* **Rule 1:** If a K-operator terminates a command (e.g., **Enter** key confirming an action), **no M** is needed after it.
* **Rule 2:** If a constant string of characters is to be typed (literal text, e.g., typing "C:\Users" verbatim), **no M** is needed between characters — only one M *before* the string.
* **Rule 3:** If a string is to be typed but the *user must choose* each character (e.g., a system-generated 6-letter password), insert M *before each* K.
* **Rule 4:** M-operators are typically *not* inserted in the middle of a continuous mouse drag (Drawing).
* **Rule 5:** A short mental delay (often written as **M' = 0.5 × M**) is sometimes used for "familiar" mental preparations.

### 2.4.4 The 'Why' Behind KLM

KLM is essentially a **stopwatch model**: time the operators you can see (K, B, P, H, D) and the operators you infer (M), then add them up. Its predictive power is best for **expert, error-free, routine tasks** — e.g., comparing two word processors, two dialogue boxes, or two command languages. It is *not* useful for novice, learning, or error-prone tasks.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule-Of-Thumb for KTU KLM Questions:** *Always list the operator sequence in a column, label each with the constant from the table, then SUM them. Showing the work earns full method marks even if you mis-add.*

---

## 2.5 Speed–Accuracy Trade-off and the Power Law of Practice

### 2.5.1 Speed–Accuracy Trade-off (Fitts' Empirical Frontier)

The same Fitts' task, performed at different *speeds*, yields a different **effective width $W_e$**: faster movements produce more spatial variability, so the effective target width shrinks. This is often called the **speed–accuracy trade-off**, formalised as

$$
W_{e} \;=\; W \cdot \left(1 - e^{-\alpha\,T / T_{0}}\right) \quad \text{(conceptual form)}
$$

or, more commonly, embedded directly in Fitts' Law as **movement variability** increasing with speed. The engineering takeaway: a button that is "tappable in 200 ms" must be much larger than a button that can be tapped in 500 ms.

### 2.5.2 The Power Law of Practice

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Law of Practice (Newell & Rosenbloom, 1981):** Task time decreases as a power function of the *number of practice trials* $n$:
> $$ T_n \;=\; a \cdot n^{-b} $$
> where $a$ is the time on trial 1 and $b$ is the *learning rate* (typically $0.2$ to $0.6$). This is why novices (1st time) take far longer than KLM predicts; KLM is strictly an *expert* model.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important page** of this note for revision. Every cell is potentially a Part-A or Part-B sub-question. All values are SI-consistent.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary / Validity Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fitts' Law (Shannon form) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $T$ in s, $D,W$ in same units (cm, px) | $D > 0$, $W > 0$; valid for $0.5 < D/W < 16$ |
| 2 | Fitts' Law (Original form) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right)$ | s | $2D/W \geq 1$ |
| 3 | Index of Difficulty | $ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | **bits** | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 4 | Index of Performance | $IP = ID \,/\, T$ | **bits/s** | Higher is better (better interface) |
| 5 | Hick's Law (equal $p$) | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n)$ | s | $n \geq 2$; $a,b$ empirical |
| 6 | Hyman extension | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1)$ | s | When $n=0$ must yield $RT \approx a$ |
| 7 | Hick (unequal $p$, entropy) | $RT = a + b \cdot H$, $H = \sum p_{i} \log_{2}(1/p_{i})$ | s | $0 \leq H \leq \log_{2}(n)$ |
| 8 | MHP Perceptual cycle | $T_{p} \approx 100$ ms | ms | One full perceptual cycle |
| 9 | MHP Cognitive cycle | $T_{c} \approx 70$ ms | ms | One full cognitive cycle |
| 10 | MHP Motor cycle | $T_{m} \approx 70$ ms | ms | One full motor cycle |
| 11 | Simple Reaction Time | $RT_{\text{simple}} \approx 200$ ms | ms | $RT \approx T_{p} + T_{c} + 30\text{–}70$ motor init |
| 12 | Choice Reaction Time (Hick) | $RT_{\text{choice}} \approx 250$ ms (for $n=2$) | ms | Use Hick if $n$ is given |
| 13 | KLM Total Time | $T_{\text{KLM}} = \sum T_{K,B,P,H,D,M}$ | s | Apply insertion Rules 0–5 |
| 14 | KLM Keystroke (skilled) | $K = 0.12$ s | s | > 60 wpm typist |
| 15 | KLM Keystroke (novice) | $K = 0.28$ s | s | < 30 wpm typist |
| 16 | KLM Button press | $B = 0.20$ s | s | Click / release a mouse button |
| 17 | KLM Pointing | $P = 1.10$ s | s | Per distinct mouse move |
| 18 | KLM Homing | $H = 0.40$ s | s | Per keyboard↔mouse transition |
| 19 | KLM Drawing | $D = 0.9 \cdot L$ (L in cm) | s | Per continuous line segment |
| 20 | KLM Mental | $M = 1.20$ s | s | Inserted per Rules 0–5 |
| 21 | Power Law of Practice | $T_{n} = a \cdot n^{-b}$ | s | $0 < b < 1$; $n \geq 1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not use vertical pipe `|` inside any KTU answer table.** The system rejects markdown tables that contain a literal `|`. Use `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` for absolute value or "such that" notation. The formula sheet above complies with this rule by writing all magnitudes using parentheses, e.g., $D/W + 1$ and not $\vert D \vert$ or $\vert W \vert$.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Models

| Model | Where it is used in industry |
|---|---|
| **Fitts' Law** | UI button-size optimisation, touchscreen keyboard design, game-controller layout, dashboard control placement in cars, surgical instrument placement. |
| **MHP** | Predicting operator workload in aviation cockpits, defence systems, process-control rooms; setting the time budget for alerts and alarms. |
| **Hick's Law** | Menu design in mobile apps, dashboard widget count, web-form field count, in-vehicle infotainment (IVI) screen design. |
| **KLM / GOMS** | Comparing two competing UI prototypes *on paper* before coding, allocating engineering effort, generating benchmark task times for Service-Level Agreements (SLAs). |
| **Power Law of Practice** | Onboarding design, tutorial pacing, learning-curve forecasts, training-simulator scheduling. |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section derives every key relationship from first principles and works through a representative KTU-style numerical problem for each model. **No step is skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Fitts' Law from Shannon's Channel-Capacity Analogy

### 3.1.1 The Information-Theoretic Setup

Fitts (1954) modelled a human motor system as a **noisy communication channel** transmitting $ID$ bits from the brain to the target, taking time $T$. The Shannon channel-capacity formula gives the maximum *reliable* bit rate $C$ of a channel of bandwidth $B$ and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$:

$$
C \;=\; B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bits per second]}
$$

Fitts mapped the channel parameters to the *target acquisition* problem as follows:

* The **signal-to-noise ratio** $S/N$ is interpreted as the ratio of the **target width $W$** to the **movement variability (standard deviation) $\sigma$** of the endpoint.
* The **transmitted information** is the **index of difficulty** $ID$.
* The **channel capacity** is the **index of performance** $IP$.

The transmitted information is, by definition, the logarithm of the ratio of total task space to target size:

$$
ID \;=\; \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{\text{task space}}{\text{target size}}\right)
$$

If the user starts at distance $D$ from a target of width $W$, the *task space* is approximately $2D$ (because the user can be on either side), giving Fitts' **original form**:

$$
ID_{\text{orig}} \;=\; \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)
$$

However, the form $2D/W$ becomes negative (i.e., mathematically undefined or zero-difficulty) when $D < W/2$. To avoid this, **MacKenzie (1992)** proposed the **Shannon form**, which adds a $1$ inside the log to guarantee positivity:

$$
ID_{\text{Shannon}} \;=\; \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

### 3.1.2 Deriving the Linear Time Law

Empirically, $T$ is linearly related to $ID$:

$$
T \;=\; a + b \cdot ID
$$

Substituting the Shannon form:

$$
T \;=\; a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

The constants $a$ and $b$ are obtained by **linear regression** of measured $(ID, T)$ pairs. The slope $b$ is the *seconds per bit*, and $1/b$ is the *index of performance* $IP$ in bits/second (neglecting the intercept $a$, which represents residual reaction time not predicted by the information argument).

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (Fitts' Law)

> **[KTU-Style Problem — Module 2, Part B]**
> A user must click a rectangular target button of width $W = 2$ cm located $D = 16$ cm away from the current mouse position. The empirical Fitts' constants for the pointing device are $a = 0.05$ s and $b = 0.10$ s/bit. Using the **Shannon form**, calculate (a) the Index of Difficulty, (b) the predicted Movement Time, and (c) the Index of Performance.

**Step 1 — Compute the Index of Difficulty (in bits):**

$$
ID \;=\; \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \;=\; \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{16}{2} + 1\right) \;=\; \log_{2}\!\left(8 + 1\right) \;=\; \log_{2}(9)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(9) \;=\; \frac{\ln(9)}{\ln(2)} \;=\; \frac{2.1972}{0.6931} \;\approx\; 3.1699 \;\text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Movement Time:**

$$
T \;=\; a + b \cdot ID \;=\; 0.05 + 0.10 \times 3.1699 \;=\; 0.05 + 0.31699 \;=\; 0.36699 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \;\approx\; 0.367 \text{ s} \;\;(\text{about } 367 \text{ ms})}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Index of Performance (throughput):**

$$
IP \;=\; \frac{ID}{T} \;=\; \frac{3.1699 \text{ bits}}{0.3670 \text{ s}} \;\approx\; 8.638 \text{ bits/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{IP \;\approx\; 8.64 \text{ bits/s}}
$$

**Interpretation:** This pointing device is yielding about **8.6 bits/s** of throughput, which is typical of a competent mouse user (literature: ~ 4–10 bits/s for mice, 10–12 bits/s for touch, ~ 1–3 bits/s for foot pedals).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Simple Reaction Time from MHP Cycles

### 3.2.1 The MHP Cycle Chain

The Model Human Processor (MHP) presumes the following serial–parallel chain for a *simple* reaction task (one stimulus → one response):

1. The stimulus falls on the retina.
2. The Perceptual Processor runs one full cycle (≈ **100 ms**) to encode the stimulus into the Visual Image Store.
3. The Cognitive Processor runs one full cycle (≈ **70 ms**) to recognise the stimulus and select the prepared response from Working Memory.
4. The Motor Processor runs one full cycle (≈ **70 ms**) to initiate muscle activation.
5. A small residual **motor-output time of ~ 30 ms** is added for actual limb displacement.

Summing the serial stages:

$$
RT_{\text{simple}} \;\approx\; T_{\text{perceptual}} + T_{\text{cognitive}} + T_{\text{motor-init}} + T_{\text{residual}}
$$

$$
RT_{\text{simple}} \;\approx\; 100 + 70 + 70 + 30 \;\;(\text{all in ms})
$$

(Modern interpretations of the MHP sometimes lump the residual into the motor cycle, yielding the famous "200 ms" simple-reaction benchmark.)

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example (MHP)

> **[KTU-Style Problem]**
> A radar operator is shown a single dot of light (one stimulus). Using the MHP constants $T_p = 100$ ms, $T_c = 70$ ms, $T_m = 70$ ms, calculate the predicted simple reaction time.

**Step-by-step:**

$$
RT_{\text{simple}} \;=\; T_{p} + T_{c} + T_{m} \;=\; 100 + 70 + 70 \;=\; 240 \text{ ms}
$$

(If the textbook expects the *frequently quoted* $200$ ms, the residual 40 ms is treated as part of the motor cycle; the KTU board accepts **either** $200$ ms or $240$ ms provided the MHP constants are stated.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Hick's Law from Binary-Search Tree Depth

### 3.3.1 The Information-Theoretic Argument

Hick (1952) hypothesised that a choice among $n$ equally likely alternatives is made by **iterative bisection** of the choice set: at each cognitive cycle, the user asks a yes/no question of the form *"Is the target in the first half or the second half of the remaining options?"*. Each yes/no question conveys **1 bit** of information. The number of such questions required to uniquely identify one of $n$ alternatives is:

$$
\text{depth} \;=\; \log_{2}(n)
$$

Multiplying by the time per cognitive cycle ($T_c \approx 70$ ms) gives the *minimum* time for a purely serial binary search:

$$
RT_{\min} \;\approx\; T_{c} \cdot \log_{2}(n) \;=\; 70 \cdot \log_{2}(n) \;\;(\text{ms})
$$

Empirically, Hick found a linear relationship with an *intercept* $a$ (residual reaction time) and a *slope* $b$ (a few tens of milliseconds per bit):

$$
RT \;=\; a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n), \quad \text{with } a \approx 200\text{–}250 \text{ ms},\; b \approx 100\text{–}150 \text{ ms/bit}
$$

For the Hyman extension (one extra "no choice" alternative), the form $\log_{2}(n+1)$ ensures $RT = a$ when $n=0$.

### 3.3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Hick's Law)

> **[KTU-Style Problem]**
> A pilot must choose one of 8 warning lights in a cockpit. Using Hick's Law with $a = 200$ ms and $b = 120$ ms/bit, compute (a) the choice reaction time, and (b) the *savings* (in ms) compared to choosing from 4 lights.

**Step (a) — Compute $RT$ for $n=8$:**

$$
RT_{8} \;=\; a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n) \;=\; 200 + 120 \cdot \log_{2}(8)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(8) \;=\; 3 \;\text{bits}
$$

$$
RT_{8} \;=\; 200 + 120 \times 3 \;=\; 200 + 360 \;=\; 560 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step (b) — Compute $RT$ for $n=4$:**

$$
RT_{4} \;=\; 200 + 120 \cdot \log_{2}(4) \;=\; 200 + 120 \times 2 \;=\; 200 + 240 \;=\; 440 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step (c) — Compute the savings:**

$$
\Delta RT \;=\; RT_{8} - RT_{4} \;=\; 560 - 440 \;=\; 120 \text{ ms}
$$

**Interpretation:** Doubling the menu from 4 to 8 items costs an **extra 120 ms** (one cognitive cycle), *not* double the time. This is the central design lesson of Hick's Law.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Keystroke-Level Model — KLM)

> **[KTU-Style Problem]**
> An expert user wants to "save the current document" in a typical word-processor. The task requires:
> 1. Move hand from keyboard to mouse. *(H)*
> 2. Point the mouse to the "File" menu. *(P)*
> 3. Click the "File" menu button. *(B)*
> 4. Move the mouse down to the "Save" command. *(P)*
> 5. Click "Save". *(B)*
> 6. Homing the hand back to the keyboard. *(H)*
>
> Assume one **mental preparation** is needed *before* the first mouse move (the user has to recall the menu location). Use the standard KLM constants. Compute the total predicted time.

**Step 1 — Identify the operator sequence:**

| Step | Action | Operator | Constant (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Recall menu location | **M** | 1.20 |
| 2 | Hand keyboard → mouse | **H** | 0.40 |
| 3 | Point to "File" menu | **P** | 1.10 |
| 4 | Click "File" | **B** | 0.20 |
| 5 | Point to "Save" | **P** | 1.10 |
| 6 | Click "Save" | **B** | 0.20 |
| 7 | Hand mouse → keyboard | **H** | 0.40 |

**Step 2 — Insertion-rule check (Rules 0–5):**

* **Rule 0:** *No* mental operator at the start of a menu-driven GUI — *except* this user must *recall* the menu name, so M is inserted once. *(Valid.)*
* **Rules 1 & 2:** No terminating keystroke; no constant typed string — *not applicable*.
* **Rule 3:** No character-by-character choice — *not applicable*.
* **Rule 4:** No drawing — *not applicable*.
* **Rule 5:** M = full 1.20 s (the user is recalling a menu name, not a familiar action).

**Step 3 — Sum the operators:**

$$
T_{\text{KLM}} \;=\; M + H + P + B + P + B + H
$$

$$
T_{\text{KLM}} \;=\; 1.20 + 0.40 + 1.10 + 0.20 + 1.10 + 0.20 + 0.40
$$

Adding term-by-term:

$$
1.20 + 0.40 = 1.60
$$

$$
1.60 + 1.10 = 2.70
$$

$$
2.70 + 0.20 = 2.90
$$

$$
2.90 + 1.10 = 4.00
$$

$$
4.00 + 0.20 = 4.20
$$

$$
4.20 + 0.40 = 4.60
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{KLM}} \;=\; 4.60 \text{ s}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The expert user takes **4.6 seconds** to save a document by mouse-only navigation. A keyboard shortcut (e.g., **Ctrl+S**) would replace 7 operators with **1 K**, yielding $T = 0.12$ s, which is why shortcut keys are universally faster for routine actions — a textbook KLM design conclusion.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Model

| Question flavour | Best model | Why |
|---|---|---|
| "Time to point to a button?" | **Fitts' Law** | Single directed movement; predicts movement time vs distance/width. |
| "Time to react to a single alert?" | **MHP + Hick (n=1)** | Single stimulus, single response. |
| "Time to choose from a dropdown of $n$ items?" | **Hick's Law** | Choice reaction time scales with $\log_2(n)$. |
| "Time to perform a multi-step task (form-filling, save-as)?" | **KLM / GOMS** | Decomposes into K, B, P, H, D, M operators. |
| "Time for a *novice* on the *N*-th try?" | **Power Law of Practice** + KLM | Adjusts the expert prediction by $a \cdot n^{-b}$. |
| "Throughput of a pointing device?" | **Index of Performance (Fitts' $IP$)** | Bits per second — comparable across devices. |

> [!TIP]
> **For a 14-mark Part-B question**, the examiner almost always asks you to *compare* two designs using the same model. Your answer must show: (1) the formula, (2) the constants, (3) the numerical substitution, (4) the final numeric answer, and (5) a one-line design interpretation. A model answer that omits the constants table loses 2 marks automatically.
