# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

Below are four **Mermaid** diagrams that capture the structure and flow of Human Interaction Speeds models in a way that is board-drawing-friendly.

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Model Human Processor (MHP) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph stim[External Stimulus Source]
        light[Visual Stimulus]
        sound[Auditory Stimulus]
    end

    subgraph pp[Perceptual Processor - cycle 100 ms]
        ppBox[Decode raw sensory input]
    end

    subgraph mem[Working Memory Stores]
        visStore[Visual Image Store VIS decay 200 ms]
        audStore[Auditory Image Store AIS decay 1500 ms]
        wm[Working Memory WM capacity 7 plus minus 2]
    end

    subgraph cp[Cognitive Processor - cycle 70 ms]
        cpBox[Decide what to do]
    end

    subgraph mp[Motor Processor - cycle 70 ms]
        mpBox[Activate muscles]
    end

    resp[Physical Response - Hand Voice Eye]

    light --> ppBox
    sound --> ppBox
    ppBox --> visStore
    ppBox --> audStore
    visStore --> wm
    audStore --> wm
    wm --> cpBox
    cpBox --> mpBox
    mpBox --> resp
```

**Reading the diagram:** Information flows from the *stimulus* through the **perceptual processor (PP)** into the *image stores* (VIS/AIS) and then into **working memory**, where the **cognitive processor (CP)** makes a decision. The **motor processor (MP)** translates the decision into muscle action, producing the *physical response*. The three processors operate in parallel where possible, but the CP–WM–MP path is largely serial.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Fitts' Law Movement-Time Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[Cursor at start position]
    dist{Distance D to target}
    width{Target Width W}
    idCalc[Index of Difficulty ID equals log2 D over W plus 1]
    slopeSlope[Empirical slope b seconds per bit]
    intercept[Empirical intercept a in seconds]
    mult[b times ID]
    sum[a plus b times ID]
    time[Movement Time T in seconds]
    ipCalc[Index of Performance IP equals ID over T in bits per second]

    start --> dist
    dist --> idCalc
    width --> idCalc
    idCalc --> mult
    slopeSlope --> mult
    mult --> sum
    intercept --> sum
    sum --> time
    time --> ipCalc
    idCalc --> ipCalc
```

**Reading the diagram:** The two independent variables $D$ (distance) and $W$ (width) feed the **Index of Difficulty** $ID$ in bits. Multiplying by the **empirical slope $b$** (seconds per bit) and adding the **empirical intercept $a$** yields the **Movement Time $T$** in seconds. The **Index of Performance $IP$** is then $ID / T$ in bits per second.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Hick's Law Choice-Reaction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nCount{Number of alternatives n}
    nPlus[Hyman extension - use log2 n plus 1]
    nEqual[Equal probability - use log2 n]
    entropyH[Unequal probability - use entropy H equals sum p sub i log2 1 over p sub i]
    bMul[b times log2 of n or H]
    aAdd[Empirical intercept a]
    rtOut[Choice Reaction Time RT in seconds]
    design[Design Lesson - Reducing n reduces RT logarithmically]

    nCount --> nPlus
    nCount --> nEqual
    nCount --> entropyH
    nPlus --> bMul
    nEqual --> bMul
    entropyH --> bMul
    bMul --> rtOut
    aAdd --> rtOut
    rtOut --> design
```

**Reading the diagram:** The choice problem branches by the *information measure* used: Hyman extension $\log_2(n+1)$ for "no-choice included", original $\log_2(n)$ for equally likely, or full entropy $H$ for unequal probabilities. The chosen $H$-value is multiplied by the empirical slope $b$, added to the intercept $a$, and yields the **Choice Reaction Time**.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — KLM Operator Pipeline for a Sample Task

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startKLM[Start of expert task]
    mOp[M - Mental preparation 1.20 s]
    hOp1[H - Homing hand to mouse 0.40 s]
    pOp1[P - Pointing to menu 1.10 s]
    bOp1[B - Button press menu 0.20 s]
    pOp2[P - Pointing to item 1.10 s]
    bOp2[B - Button press item 0.20 s]
    hOp2[H - Homing back to keyboard 0.40 s]
    sumKLM[Total Task Time equals sum of operator times]
    outKLM[T KLM in seconds]

    startKLM --> mOp
    mOp --> hOp1
    hOp1 --> pOp1
    pOp1 --> bOp1
    bOp1 --> pOp2
    pOp2 --> bOp2
    bOp2 --> hOp2
    hOp2 --> sumKLM
    sumKLM --> outKLM
```

**Reading the diagram:** The KLM models a routine task as a *linear pipeline of primitive operators*. The standard order for a typical "menu-driven save" task is **M → H → P → B → P → B → H**, each operator contributing a fixed time. The total task time is simply the **sum** of all operator times.

> [!TIP]
> **Board exam strategy:** When asked to draw or describe a model, *labelling every node with both the operator name AND the time constant* earns full diagram marks. A Mermaid block counts as a labelled schematic for KTU valuation purposes.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This question bank is **modelled exactly on the KTU 2024 Scheme** assessment pattern (Part A 3-mark short-answers, Part B 14-mark long-answers with internal choice, sub-parts of 7 + 7). Each question carries a *simulated past-year tag*. The model answers are **board-valuation-ready**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECPT414]**
**Define Fitts' Law and state its significance in HCI design. Write the standard equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Fitts' Law (1954) is a predictive model of human motor performance that quantifies the time taken to **rapidly move to a target area** as a logarithmic function of the **distance** to the target and the **width** of the target.
>
> The standard (Shannon form) equation is:
>
> $$ T \;=\; a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) $$
>
> where $T$ is movement time in seconds, $D$ is the distance to the target, $W$ is the target width, and $a$ and $b$ are empirically determined constants.
>
> **Significance in HCI design:** Fitts' Law provides a quantitative basis for **designing and evaluating user interfaces**. It tells designers to (i) make interactive targets **large** (increase $W$), (ii) place frequently used targets **close** to the user's current focus (decrease $D$), and (iii) place the most important controls along screen edges where the effective width is infinite. The Index of Performance $IP = ID/T$ in bits per second also allows **objective comparison** of pointing devices (mouse, touch, stylus, eye-gaze).

**Mark breakdown (board convention):** Definition (1), equation with all symbols (1), design significance (1).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECPT414]**
**List the six operators of the Keystroke-Level Model (KLM) with their standard time constants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The KLM decomposes an expert task into the following six primitive operators, with the empirical time constants proposed by Card, Moran and Newell (1983):
>
> | Code | Operator | Time (s) |
> |---|---|---|
> | **K** | Keystroke (skilled typist) | **0.12** |
> | **B** | Button press (mouse click) | **0.20** |
> | **P** | Pointing with mouse | **1.10** |
> | **H** | Homing (hand between keyboard and mouse) | **0.40** |
> | **D** | Drawing (mouse drag, per segment) | **0.9 × L** (L in cm) |
> | **M** | Mental preparation | **1.20** |
>
> The total predicted task time is the **sum of all operator times** in the sequence, with M-operators inserted as per KLM insertion rules 0–5.

**Mark breakdown:** Six operators with correct codes (1), six correct times (1), formula and reference to insertion rules (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Each Part-B question is split into two sub-parts, typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Internal choice** means the student picks **one of two parallel questions** of equal marks.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

#### **(a)** *Explain the Model Human Processor (MHP) of Card, English and Burr. List the three processors, the three memories, and their empirical time constants. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **Model Human Processor (MHP)**, proposed by **Card, English and Burr (1978)**, is a simplified, computer-inspired model of human information processing. It treats the human as three coupled processors operating on three memory stores, each with a measured cycle time.

**The Three Processors:**

1. **Perceptual Processor (PP)** — receives raw sensory input from the eyes, ears, and skin; encodes it into a form that can be stored in the **Visual Image Store (VIS)** or **Auditory Image Store (AIS)**. **Cycle time ≈ 100 ms.**
2. **Cognitive Processor (CP)** — operates on the contents of **Working Memory (WM)** to make decisions, retrieve knowledge from Long-Term Memory, and prepare actions. **Cycle time ≈ 70 ms.** The CP is the *bottleneck* of the system because decisions are essentially serial.
3. **Motor Processor (MP)** — activates muscles to execute the chosen action (e.g., finger press, eye saccade, vocalisation). **Cycle time ≈ 70 ms.** An additional 30 ms of residual motor time is often added for limb displacement.

**The Three Memory Stores:**

1. **Visual Image Store (VIS)** — iconic, pre-categorical "after-image" of what the retina just saw. **Decay ≈ 200 ms**; capacity is large (≈ 17 items), spatial rather than verbal.
2. **Auditory Image Store (AIS)** — echoic memory of what the ear just heard. **Decay ≈ 1500 ms**; capacity ≈ 5 items.
3. **Working Memory (WM)** — the activated portion of Long-Term Memory on which the CP operates. **Capacity ≈ 7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller 1956).

**How they interact:** A stimulus is encoded by the PP into the appropriate image store, transferred to WM, processed serially by the CP, and the resulting motor plan is executed by the MP. The empirical time constants allow **quantitative prediction** of simple and choice reaction times.

**Why the MHP matters:** It provides the *clock* of the human information-processing system and is the basis for more elaborate cognitive-architecture models such as ACT-R and EPIC.

**Mark breakdown:** Three processors with cycle times (3), three memories with decay/capacity (3), explanation of interaction and one-line significance (1).

---

#### **(b)** *A user is shown a single light stimulus and must press a button. Using the MHP constants (Perceptual = 100 ms, Cognitive = 70 ms, Motor = 70 ms), predict the simple reaction time. Now, suppose the user is shown 8 lights and must press a button corresponding to the lit one. Using Hick's Law with a = 200 ms and b = 120 ms/bit, predict the choice reaction time. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Simple reaction time from the MHP:**

For a single stimulus, the perceptual, cognitive, and motor processors each execute one full cycle, serially:

$$
RT_{\text{simple}} \;=\; T_{p} + T_{c} + T_{m} \;=\; 100 \text{ ms} + 70 \text{ ms} + 70 \text{ ms} \;=\; 240 \text{ ms}
$$

(If the textbook rounded total to 200 ms by including the motor initiation inside the 70 ms motor cycle, then $RT = 100 + 70 + 30 = 200$ ms is also accepted.)

**Step 2 — Choice reaction time from Hick's Law for $n = 8$:**

Hick's Law (equally probable form) is

$$
RT \;=\; a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n)
$$

Substituting $n=8$, $a = 200$ ms, $b = 120$ ms/bit:

$$
RT_{\text{choice}} \;=\; 200 + 120 \cdot \log_{2}(8)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(8) \;=\; 3 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
RT_{\text{choice}} \;=\; 200 + 120 \times 3 \;=\; 200 + 360 \;=\; 560 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and interpretation:**

| Scenario | Number of choices | Predicted RT |
|---|---|---|
| Single stimulus (MHP) | $n = 1$ | **240 ms** |
| Eight-stimulus choice (Hick) | $n = 8$ | **560 ms** |

The choice task costs an **extra 320 ms** because the cognitive processor must execute additional cycles to discriminate among 8 alternatives (≈ 3 bits of decision information × 120 ms/bit = 360 ms above the 200 ms baseline). **Design lesson:** Reducing the number of simultaneous options a user must discriminate from (e.g., by grouping, defaults, or progressive disclosure) reduces choice reaction time logarithmically — a direct, quantitative payoff for Hick-aware interface design.

**Mark breakdown:** MHP formula and substitution (2), Hick formula and substitution (2), numerical answers (1), comparison and design lesson (2).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

#### **(a)** *Explain Fitts' Law. Define the Index of Difficulty (ID) and the Index of Performance (IP). A user must click a button of width 2 cm located 10 cm from the current cursor position. Using the Shannon form with a = 50 ms and b = 100 ms/bit, calculate the predicted movement time and the Index of Performance. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Fitts' Law — definition:**

> Fitts' Law (1954) states that the **time taken to rapidly move to a target** is a logarithmic function of the *distance* to the target and the *size* of the target. The more distant or smaller the target, the longer the movement.

**Shannon form equation:**

$$
T \;=\; a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

**Definitions:**

* **Index of Difficulty (ID):** the logarithmic term itself, measured in **bits**:
$$ ID \;=\; \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) $$
* **Index of Performance (IP):** the ratio $ID / T$, measured in **bits per second**. It is the *human motor channel capacity* — a higher IP means a faster, more accurate interface/device.

**Numerical calculation for $D = 10$ cm, $W = 2$ cm, $a = 50$ ms, $b = 100$ ms/bit:**

**Step 1 — Compute ID:**

$$
ID \;=\; \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{10}{2} + 1\right) \;=\; \log_{2}\!\left(5 + 1\right) \;=\; \log_{2}(6)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(6) \;=\; \frac{\ln 6}{\ln 2} \;=\; \frac{1.7918}{0.6931} \;\approx\; 2.5850 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T$:**

$$
T \;=\; a + b \cdot ID \;=\; 50 + 100 \times 2.5850 \;=\; 50 + 258.50 \;=\; 308.50 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \;\approx\; 308.5 \text{ ms} \;\;(\approx 0.31 \text{ s})}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $IP$:**

$$
IP \;=\; \frac{ID}{T} \;=\; \frac{2.5850 \text{ bits}}{0.3085 \text{ s}} \;\approx\; 8.38 \text{ bits/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{IP \;\approx\; 8.4 \text{ bits/s}}
$$

**Design interpretation:** An $IP$ of ~ 8 bits/s is typical of a competent mouse user, confirming that the device is performing close to the expected throughput for the given target geometry. To improve throughput, the designer should **increase $W$** (larger buttons) or **decrease $D$** (relocate the button).

**Mark breakdown:** Definition + equation (2), ID & IP definitions (1), numerical calculation of $T$ (2), numerical calculation of $IP$ (1), design interpretation (1).

---

#### **(b)** *Explain the Keystroke-Level Model (KLM). An expert user is moving a paragraph of text in a word processor. The task requires the following actions:*
*(i) Hand from keyboard to mouse (H)*
*(ii) Point to the paragraph (P)*
*(iii) Press and hold the mouse button to select the paragraph (B + drag = D — assume a 4 cm drag)*
*(iv) Point to the new location (P)*
*(v) Release the mouse button (B)*
*(vi) Hand back to keyboard (H)*
*Assume no mental preparation is needed. Using the standard KLM constants and D = 0.9 × L (in seconds, L in cm), compute the total predicted task time. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**KLM — definition:** The Keystroke-Level Model (KLM), proposed by **Card, Moran & Newell (1983)**, is a simplified GOMS model that predicts an *expert user's* task time by decomposing the task into a sequence of **primitive operators** (K, B, P, H, D, M) and **summing their empirical execution times**.

**Operator sequence for the move-paragraph task:**

| Step | Action | Operator(s) | Time (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hand keyboard → mouse | **H** | 0.40 |
| 2 | Point to the paragraph | **P** | 1.10 |
| 3 | Press mouse button (start of drag) | **B** | 0.20 |
| 4 | Drag the mouse 4 cm | **D = 0.9 × 4** | 3.60 |
| 5 | Point to the new location (continuous drag — no new P) | — | 0.00 |
| 6 | Release mouse button (end of drag) | **B** | 0.20 |
| 7 | Hand mouse → keyboard | **H** | 0.40 |

(Note: in the KLM, the "point to the new location" is *part of* the Drawing operator — it is the same continuous drag, so a *separate* P is **not** inserted in the middle of a D. This is **KLM Rule 4**.)

**Mental operators:** None are inserted by the problem statement.

**Sum the operator times:**

$$
T_{\text{KLM}} \;=\; H + P + B + D + B + H
$$

$$
T_{\text{KLM}} \;=\; 0.40 + 1.10 + 0.20 + 3.60 + 0.20 + 0.40
$$

Adding step-by-step:

$$
0.40 + 1.10 = 1.50
$$

$$
1.50 + 0.20 = 1.70
$$

$$
1.70 + 3.60 = 5.30
$$

$$
5.30 + 0.20 = 5.50
$$

$$
5.50 + 0.40 = 5.90
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{KLM}} \;=\; 5.90 \text{ s}}
$$

**Design interpretation:** The drag-to-move operation is dominated by the **D (drawing) operator** which alone consumes **3.6 s** — about **61 %** of the total time. A keyboard-only alternative (e.g., **cut-paste with Ctrl+X / Ctrl+V** in 6 keystrokes) would take only $6 \times 0.12 = 0.72$ s plus 1 mental operator of 1.20 s = **1.92 s**, a **3× speed-up**. This illustrates how KLM can be used **comparatively** to evaluate two competing interface designs *on paper* before implementation.

**Mark breakdown:** KLM definition (1), operator sequence with correct times (3), rule-4 application for the D operator (1), numerical sum (1), design interpretation (1).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 6 ways students lose marks on Module-2 HCI questions (with examiner commentary):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the unit on a constant.** Writing "$T = 0.05 + 0.10 \times 3.17$" instead of "$\;0.05\,\text{s} + 0.10\,\text{s/bit} \times 3.17\,\text{bits} = 0.367\,\text{s}$". — **−1 mark.**
> 2. **Using the original Fitts form $\log_2(2D/W)$** when the question specifies the Shannon form. — **−1 to −2 marks** depending on the examiner's strictness.
> 3. **Inserting an extra P-operator inside a D (drag) operation.** A drag is **one D**, not "P + B + ... + P + B". Apply Rule 4. — **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Adding an M-operator at the start of a menu-driven GUI task by default.** Rule 0 says M is *not* inserted unless the user must *recall* a command. — **−1 mark.**
> 5. **Quoting the wrong MHP cycle time** (e.g., 50 ms or 200 ms) instead of the canonical 100 ms (perceptual), 70 ms (cognitive), 70 ms (motor). — **−1 mark.**
> 6. **Confusing $ID$ (bits) with $IP$ (bits/s).** $ID$ is *difficulty*, $IP$ is *throughput*. They are not interchangeable. — **−1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

* [x] **Fitts' Law** is the *pointing* model. Standard form: $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ with **Shannon**. $ID$ in **bits**, $IP$ in **bits/s**.
* [x] Fitts' **design principles**: BIG targets, CLOSE targets, **infinite edge trick**, snap-to-edges, sticky corners.
* [x] **Model Human Processor (MHP)** has 3 processors (P=100 ms, C=70 ms, M=70 ms) and 3 memories (VIS, AIS, WM capacity 7±2).
* [x] **Simple RT ≈ 200 ms**; **Choice RT** is predicted by **Hick's Law**: $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)$.
* [x] **Hick's Law** says choice time grows **logarithmically** — not linearly — with the number of options.
* [x] **KLM operators**: K = 0.12 s, B = 0.20 s, P = 1.10 s, H = 0.40 s, M = 1.20 s, D = 0.9 × L (L in cm).
* [x] **KLM Insertion Rules 0–5** decide *when* to insert M-operators; **Rule 0** (no M at start of menu GUI) and **Rule 4** (no P inside a D) are the most-tested.
* [x] **KLM is for EXPERT, error-free, routine tasks**; for novice tasks use the **Power Law of Practice** $T_n = a \cdot n^{-b}$.
* [x] **Speed–Accuracy Trade-off**: faster movements increase endpoint variability — effective target width shrinks.
* [x] **Design lesson summary**: reduce Hick ($n$), reduce Fitts ($D$/$W$), reduce KLM (replace long mouse paths with keyboard shortcuts).
* [x] **Comparison framework**: when the board asks to "compare two designs", use the *same* model on both, list the operator sequences side-by-side, sum each, and state the design conclusion in **one line**.
* [x] **Always state units** (s, ms, bits, bits/s) and **always show the formula** before substituting — board valuation awards method marks explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding Business Functions. Screen Designing: Design Goals - Screen Planning and Purpose
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Understanding Business Functions. Screen Designing: Design Goals - Screen Planning and Purpose

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Understanding Business Functions

#### Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
In the context of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI), **Business Functions** refer to the structured set of activities, processes, and operations performed within an organization to accomplish specific commercial or operational objectives. A business function represents a logical grouping of tasks that produce value for stakeholders, customers, or the internal workflow of the enterprise. Common business functions include **Sales**, **Marketing**, **Finance**, **Human Resources**, **Operations**, **Supply Chain Management**, **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)**, and **Information Technology Services**.

From an HCI design perspective, understanding business functions means analyzing **how users perform work**, **what information they need**, **what decisions they make**, and **what tools/interface elements support those decisions**. This understanding forms the foundation of **user-centered design (UCD)** and **task analysis**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Business functions are studied in HCI not from a management perspective, but from a **systems design perspective** — to identify user requirements, design relevant interface flows, and align the software's usability with the organizational goals it serves.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you walk into a hospital. You see different departments — Reception, Emergency, Pharmacy, Lab, Billing, and Doctor's Cabin. Each of these is a **business function**. Now, think of the computer systems used in the hospital. The Reception desk uses one screen, the Doctor uses another, the Billing counter uses a third. Why are these screens different? Because the **business function** each person performs is different. The Receptionist needs quick search and registration, the Doctor needs patient history and prescription entry, and the Billing clerk needs cost calculation and invoice generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition for Exams:** A business function is a high-level description of the work an organization does, and HCI designers must understand these functions to design interfaces that are **contextually appropriate, role-based, and goal-oriented**.

### 1.2 Screen Designing: Design Goals

#### Formal Academic Definition
**Screen Design** is the process of planning and arranging the visual and interactive elements of a graphical user interface (GUI) on a display screen to facilitate effective human-computer interaction. **Design Goals** in screen design are the guiding principles that ensure the interface is usable, efficient, learnable, and satisfying. The primary design goals include:

- **Usability** — Ease of use with minimal cognitive load
- **Efficiency** — Quick task completion with minimal steps
- **Learnability** — Easy to learn for first-time users
- **Error Prevention & Recovery** — Minimizing user errors and providing recovery mechanisms
- **User Satisfaction** — Pleasant and engaging user experience
- **Aesthetic Appeal** — Visually pleasing layout and typography
- **Consistency** — Uniformity across screens and components
- **Accessibility** — Usable by people with diverse abilities

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Standard:** When asked about "Design Goals of Screen Design," always mention the **Eight Golden Rules of Interface Design by Ben Shneiderman** along with **Norman's Seven Principles** wherever relevant.

### 1.3 Screen Planning and Purpose

#### Formal Academic Definition
**Screen Planning** is the pre-design phase where designers determine the structure, layout, content, and navigation of each screen in the application. It involves **wireframing**, **storyboarding**, **information architecture mapping**, and **task-to-screen allocation**. The **Purpose of a Screen** defines the specific role it plays in the user's workflow — whether it is a **data entry screen**, **data display screen**, **navigation screen**, **transaction screen**, or **dashboard/summary screen**.

#### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a movie theatre. Before the film is shot, the director creates a **storyboard** — sketches of each scene with notes on what happens, who is present, and what the camera captures. **Screen planning in HCI is exactly this storyboarding process** — you sketch each screen before coding it, deciding what goes where, why it is there, and how the user moves from one screen to another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:**
> - **Screen Planning** = The *process* of deciding structure and layout (BEFORE design)
> - **Screen Purpose** = The *role* a screen plays in the user's task flow (DURING design)
> - **Screen Design** = The *actual* visual implementation (AFTER planning)

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Bold Highlighting)

The following are critical standard terms in HCI screen design that must be memorized:

- **Fitts's Law** — Time to move to a target depends on distance and size
- **Hick's Law** — Decision time increases with the number of choices
- **Miller's Law** — Working memory holds **7 ± 2** items at a time
- **GOMS** — Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules (predictive model)
- **Nielsen's Heuristics** — **10** usability principles
- **Shneiderman's Rules** — **8** golden rules of interface design
- **ISO 9241-11** — Standard defining **Effectiveness**, **Efficiency**, and **Satisfaction**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Business Functions — Analytical Breakdown

A business function can be decomposed into the following structured layers:

1. **Strategic Layer (Top-Level Function)**
   - Defines the overall mission of the function (e.g., "Manage customer relationships")
   - Aligned with organizational vision

2. **Tactical Layer (Sub-Functions)**
   - Specific capabilities within the function (e.g., "Lead tracking," "Complaint resolution")
   - Maps to software modules

3. **Operational Layer (Tasks)**
   - Day-to-day activities performed by users (e.g., "Enter lead details," "Generate invoice")
   - Maps to individual screens and forms

4. **Interaction Layer (User Actions)**
   - Specific clicks, keystrokes, and decisions made by users
   - Maps to UI elements (buttons, fields, menus)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in HCI:** A designer who understands the business function can correctly allocate screens, design appropriate workflows, and avoid overloading or under-supporting the user.

### 2.2 Business Functions — HCI Mapping Table

The following table maps common business functions to their HCI design implications:

| Business Function | User Roles Involved | Typical Screens | Key HCI Considerations |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sales | Sales Executive, Manager | Lead entry, Order form, Invoice | Quick data entry, autocomplete, validations |
| Marketing | Marketing Analyst | Campaign dashboard, Analytics | Data visualization, filtering, charts |
| Finance | Accountant, CFO | Transaction entry, Reports | Numeric input accuracy, audit trails |
| Human Resources | HR Manager, Employee | Employee form, Payroll screen | Privacy, role-based access, form validation |
| Operations | Supervisor, Worker | Inventory, Schedule | Real-time updates, clear status indicators |
| Customer Support | Agent, Customer | Ticket screen, Chat interface | Quick navigation, search, history view |

### 2.3 Design Goals of Screen Design — Detailed Analysis

The Eight Golden Rules (Shneiderman, 1987) form the backbone of screen design goals:

1. **Strive for Consistency** — Same terminology, actions, and layouts across screens
2. **Cater to Universal Usability** — Design for diverse users (novice to expert)
3. **Offer Informative Feedback** — Every action should produce a visible response
4. **Design Dialogs to Yield Closure** — Group actions into clear beginning, middle, end
5. **Prevent Errors** — Design constraints so users cannot make serious mistakes
6. **Permit Easy Reversal of Actions** — Undo/redo for all actions
7. **Support Internal Locus of Control** — User feels in charge, not the system
8. **Reduce Short-Term Memory Load** — Keep displays simple, limit choices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For 14-mark questions:** Always relate design goals to **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** for completeness.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipe `|` characters used in any formula below to prevent markdown breakage.**

| Formula / Law | Mathematical Form | Units | Application in Screen Design |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fitts's Law | $T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{D}{W}\right)$ | Time (ms), Distance (px), Width (px) | Larger targets closer to user reduce selection time |
| Hick's Law | $T = b \cdot \log_{2}(n + 1)$ | Time (ms), n = number of choices | Fewer menu items reduce decision time |
| Miller's Law | Working Memory $= 7 \pm 2$ chunks | Items | Group navigation into max 7 categories |
| Steering Law | $T = a + b \cdot \frac{D}{L}$ | Time, Distance, Length | Time to navigate through a menu path |
| ISO 9241-11 Usability | $E = \frac{\text{Goals Achieved}}{\text{Total Goals}}$ | Ratio (0 to 1) | Measures effectiveness |
| Throughput | $TP = \frac{\text{Total Tasks}}{\text{Total Time}}$ | Tasks per second | Measures efficiency |
| Error Rate | $ER = \frac{\text{Errors}}{\text{Total Actions}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Measures reliability |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding business functions and screen design goals is critical in:

- **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) software** (SAP, Oracle) — where role-based screens are designed for each business function
- **Hospital Management Systems (HMS)** — where every department has a unique screen design
- **E-commerce platforms** (Amazon, Flipkart) — where customer, seller, and admin screens differ based on business function
- **Banking applications** — where transaction, account management, and compliance screens are purpose-specific
- **Air Traffic Control interfaces** — where screen purpose and goal-oriented design are life-critical
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fitts's Law for Screen Design

**Fitts's Law** is foundational for understanding target size and distance in screen design. Let us derive and apply it step-by-step.

#### Given Information
- A user must move the mouse pointer from point A to a target button on the screen
- Distance from current pointer position to target center: $D$ (in pixels)
- Width of the target button: $W$ (in pixels)
- Empirical constants (from experiments): $a \approx 50$ ms, $b \approx 150$ ms/bit

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the Index of Difficulty (ID)**

The Index of Difficulty quantifies how hard it is to reach a target. It is measured in **bits** (because of the logarithmic base 2).

$$
ID = \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{D}{W}\right) \quad \text{[bits]}
$$

**Logic:** If the target is very small (small $W$) and very far (large $D$), the ID is high → difficult to reach. If the target is large and close, ID is low → easy to reach.

**Step 2: Define the Movement Time (MT) Formula**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID
$$

**Logic:** Total time is the sum of a base reaction time ($a$) plus the time proportional to the difficulty index.

**Step 3: Substitute ID into MT**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{D}{W}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Apply to a Real Screen Design Scenario**

Consider a "Submit" button on a registration form:
- Distance from current cursor position to button: $D = 300$ pixels
- Width of the button: $W = 80$ pixels
- Constants: $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit

Compute ID first:
$$
ID = \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{300}{80}\right)
$$

$$
ID = \log_{2}\left(1 + 3.75\right)
$$

$$
ID = \log_{2}\left(4.75\right)
$$

$$
ID \approx 2.247 \text{ bits}
$$

Now compute MT:
$$
MT = 50 + 150 \cdot 2.247
$$

$$
MT = 50 + 337.05
$$

$$
MT \approx 387.05 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation for the Designer**

If the button is moved closer (say, $D = 100$ px) or made larger (say, $W = 120$ px), the time reduces significantly:
$$
ID_{\text{new}} = \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{100}{120}\right) = \log_{2}(1.833) \approx 0.874 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
MT_{\text{new}} = 50 + 150 \cdot 0.874 \approx 181.1 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule from Fitts's Law:** Make important and frequently-used buttons **large**, place them **close** to the user's current focus point, and place related actions **adjacent to each other**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Hick's Law for Menu Design

**Step 1: Define the Problem**

A user must select one option from a menu containing $n$ equally probable choices. We want to predict the decision time $T$.

**Step 2: Apply Hick's Law**

$$
T = b \cdot \log_{2}(n + 1)
$$

where $b \approx 150$ ms/bit (empirical constant).

**Step 3: Numerical Example — 4 items vs 16 items**

For $n = 4$:
$$
T_{4} = 150 \cdot \log_{2}(5) = 150 \cdot 2.322 \approx 348.3 \text{ ms}
$$

For $n = 16$:
$$
T_{16} = 150 \cdot \log_{2}(17) = 150 \cdot 4.087 \approx 613.05 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4: Design Implication**

> [!NOTE]
> Reducing menu items from 16 to 4 (by hierarchical grouping) reduces decision time by **approximately 43.2%**. This justifies **hierarchical menu structures** in HCI.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Screen Types and Their Purposes

| Screen Type | Primary Purpose | Key Elements | Business Function Context | Critical Design Goal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data Entry Screen | Collect user input | Text fields, dropdowns, checkboxes | HR onboarding, Sales order | Error prevention, validation |
| Data Display Screen | Show information | Tables, lists, labels | Reports, dashboards | Readability, scannability |
| Navigation Screen | Help user move | Menus, tabs, breadcrumbs | Site maps, app launchers | Consistency, learnability |
| Transaction Screen | Process an action | Confirmation dialogs, action buttons | Payment, booking | Confirmation, undo support |
| Dashboard Screen | Summarize KPIs | Charts, graphs, KPIs | Analytics, management | Glanceable, prioritized |
| Search Screen | Locate information | Search box, filters, results | Customer support, library | Fast results, refinement options |
| Wizard Screen | Step-by-step guidance | Progress bar, next/back buttons | Installation, complex forms | Closure, feedback |

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Aligning Screen Design with Business Functions

**Step 1: Identify the Business Function**
Example: *Customer Complaint Management*

**Step 2: Identify User Roles within the Function**
- Customer (files complaint)
- Support Agent (resolves complaint)
- Supervisor (monitors complaints)

**Step 3: Map Roles to Screens**
- Customer → Complaint Form, Status Check
- Agent → Ticket List, Ticket Detail, Resolution Form
- Supervisor → Dashboard, Reports

**Step 4: Apply Design Goals to Each Screen**

For the **Agent's Ticket Detail Screen**:
- **Consistency** — Use the same colors, fonts, and button styles as other agent screens
- **Feedback** — Show real-time status changes (e.g., "Ticket escalated")
- **Error Prevention** — Validate resolution notes before submission
- **Reversibility** — Allow status rollback if a wrong action is taken
- **Memory Load** — Show ticket history in a collapsible panel, not all at once

**Step 5: Validate with Heuristics**
Walk through Nielsen's 10 heuristics and confirm each is satisfied.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram: Hierarchical Mapping of Business Functions to Screens

The following Mermaid block diagrams how business functions decompose into sub-functions, tasks, and finally into individual screens.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Organization Mission] --> B[Business Function: Sales]
    A --> C[Business Function: Marketing]
    A --> D[Business Function: Finance]

    B --> B1[Sub-Function: Lead Management]
    B --> B2[Sub-Function: Order Processing]
    B --> B3[Sub-Function: Invoicing]

    B1 --> B1T1[Task: Add Lead]
    B1 --> B1T2[Task: Update Lead Status]

    B2 --> B2T1[Task: Create Order]
    B2 --> B2T2[Task: Approve Order]

    B3 --> B3T1[Task: Generate Invoice]
    B3 --> B3T2[Task: Track Payment]

    B1T1 --> S1[Screen: Lead Entry Form]
    B1T2 --> S2[Screen: Lead Status Board]
    B2T1 --> S3[Screen: Order Creation Screen]
    B2T2 --> S4[Screen: Order Approval Screen]
    B3T1 --> S5[Screen: Invoice Generator]
    B3T2 --> S6[Screen: Payment Tracker Dashboard]
```

### 4.2 Diagram: Screen Planning Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This block diagram represents the structured process of screen planning from concept to delivery.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Requirement Analysis] --> P2[Step 2: Task Analysis]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: User Persona Definition]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Information Architecture]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Wireframing]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Screen Flow Design]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Prototype Creation]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Usability Testing]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Iteration and Final Design]

    P1 -.feedback.-> P3
    P5 -.feedback.-> P4
    P8 -.feedback.-> P5
```

### 4.3 Diagram: Screen Purpose Classification (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SC[Screen Categories by Purpose] --> SC1[Data Entry Screens]
    SC --> SC2[Data Display Screens]
    SC --> SC3[Navigation Screens]
    SC --> SC4[Transaction Screens]
    SC --> SC5[Feedback Screens]

    SC1 --> SC1A[Forms, Input Fields]
    SC1 --> SC1B[Validation Messages]

    SC2 --> SC2A[Tables, Reports]
    SC2 --> SC2B[Charts, Dashboards]

    SC3 --> SC3A[Menus, Tabs]
    SC3 --> SC3B[Search Bars]

    SC4 --> SC4A[Confirmation Dialogs]
    SC4 --> SC4B[Action Buttons]

    SC5 --> SC5A[Success Messages]
    SC5 --> SC5B[Error Notifications]
    SC5 --> SC5C[Progress Indicators]
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Relationship Between Business Functions and Design Goals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BF[Business Function Analysis] --> UP[User Profiling]
    BF --> TA[Task Analysis]

    UP --> DG1[Design Goal: Learnability]
    UP --> DG2[Design Goal: Accessibility]

    TA --> DG3[Design Goal: Efficiency]
    TA --> DG4[Design Goal: Error Prevention]

    DG1 --> SD[Screen Design Decisions]
    DG2 --> SD
    DG3 --> SD
    DG4 --> SD

    SD --> OUT1[Consistent Layouts]
    SD --> OUT2[Feedback Mechanisms]
    SD --> OUT3[Intuitive Navigation]
    SD --> OUT4[Validation Rules]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each design goal flows naturally from the analysis of business functions. Skipping the business function analysis step leads to screens that are aesthetically pleasing but functionally misaligned with user needs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define business functions in the context of HCI. Why is understanding them important for screen design?**
> `[Module 2 | 3 Marks | CO2 | Bloom Level: Remember/Understand]`

**Model Answer:**
In HCI, business functions refer to the structured set of activities an organization performs to achieve its goals, such as sales, marketing, finance, and operations. Understanding business functions is critical for screen design because it enables the designer to identify user roles, map tasks to interface elements, and ensure that the software supports real workflows. Without this understanding, screens may look good but fail to support actual user tasks, leading to poor usability and low productivity.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. List and briefly explain any THREE design goals of screen design.**
> `[Module 2 | 3 Marks | CO2 | Bloom Level: Understand]`

**Model Answer:**
The three design goals of screen design are:
1. **Usability** — The interface should be easy to learn and use, requiring minimal training for the user.
2. **Consistency** — Similar actions, terminology, and layouts should be used across all screens to reduce learning time.
3. **Error Prevention** — The system should be designed to prevent users from making mistakes, for example, by disabling invalid options or validating input.

---

### 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
> **Q. (a)** Explain the concept of business functions in HCI. Discuss how understanding business functions influences the design of user interfaces with a suitable example. **[(7 Marks)]**
> **(b)** Explain the design goals of screen design. Discuss how Shneiderman's Eight Golden Rules can be applied to create effective user interfaces. **[(7 Marks)]**
> `[Module 2 | 14 Marks | CO2 | Bloom Level: Understand/Apply]`

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A — 14 Marks Model Solution

**Part (a) — Business Functions in HCI (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Definition (2 marks)**
A business function is a high-level activity or process that an organization performs to fulfill its mission, such as sales, finance, HR, or customer support. In HCI, business functions are studied to understand what users do, why they do it, and what tools they need.

**Step 2: Why Understanding Business Functions Matters (2 marks)**
- It helps designers identify **user roles** and **permissions**
- It enables **task-to-screen mapping**
- It ensures the interface supports **real workflows** rather than imaginary ones
- It supports **role-based access control (RBAC)** design

**Step 3: Example — Hospital Management System (3 marks)**

Consider a Hospital Management System. The business functions are:
- **Reception** → Patient registration, appointment booking
- **Doctor** → View patient history, write prescription
- **Pharmacy** → Dispense medicines, check stock
- **Billing** → Generate invoice, accept payment

Each function requires **different screens** designed for different user goals:
- Reception needs **fast search and registration forms**
- Doctor needs **patient history and prescription entry screens**
- Billing needs **transaction confirmation and invoice screens**

Thus, the interface is **context-aware** and **role-specific**, directly derived from business function analysis.

**Part (b) — Design Goals and Shneiderman's Rules (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Design Goals Overview (2 marks)**
Design goals are principles that guide the creation of effective screens. They include usability, efficiency, learnability, error prevention, satisfaction, accessibility, and consistency.

**Step 2: Shneiderman's Eight Golden Rules (5 marks)**

1. **Strive for Consistency** — Use the same color for "Save" across all screens.
2. **Cater to Universal Usability** — Provide shortcuts for experts and help text for novices.
3. **Offer Informative Feedback** — Show a green checkmark after a successful save.
4. **Design Dialogs to Yield Closure** — Group actions into clear sequences (e.g., "Add to Cart → Checkout → Confirmation").
5. **Prevent Errors** — Disable the "Submit" button until all mandatory fields are filled.
6. **Permit Easy Reversal of Actions** — Provide an "Undo" button after deletion.
7. **Support Internal Locus of Control** — Avoid modal popups that lock the user out of the system.
8. **Reduce Short-Term Memory Load** — Show recently accessed items, avoid hidden menus.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students only list the rules without giving **concrete UI examples**. Always pair each rule with a **real interface example** to score full marks. Also, students often forget to **introduce** what Shneiderman's Rules are before listing them — this costs 0.5–1 mark.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B — 14 Marks Model Solution (Alternative Choice)

**Part (a) — Screen Planning Process (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Definition of Screen Planning (2 marks)**
Screen planning is the pre-design phase in which designers determine the structure, content, and navigation of each screen. It involves wireframing, storyboarding, and information architecture before actual visual design.

**Step 2: Phases of Screen Planning (3 marks)**
1. **Requirement Gathering** — Interview users, observe workflows
2. **Task Analysis** — Break down business functions into tasks
3. **Wireframing** — Sketch low-fidelity layouts
4. **Storyboarding** — Visualize user flow across screens
5. **Prototype Creation** — Build interactive mockups
6. **Usability Testing** — Validate with real users

**Step 3: Example — E-commerce Checkout Flow (2 marks)**
- Screen 1: Cart Summary
- Screen 2: Address Selection
- Screen 3: Payment Method
- Screen 4: Order Confirmation

Each screen has a **clear purpose** and is **linked** to the next via navigation buttons.

**Part (b) — Screen Purpose and Classification (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: What is Screen Purpose? (2 marks)**
The purpose of a screen is the specific role it plays in the user's task flow — whether it is to collect data, display data, enable navigation, or process a transaction.

**Step 2: Types of Screens by Purpose (3 marks)**
1. **Data Entry Screens** — Forms for user input
2. **Data Display Screens** — Reports, tables, dashboards
3. **Navigation Screens** — Menus, tabs, home screens
4. **Transaction Screens** — Payment, booking, confirmation
5. **Feedback Screens** — Success, error, progress messages

**Step 3: How Purpose Drives Design (2 marks)**
- Data entry screens need **clear labels and validation**
- Display screens need **good typography and scannable layouts**
- Navigation screens need **consistency and clear icons**
- Transaction screens need **confirmation dialogs and undo support**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Area:** Students often **describe** screen types but fail to **explain how the purpose influences the design decisions**. Always link "purpose" to "design choices" explicitly.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Generic answers** — Writing only definitions without HCI-specific examples
> 2. **Missing linkages** — Not connecting business functions to design goals
> 3. **No diagrams** — Failing to include wireframes or flow diagrams in long answers
> 4. **Forgetting to introduce** — Listing Nielsen's/Shneiderman's rules without context
> 5. **Skipping Miller's Law and Fitts's Law** — These are high-weightage in KTU board exams

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Business Function** = A logical grouping of organizational activities (Sales, HR, Finance, etc.)
- ✅ HCI studies business functions to **align interface design with user workflows**
- ✅ Business functions are decomposed into **Strategic → Tactical → Operational → Interaction** layers
- ✅ **Design Goals** of screen design include: Usability, Efficiency, Learnability, Error Prevention, Satisfaction, Consistency, Accessibility, Aesthetics
- ✅ **Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules** are foundational design goals
- ✅ **Screen Planning** happens BEFORE design — it includes wireframing, storyboarding, IA mapping
- ✅ **Screen Purpose** defines the role of a screen in user workflow
- ✅ **Five types of screens** by purpose: Entry, Display, Navigation, Transaction, Feedback
- ✅ **Fitts's Law** says: $MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(1 + D/W)$ — larger, closer targets are faster to select
- ✅ **Hick's Law** says: $T = b \cdot \log_{2}(n + 1)$ — fewer choices = faster decisions
- ✅ **Miller's Law** says: Working memory holds **7 ± 2** items
- ✅ **ISO 9241-11** defines usability via **Effectiveness, Efficiency, Satisfaction**
- ✅ Always pair design principles with **real UI examples** in exam answers
- ✅ Diagrams (wireframes, flowcharts) carry **bonus marks** in 14-mark questions
- ✅ Map business functions to **user roles** to **tasks** to **screens** for structured answers
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organizing Screen Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Organizing Screen Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Organizing Screen Elements):**
> *Organizing Screen Elements* refers to the systematic application of **visual design principles**, **perceptual psychology**, and **Gestalt laws of perception** to structure, group, align, and prioritize on-screen components (such as text, icons, controls, images, and whitespace) so that users can **scan, locate, comprehend, and interact** with information efficiently while minimizing **cognitive load** and maximizing **aesthetic clarity**.

In simpler academic terms, it is the **architectural planning of the visual surface** of an interactive system — deciding *what goes where, why, and in what order of importance*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Newspaper Front Page**
> Think of a screen like the **front page of a leading newspaper** (e.g., *The Hindu* or *The Times of India*). Have you ever noticed that:
> - The **headline** is in the **largest font**, placed at the **top-left** (where Western readers begin scanning).
> - Related news items (sports, business, entertainment) are **boxed into separate columns**.
> - There is **breathing room (whitespace)** between columns so your eyes don't get tired.
> - Photos are placed **near the most important story**, not randomly.
> - The page follows a **predictable grid** every single day.
>
> Now, replace the newspaper with a **mobile app screen, a desktop dashboard, or an ATM interface** — the principles are identical. A well-organized screen is one where the user **never has to *think* about where to look next**; the structure guides them intuitively.

---

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standards Used in KTU Evaluation

| Metric / Standard | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Optimum Information Density** | **30%–60%** screen coverage (per Tullis, 1988) |
| **Recommended Whitespace Ratio** | **Minimum 20%** of total screen area |
| **Line Length (Readability)** | **45–75 characters** per line |
| **Font Size (Body Text)** | **12–14 pt** for desktop, **16+ pt** for mobile |
| **Maximum Distinct Colors** | **5 ± 2** (Miller's Law) |
| **Field of View (Foveal)** | **2°–5°** for sharp focus |
| **F-Pattern Reading Hotspots** | Top-left, top-right, left-margin mid-screen |

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in HCI (KTU Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU **Course Outcome CO2** of PECPT414, students must *"Apply design principles in organizing screen elements for effective user interaction."* This topic directly tests:
> - **Perceptual psychology** (Gestalt laws)
> - **Visual hierarchy design**
> - **Layout pattern recognition**
> - **Cognitive load minimization**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Screen Organization (Robin Williams — *CRAP* Principles)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRAP** is a widely-taught framework (Robin Williams, *The Non-Designer's Design Book*) for organizing visual elements on any screen.

### Pillar 1: **Contrast**
- The visual difference between elements that makes them distinguishable.
- Achieved through variation in **size, color, weight, font, position, texture**.
- **Rule:** If two elements are *not the same*, make them *very different* — never *almost the same*.

### Pillar 2: **Repetition**
- Repeating visual elements (colors, fonts, shapes, icon styles) throughout the interface to create **unity and consistency**.
- **Rule:** Repeat at least one element across *every* screen of the application.

### Pillar 3: **Alignment**
- Every element must have a **visual connection** with another element on the screen.
- **Rule:** Nothing should be placed arbitrarily; even "random" elements should align to an invisible grid.

### Pillar 4: **Proximity**
- Related items should be **grouped close together**; unrelated items should be **spaced apart**.
- **Rule:** Proximity creates *implicit relationships* without using lines, boxes, or arrows.

---

## 2.2 Gestalt Principles of Perceptual Organization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **psychological laws** the human visual system applies *automatically* when interpreting a screen. KTU frequently tests these.

| # | Principle | Definition | Screen Design Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Proximity** | Objects close together are perceived as a group | Cluster related form fields; separate unrelated sections with whitespace |
| **2** | **Similarity** | Objects with similar visual properties are grouped | Use the same color for all "Submit" buttons across screens |
| **3** | **Closure** | The mind completes incomplete shapes | Use partial borders or dashed lines; users mentally "close" the form |
| **4** | **Continuity** | The eye follows smooth paths/lines | Align icons along a vertical axis; use breadcrumb trails |
| **5** | **Figure-Ground** | The mind separates an object from its background | Use contrasting colors for modal dialogs over dimmed backgrounds |
| **6** | **Common Region** | Elements within the same enclosed area are grouped | Group settings inside a bordered "Preferences" box |
| **7** | **Symmetry & Order** | Symmetrical elements are perceived as a unified group | Place OK/Cancel buttons symmetrically at the bottom of dialogs |
| **8** | **Common Fate** | Elements moving in the same direction are grouped | Animate related items together during transitions |

---

## 2.3 Visual Reading Patterns (Eye-Tracking Models)

These are **empirically validated** scan paths that users follow on screens.

### 2.3.1 The **F-Pattern** (Jakob Nielsen, 2006)
- Users reading **text-heavy screens** (e.g., search results, articles) scan in an "F" shape:
  1. **Horizontal scan** across the top.
  2. **Second horizontal scan** slightly lower, shorter.
  3. **Vertical scan** down the left side.
- **Design Implication:** Place critical content and CTAs (*Call-to-Actions*) along the **left margin** and **top of the screen**.

### 2.3.2 The **Z-Pattern**
- Users on **visually-rich, low-text screens** (e.g., landing pages, dashboards) scan in a "Z" shape:
  1. Top-left → Top-right (horizontal).
  2. Top-right → Bottom-left (diagonal).
  3. Bottom-left → Bottom-right (horizontal).
- **Design Implication:** Place **logo top-left**, **primary CTA top-right**, and **secondary CTA bottom-right**.

### 2.3.3 The **Gutenberg Diagram**
- Divides the screen into four quadrants:
  - **Optical Center** (slightly above geometric center) = primary attention.
  - **Fallowswept** (bottom-left) = terminal area.
  - **Strong Fallow** (top-right) = secondary attention.
  - **Weak Fallow** (bottom-left) = least attention.
- **Design Implication:** Place key messages in the optical center.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Heuristic Sheet

| Formula / Heuristic | Expression | Units / Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Tullis Information Density** | $D = \frac{\text{Number of characters displayed}}{\text{Total screen area}}$ | $D \in [0.30, 0.60]$ (30%–60% coverage) |
| **Miller's Magical Number** | $C = 7 \pm 2$ chunks of information | Short-term memory limit |
| **Fitts's Law (for clickable targets)** | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $MT$ = movement time, $D$ = distance, $W$ = target width |
| **Hick's Law (for choices)** | $T = k \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | $T$ = decision time, $n$ = number of choices |
| **Whitespace Ratio** | $R_w = \frac{A_{\text{whitespace}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$ | Recommended $R_w \geq 0.20$ |
| **Readability Line Length** | $L_c = 45$ to $75$ | Characters per line |
| **Contrast Ratio (WCAG 2.1)** | $CR = \frac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05}$ | $L_1$ = lighter, $L_2$ = darker luminance; $\geq 4.5:1$ for normal text |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You are **not required to derive** Fitts's or Hick's Law from scratch, but you **must be able to apply them** to evaluate button placement, menu structure, and screen density problems.

---

## 2.5 Screen Element Categories (What Gets Organized)

According to KTU Module 2 syllabus, screen elements fall into **four functional classes**:

1. **Informational Elements** — text, labels, data values, tooltips.
2. **Navigational Elements** — menus, tabs, breadcrumbs, links, pagination.
3. **Functional/Command Elements** — buttons, checkboxes, radio buttons, sliders, dropdowns.
4. **Feedback/Status Elements** — progress bars, error messages, notifications, breadcrumbs.

**Organizing** these means deciding their **spatial relationship, sequence, and grouping** so that related functions sit together and unrelated functions are visually separated.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Information Density (Tullis's Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks students to **compute and evaluate the information density** of a given screen. The derivation below is the standard approach.

### Step 1: Define the Information Density Formula

Information density is defined as the ratio of meaningful characters (or pixels) to the total screen area.

$$
D = \frac{N_c}{A_s}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = information density (dimensionless ratio, typically expressed as percentage)
- $N_c$ = total number of displayable characters (or character-equivalent pixels)
- $A_s$ = total available screen area (in pixels² or character cells)

### Step 2: Normalize the Density

Tullis's empirical studies (1988) found that users perform optimally when:

$$
0.30 \leq D \leq 0.60
$$

This is the **operational range**. We can express it as:

$$
D_{\text{min}} = 0.30, \quad D_{\text{max}} = 0.60
$$

### Step 3: Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A 1920×1080 monitor displays a screen containing 1,245,000 characters. Compute the information density and comment on whether the screen is over- or under-loaded.

**Step 3.1:** Calculate total screen area in pixels.

$$
A_s = 1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels}^2
$$

**Step 3.2:** Assume standard character cell = 1 pixel² for this calculation.

$$
A_s = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ character cells}
$$

**Step 3.3:** Apply the density formula.

$$
D = \frac{N_c}{A_s} = \frac{1{,}245{,}000}{2{,}073{,}600}
$$

**Step 3.4:** Perform the division.

$$
D = 0.6004 \approx 60.04\%
$$

**Step 3.5:** Compare with the optimal range.

$$
D = 0.6004 \quad \text{vs.} \quad D_{\text{max}} = 0.60
$$

Since $D$ is **just slightly above** $D_{\text{max}}$, the screen is **at the upper limit of acceptable density** — it risks being perceived as **cluttered**. **Recommendation:** Add 5–10% more whitespace or split into two screens.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** This is a *guaranteed 7-mark question* in either Part A or as a sub-part of Part B.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Decision Time using Hick's Law

### Step 1: State Hick's Law

The time taken for a user to make a decision among $n$ equally-probable choices is:

$$
T = k \cdot \log_2(n+1)
$$

Where:
- $T$ = average decision time (seconds)
- $k$ = empirical constant ($\approx 0.155$ s for skilled users, $\approx 0.300$ s for novice users)
- $n$ = number of choices

### Step 2: Compare Two Menu Designs

> **Problem:** A designer is choosing between a menu with $n_1 = 4$ items vs. $n_2 = 16$ items. Using $k = 0.2$ s, compute and compare decision times.

**For $n_1 = 4$:**

$$
T_1 = 0.2 \cdot \log_2(4 + 1) = 0.2 \cdot \log_2(5)
$$

$$
\log_2(5) = \frac{\ln 5}{\ln 2} = \frac{1.6094}{0.6931} \approx 2.3219
$$

$$
T_1 = 0.2 \times 2.3219 = 0.4644 \text{ seconds}
$$

**For $n_2 = 16$:**

$$
T_2 = 0.2 \cdot \log_2(16 + 1) = 0.2 \cdot \log_2(17)
$$

$$
\log_2(17) = \frac{\ln 17}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.8332}{0.6931} \approx 4.0875
$$

$$
T_2 = 0.2 \times 4.0875 = 0.8175 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Difference**

$$
\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 0.8175 - 0.4644 = 0.3531 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
\text{Percentage increase} = \frac{\Delta T}{T_1} \times 100\% = \frac{0.3531}{0.4644} \times 100\% \approx 76.03\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Quadrupling the menu items from 4 to 16 increases decision time by **~76%**. **Design Recommendation:** Apply **Miller's Law** ($7 \pm 2$) — group the 16 items into 2–3 hierarchical submenus of $7 \pm 2$ items each.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Gestalt Principles to Screen Layout

| Screen Element | Applicable Gestalt Principle | Design Action |
|---|---|---|
| Form fields (Name, Email, Phone) | **Proximity** + **Common Region** | Place all 3 fields inside one bordered box, with 8px vertical spacing between them and 24px from the section title |
| Submit / Cancel buttons | **Similarity** + **Figure-Ground** | Use identical button shapes; make Submit (primary) higher contrast than Cancel (secondary) |
| Dashboard cards (Stats, Charts) | **Continuity** + **Symmetry** | Align all cards to a 12-column grid; ensure equal heights |
| Modal dialog over a dimmed screen | **Figure-Ground** | Apply a 50% black overlay behind the dialog to focus attention |
| Top navigation bar | **Repetition** | Keep logo, menu, profile icon in identical positions on every page |
| Animated menu expanding | **Common Fate** | Animate all submenu items sliding down together (same direction, same speed) |
| Progress indicator dots (Step 1 ● ○ ○) | **Closure** | Use 3 circles; the filled one "completes" the user's mental model of progress |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Framework: Five-Step Screen Organization Process

1. **Identify User Tasks** — What is the user trying to accomplish on this screen?
2. **Group Tasks (Chunking)** — Apply Miller's Law to cluster tasks into 5–9 groups.
3. **Apply Visual Hierarchy** — Assign importance levels (1° action, 2° action, 3° action).
4. **Choose a Layout Pattern** — Select F-pattern, Z-pattern, or Grid based on content type.
5. **Validate with Heuristics** — Check against Nielsen's 10 heuristics and the CRAP principles.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In 14-mark Part B questions, the model answer expects students to *explicitly mention* which Gestalt principle or CRAP rule they are applying at each design decision. Merely stating "use whitespace" is insufficient — you must say *"I apply the **Proximity** principle by separating the login form from the navigation bar with 30px of whitespace."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Screen Organization Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Opens Screen] --> B[Identify Primary Task]
    B --> C[Apply Miller 7 plus or minus 2 Chunking]
    C --> D[Group Elements by Proximity and Common Region]
    D --> E[Establish Visual Hierarchy]
    E --> F[Select Layout Pattern]
    F --> F1[F Pattern for Text Heavy]
    F --> F2[Z Pattern for Visual Landing]
    F --> F3[Grid for Dashboards]
    F --> G[Apply CRAP Principles]
    G --> G1[Contrast]
    G --> G2[Repetition]
    G --> G3[Alignment]
    G --> G4[Proximity]
    G --> H[Validate with Gestalt Laws]
    H --> H1[Figure Ground]
    H --> H2[Similarity]
    H --> H3[Continuity]
    H --> I[Check Information Density 30 to 60 percent]
    I --> J[Final Screen Ready]
    J --> K[User Task Completion]

    style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style J fill:#27AE60,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#27AE60,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F1 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F2 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F3 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G1 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G2 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G3 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G4 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Visual Hierarchy of a Typical Login Screen

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Top Tier Primary Focus]
        L1[Logo Top Left Z Pattern Anchor]
        H1[Heading Sign In to Account]
    end
    subgraph S2[Middle Tier Form Zone Proximity Grouped]
        F1[Username Field]
        F2[Password Field]
        F3[Forgot Password Link]
    end
    subgraph S3[Bottom Tier Action Zone Common Region]
        B1[Secondary Cancel Button Low Contrast]
        B2[Primary Submit Button High Contrast Figure Ground]
    end

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: Screen Element Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Screen to Design] --> Q1{Content Type?}
    Q1 -->|Text Heavy| QT[Choose F Pattern Layout]
    Q1 -->|Visual Heavy| QV[Choose Z Pattern Layout]

    QT --> A1[Place Headlines Top Left]
    QV --> A2[Place CTA Top Right and Bottom Right]
    QG --> A3[Use 12 Column Grid for Cards]

    A3 --> V1
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Gestalt principle of *Proximity* and explain how it is used to organize screen elements. Give one example.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** The Gestalt principle of *Proximity* states that **objects placed close to one another are perceived by the user as belonging to the same group**, while objects spaced apart are perceived as belonging to separate groups.
>
> **Application to Screen Design (1 mark):** In interface design, related elements (e.g., a form's *username* and *password* fields) are placed within **8–12 pixels** of each other, while unrelated sections (e.g., the login form and the navigation menu) are separated by **at least 30–40 pixels of whitespace**. This implicit grouping eliminates the need for boxes, borders, or lines.
>
> **Example (1 mark):** On Google's search results page, the title, URL, and meta-description of each result are tightly grouped (proximity), while distinct results are separated by larger vertical gaps, signalling that they are independent units.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the F-Pattern in screen design? In which type of screens is it most applicable?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **F-Pattern** is an **eye-tracking scan path** discovered by Jakob Nielsen (2006) that describes how users read **text-heavy web pages**. It consists of:
> 1. A **horizontal scan** across the top of the screen.
> 2. A **second, shorter horizontal scan** below the first.
> 3. A **vertical scan** down the **left side** of the page.
>
> This produces a shape resembling the letter **"F"**.
>
> **Applicability (1 mark):** It is most applicable to **text-dense screens** such as **search engine results pages (SERPs), news articles, blogs, and Wikipedia-like content pages** where users skim for keywords rather than read every word.
>
> **Design Implication (1 mark):** Designers must place **primary headlines, navigation, and call-to-action (CTA) elements along the left margin and top of the screen** to align with the F-pattern's high-attention zones.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Below are **two alternative questions** (A and B). Each contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]**

> **(a)** Explain the **CRAP principles** of screen organization in detail. For each principle, give **one concrete screen design example**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A designer is creating the **home screen of a hospital appointment-booking mobile app**. Apply the **Five-Step Screen Organization Process** (Identify Tasks → Chunking → Visual Hierarchy → Layout Pattern → Heuristic Validation) to design the screen. Justify each design decision using either a **Gestalt principle** or a **HCI heuristic**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 marks**

> **CRAP is an acronym coined by Robin Williams** in *The Non-Designer's Design Book* and refers to four foundational principles of visual design used in HCI screen organization.

**1. Contrast (2 marks)**
- **Definition:** Visual differentiation between elements. Achieved via variations in size, color, weight, or font.
- **Example:** On a banking app's transaction screen, the *primary "Pay Now" button* is rendered in **solid dark blue with white bold text**, while the *secondary "Cancel" button* uses a **light grey outline with grey text**. The contrast hierarchy instantly tells the user which action is the dominant one.
- **Design Rule:** If two elements are not the same, make them **very different** — never *almost* the same.

**2. Repetition (2 marks)**
- **Definition:** Reusing visual elements (colors, fonts, icon styles) across the interface to create unity.
- **Example:** All "Submit"-type buttons across the app use the **same blue color, same rounded rectangle shape, and same 16pt bold font**, so the user instantly recognizes them as actionable submits on any screen.
- **Design Rule:** Repeat at least one element (color, font, or shape) consistently across all screens.

**3. Alignment (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Every element must have a visual connection with another element on the screen.
- **Example:** All form field labels and input boxes on a sign-up screen are **left-aligned to the same invisible vertical axis**, creating a clean, scannable form.

**4. Proximity (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Grouping related items close together; spacing unrelated items apart.
- **Example:** In a checkout screen, the *card-number, expiry-date, and CVV* fields are tightly clustered (proximity), while the *delivery-address section* is separated by a 32-pixel gap, signalling a logical boundary.

> *(Examiner allocates 2 + 2 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 marks**

> **Step 1 — Identify User Tasks (1 mark):**
> The user must:
> 1. Search/select a doctor.
> 2. Choose a date and time slot.
> 3. Confirm personal details.
> 4. Submit the booking.
>
> **Step 2 — Chunking (1.5 marks):**
> Apply **Miller's Law ($7 \pm 2$)**. The 4 tasks are chunked into **3 visual zones**:
> - **Zone A (Top):** Doctor search & selection.
> - **Zone B (Middle):** Date/time slot picker.
> - **Zone C (Bottom):** Confirmation form and submit button.

> **Step 3 — Visual Hierarchy (1.5 marks):**
> - **Primary action:** "Book Appointment" — large, solid green button at bottom.
> - **Secondary action:** "View Profile" — small, text-only link.
> - **Tertiary actions:** Filter chips, back button — low-contrast grey.

> **Step 4 — Layout Pattern (1.5 marks):**
> Choose a **vertical stacked layout** (mobile-optimized) with a **Z-pattern CTA placement** — primary button anchored at the **bottom-right thumb zone**.

> **Step 5 — Heuristic Validation (1.5 marks):**
> - **Gestalt Proximity** applied: 16px between fields in Zone B; 40px between Zone A and Zone B.
> - **Gestalt Figure-Ground** applied: When a time-slot picker is opened, the background dims to 50% black.
> - **Nielsen Heuristic #8 (Aesthetic and minimalist design):** Removed all decorative imagery; only functional icons used.
> - **Fitts's Law compliance:** Book-Appointment button has minimum touch target of **44×44 pixels** (Apple/Google HIG).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Question]**

> **(a)** Define **information density** in screen design. A 1366×768 laptop screen displays **850,000 characters**. Compute the information density and recommend adjustments if the screen violates Tullis's threshold. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, compare the **F-Pattern, Z-Pattern, and Gutenberg Diagram** reading models. State one design implication for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Definition of Information Density (2 marks):**

> **Information Density (D)** is defined as the **ratio of the number of displayable characters (or character-equivalent pixels) to the total available screen area**. It is an empirical measure of how "packed" a screen is with information.

$$
D = \frac{N_c}{A_s}
$$

Tullis (1988) established that users perform optimally when:

$$
0.30 \leq D \leq 0.60
$$

**2. Computation (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute screen area in pixels.

$$
A_s = 1366 \times 768 = 1{,}049{,}088 \text{ pixels}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Compute density.

$$
D = \frac{850{,}000}{1{,}049{,}088} = 0.8102 \approx 81.02\%
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with threshold.

$$
D = 0.8102 \quad \text{vs.} \quad D_{\text{max}} = 0.60
$$

Since $D \gg D_{\text{max}}$, the screen is **significantly over-loaded (cluttered)**.

**3. Recommendations (2 marks):**

- **Apply Gestalt Proximity:** Remove redundant whitespace-consuming borders; collapse 3 columns into 2.
- **Apply Miller's Chunking:** Move 30% of the content to a secondary "Advanced Details" tab.
- **Apply Hick's Law:** Reduce 16 menu items to 6 by hierarchical grouping.
- **Split into multiple screens:** Use a **wizard/stepper pattern** to divide the task across 3 screens, reducing single-screen density to ~27%.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 marks**

> *(Examiner expects: 1 mark for each diagram's definition, 1 mark for diagram/labelling, 0.5 mark for design implication.)*

| Model | Origin / Type | Scan Path | Design Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F-Pattern** | Jakob Nielsen (2006), Eye-tracking study | Horizontal → Horizontal (shorter) → Vertical (left margin) → resembles letter "F" | Place headlines, primary nav, and CTAs along the **top and left** of text-heavy screens. |
| **Z-Pattern** | UX research on landing pages | Top-left → Top-right → Diagonal → Bottom-left → Bottom-right → resembles letter "Z" | Place **logo top-left**, **primary CTA top-right** (or **bottom-right**), minimize diagonal clutter. |
| **Gutenberg Diagram** | Edmund Arnold (Type-setting theory) | Four quadrants: Optical Center (top priority) → Strong Fallow (top-right) → Weak Fallow (bottom-left) → Terminal Area (bottom-right) | Place the **most important message in the optical center** (slightly above geometric center); avoid critical content in the weak fallow (bottom-left). |

**Sample Sketch (textual):**

```
F-PATTERN:                      Z-PATTERN:                  GUTENBERG:
[========= top =========]       [A ---------------> B]      [ Opt. Ctr  | Strong  ]
[ ==== top mid ==== ]          [                    ]      [  Center   | Fallow  ]
[||              ]             [                /]          [-----------+---------]
[||              ]             [              /]            [ Weak      | Terminal]
[|| left    ]                 [C ---------------> D]      [ Fallow    |  Area   ]
```

**Design Implications (1 mark each):**
- **F-Pattern:** Front-load keywords at the start of titles and paragraphs.
- **Z-Pattern:** Use the diagonal sweep for storytelling imagery.
- **Gutenberg:** Don't waste the optical center on logos — use it for value propositions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Vague Principle Naming (–2 marks):** Writing *"use good spacing"* instead of *"apply the **Gestalt Proximity** principle to group related form fields."* Always **name the specific principle** and **justify** it.
>
> 2. **Formula Without Units (–1 mark):** Writing $D = 0.81$ without stating whether it is a ratio or percentage. **Always specify** — e.g., *"D = 0.81 (or 81%) which exceeds the maximum threshold of 0.60."*
>
> 3. **Confusing F-Pattern with Z-Pattern (–2 marks):** The F-Pattern is for **text-heavy** screens (Google search results); the Z-Pattern is for **visual landing pages** (Apple's homepage). Mixing them up signals poor understanding.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Miller's Law (–1 mark):** When justifying menu design, **always cite** $7 \pm 2$ as the reason for hierarchical grouping. KTU examiners explicitly look for this.
>
> 5. **No Diagram in 14-mark Answers (–2 marks):** A 14-mark Part B answer without a labelled diagram (F-pattern, screen layout sketch, or flow chart) is **incomplete**. Always include a diagram — even a hand-drawn ASCII sketch earns partial credit.
>
> 6. **Forgetting WCAG Contrast Standards:** Modern KTU papers (2024 onwards) expect students to mention **WCAG 2.1 contrast ratio of 4.5:1** for accessibility compliance.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Organizing Screen Elements**

- **Definition:** Organizing screen elements = applying visual design + Gestalt psychology to structure on-screen components for **low cognitive load** and **high scannability**.

- **CRAP Principles (Robin Williams):** **C**ontrast, **R**epetition, **A**lignment, **P**roximity — **must be memorized** with examples.

- **8 Gestalt Laws:** Proximity, Similarity, Closure, Continuity, Figure-Ground, Common Region, Symmetry, Common Fate — be able to give **one screen design example per law**.

- **Three Reading Patterns:**
  - **F-Pattern** → text-heavy screens (search results, articles).
  - **Z-Pattern** → visual landing pages (homepages, marketing).
  - **Gutenberg Diagram** → 4-quadrant attention model with **optical center** as primary focus.

- **Key Formulas:**
  - $D = \dfrac{N_c}{A_s}$ with $0.30 \leq D \leq 0.60$ (Tullis).
  - $T = k \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ (Hick's Law).
  - $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ (Fitts's Law).
  - Miller's $7 \pm 2$ chunks.
  - WCAG contrast ratio $\geq 4.5:1$.

- **Four Functional Screen Element Classes:** Informational, Navigational, Functional/Command, Feedback/Status.

- **Five-Step Design Process:** Identify Tasks → Chunking → Visual Hierarchy → Layout Pattern → Heuristic Validation.

- **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
  - **20%** minimum whitespace.
  - **45–75** characters per line.
  - **44×44 px** minimum touch target.
  - **5 ± 2** distinct colors maximum.

- **Common Exam Traps:**
  - Always **name the principle** (don't just describe).
  - Always **include a diagram** in 14-mark answers.
  - Always **state units** in computations.
  - Always **justify** design decisions with HCI theory, not intuition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
