topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ordering of Screen Data and Content - Screen Navigation and Flow - Visually Pleasing Composition - Amount of Information - Focus and Emphasis - Presentation of Information Simply and Meaningfully - Information Retrieval on Web - Statistical Graphics - Technological Consideration in Interface Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ordering of Screen Data and Content

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):** *Ordering of Screen Data and Content* refers to the deliberate, structured arrangement of textual, graphical, and interactive elements on a digital interface so that users can perceive, interpret, and act upon information in a predictable, meaningful sequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea:** Ordering is the spatial and temporal sequence in which information is presented to the user. It directly affects the **information scent**, **cognitive load**, and the **Gulf of Execution** (the gap between user goal and available actions).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Front Page

Imagine walking to a doorstep each morning and picking up a newspaper. The editor (the "interaction designer") has already decided:

1. The **masthead** (brand name) goes at the very top — the identity comes first.
2. The **lead story** (most important news) is placed in the upper right or top-fold (where the eye lands first in Western reading patterns).
3. **Headlines** are larger than body text (hierarchy).
4. **Related stories** are clustered together in a "module" (grouping).
5. **Advertisements** are placed where they do not break the reading flow but still catch attention.

A digital screen is no different. Every **window, tab, panel, card, and form field** is positioned according to a design grammar. The order in which data appears — left-to-right, top-to-bottom, or by relevance — determines how easily a user can build a **mental model** of the system.

### Why Ordering Matters in HCI

- **Reduces search time:** Well-ordered content is found in $O(\log n)$ scanning effort rather than $O(n)$.
- **Supports pattern recognition:** The human visual system uses *Gestalt principles* (proximity, similarity, continuity, closure) to chunk information.
- **Aligns with F-pattern / Z-pattern eye-tracking:** Studies by Nielsen Norman Group confirm that users scan screens in predictable geometric paths.

---

## 1.2 Screen Navigation and Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Screen Navigation* is the set of mechanisms, controls, and pathways (menus, links, breadcrumbs, tabs, gestures) that allow a user to move between screens, states, or sections of an application. *Flow* refers to the seamless, goal-directed trajectory the user experiences as they navigate.

### Conceptual Analogy — Shopping Mall Directory

Consider a multi-floor shopping mall. At every escalator landing, you find:

- A **directory board** (navigation menu).
- **Floor indicators** (breadcrumb trail: "Ground Floor → Electronics → Audio").
- **Clear arrows** directing foot traffic.
- **A consistent visual identity** (the mall's brand color) on every sign.

If a user enters a "dead-end" store with no exit sign, the experience is broken. The same applies to UI: every screen must answer the three navigation questions:

1. **Where am I?** (orientation)
2. **Where can I go?** (available paths)
3. **How do I get back?** (escape route / undo)

### KTU High-Yield Navigation Models

| Navigation Model | Best Use Case | User Mental Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Hierarchical** | File systems, admin panels | Tree / parent-child |
| **Sequential (Linear)** | Checkout wizards, tutorials | Step 1 → Step 2 → Step 3 |
| **Tabular / Matrix** | Spreadsheets, dashboards | Grid of equal cells |
| **Associative / Hypertext** | Web, knowledge bases | Network of links |
| **Radial / Hub-and-Spoke** | Dashboards, mobile apps | One center, many spokes |

---

## 1.3 Visually Pleasing Composition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Composition* in interface design is the artful application of layout principles — **balance, contrast, alignment, proximity, repetition, and white space** — to produce a screen that is both functional and aesthetically satisfying.

### Conceptual Analogy — A Well-Set Dining Table

A chef plates food on a plate. The **main course** sits in the center (focal point), **garnish** sits at 2 o'clock (visual accent), **sauce** is drizzled to one side (asymmetric balance), and the **plate's rim** acts as white space. Nothing competes with the main element. The plate "composes" the meal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetry vs. Asymmetry:** Symmetric compositions feel *formal and stable*; asymmetric compositions feel *dynamic and modern*. Both are valid; what matters is **visual equilibrium**.

---

## 1.4 Amount of Information

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** The *Amount of Information* on a screen is the measured quantity of data (text, icons, controls, graphics) presented to the user at any given moment. It must be balanced against the user's **working memory capacity** (typically **$7 \pm 2$ chunks**, per Miller's Law).

### Conceptual Analogy — Airport Departure Board

An airport departure board lists *only* essentials: flight number, destination, gate, time, status. It does **not** show the captain's name, the catering company, or the fuel type. This is **information minimalism** — every datum answers a real user question.

Overload ("information glut") occurs when designers dump raw data (admin tables, debug logs) onto a user-facing screen.

---

## 1.5 Focus and Emphasis

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Focus* is the element of the screen that the designer wants the user to look at first. *Emphasis* is the set of visual techniques (color, size, weight, isolation, motion) used to draw the eye to that element.

### Conceptual Analogy — Stage Lighting in a Theatre

In a play, the **lead actor** is lit by a **spotlight** while the rest of the stage is in dim light. The audience's gaze is involuntarily drawn to the lit actor. In UI:

- **Bold color, large size, whitespace, and animation** = spotlight.
- **Muted color, small size, dense placement** = background.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Marking **everything** as important makes **nothing** important. Emphasis is a **scarce resource**.

---

## 1.6 Presentation of Information Simply and Meaningfully

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** The *Meaningful Presentation* principle states that information should be encoded in a form (visual, textual, auditory) that matches the user's mental model, leverages pre-attentive processing, and requires the minimum number of cognitive translations.

### Conceptual Analogy — Traffic Signals

A red light does not say "Please stop your vehicle if you wish to comply with the traffic code." It simply shows a **red circle**. The encoding is **direct**, **cross-cultural**, and **pre-attentive** (processed in <200 ms without conscious effort).

---

## 1.7 Information Retrieval on the Web

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Web Information Retrieval (Web IR)* is the process, system, and methodology by which a user locates relevant information from the vast, distributed, hyperlinked corpus of the World Wide Web. It combines **search algorithms**, **ranking models**, and **user-interface design**.

### Conceptual Analogy — Library Catalog vs. Google

In a physical library, you use a **card catalog** (controlled vocabulary, exact match). On the web, you use **Google** (free-text query, ranked results, fuzzy matching). Web IR is "library catalog" scaled to billions of "books" with no librarian.

The **Boolean retrieval model** can be expressed as:

$$R(q) = \{ d \in D \mid f(d, q) = \text{TRUE} \}$$

where $R(q)$ is the result set, $D$ is the document collection, and $f$ is the matching predicate.

---

## 1.8 Statistical Graphics

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Statistical Graphics* are visual encodings (charts, plots, infographics) that map quantitative data to perceptual primitives (position, length, angle, area, color) so that the user can perceive trends, outliers, and distributions at a glance.

### Conceptual Analogy — Weather Forecast Map

A weather map uses **isobars** (lines), **color gradients** (temperature), and **icons** (sun, cloud) to compress terabytes of meteorological data into a single image a child can read. This is the power of statistical graphics: **high data density, low cognitive cost**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cleveland & McGill's Hierarchy of Perceptual Tasks** ranks position and length as the most accurate encodings, while area and color saturation are the least accurate. Always encode the most important data on the most accurate channel.

---

## 1.9 Technological Consideration in Interface Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Technological Considerations* are the hardware, software, network, and platform constraints — **input devices, output devices, bandwidth, screen size, OS conventions, accessibility APIs** — that bound the design space of an interface.

### Conceptual Analogy — Building a House

A civil engineer cannot design a 30-floor skyscraper on a swampy plot. The **soil condition** is the technological constraint; the **building code** is the platform convention. The architect must work *with* these constraints, not against them. Similarly, an HCI designer must respect:

- **Device pixel density** (DPI / PPI).
- **Latency budget** (response time).
- **Memory and CPU** available to the UI thread.
- **Cross-platform UI libraries** (Material Design, Human Interface Guidelines).

---

## 1.10 Section 1 Quick Recap Table

| Subtopic | One-Line Essence |
|---|---|
| **Ordering** | Decide what the user sees first, second, third. |
| **Navigation & Flow** | Provide clear paths, signs, and escape routes. |
| **Composition** | Apply visual design principles for balance and clarity. |
| **Amount of Information** | Respect working memory limits ($7 \pm 2$). |
| **Focus & Emphasis** | Use scarce visual weight to direct attention. |
| **Simple Presentation** | Match encoding to mental model; pre-attentive is best. |
| **Web IR** | Help users find needles in the global haystack. |
| **Statistical Graphics** | Encode data on the most accurate perceptual channels. |
| **Technological Constraints** | Design *with* the platform, not against it. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Information Architecture Foundation

Information Architecture (IA) is the **structural skeleton** of an interface. It is built on three pillars:

1. **Organization schemes** — how content is grouped (by topic, by task, by audience, by chronology, by geography).
2. **Labeling systems** — how content is named (controlled vocabularies, folksonomies).
3. **Navigation systems** — how users move (menus, indexes, guides, search).
4. **Search systems** — how users query (full-text, faceted, parametric).

### The Information Foraging Theory (Pirolli & Card)

Users behave like predators hunting for "information prey." They choose paths that **maximize the rate of valuable information gained per unit cost**:

$$R = \frac{G}{T + C}$$

where:
- $R$ = Rate of information gain
- $G$ = Information gained (patch value)
- $T$ = Time spent on the current patch
- $C$ = Cost of moving between patches

**Design Implication:** Place high-value information where it can be reached with minimum cost (low $C$). This is why search boxes and primary navigation appear at the **top** of nearly every web page.

---

## 2.2 Principles of Effective Screen Ordering

### 2.2.1 Reading Patterns (F-Pattern & Z-Pattern)

**Nielsen Norman Group (2006) eye-tracking study** established that on text-heavy pages:

- Users scan in an **F-shape**: horizontally at the top, then a shorter horizontal sweep lower down, then a vertical scan down the left margin.
- On visually-rich pages (landing pages), users scan in a **Z-shape**: top-left → top-right → bottom-left → bottom-right.

**Design Rule:** Place the **most important CTA (Call to Action)** along the F or Z path.

### 2.2.2 The Inverted Pyramid (Journalism → UI)

Borrowed from journalism: present the **conclusion first**, then the supporting evidence, then the background details. In UI:

```
[ Headline / Primary Result ]        ← User's goal
[ Supporting Detail / Filters ]      ← Context
[ Background / History / Logs ]      ← Deep dive
```

---

## 2.3 Navigation Theory in Detail

### 2.3.1 Three Questions of Wayfinding (Arthur Passini, 1984)

Every navigation system must answer:

1. **Where am I?** — Provided by *location indicators* (URL, breadcrumb, active tab).
2. **Where am I going?** — Provided by *destination cues* (links, buttons).
3. **How do I get there?** — Provided by *route information* (menus, maps, directions).

### 2.3.2 Law of Proximity (Gestalt)

Elements close to each other are perceived as a group. The minimum effective gap is approximately **$1.5 \times$ the element's own size** for visual separation without explicit dividers.

### 2.3.3 Hick's Law (Reaction Time)

The time to make a choice increases **logarithmically** with the number of choices:

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$$

where $n$ is the number of options. **Design Rule:** Limit visible choices to **5–7 primary items**; use progressive disclosure for the rest.

### 2.3.4 Fitts's Law (Movement Time)

The time to acquire a target depends on its distance and size:

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{D}{W}\right)$$

where $D$ is distance to target and $W$ is target width. **Design Rule:** Make primary targets **large** and place them **close** to the user's current pointer position.

---

## 2.4 Composition Principles — The C.R.A.P. Framework

| Letter | Principle | Definition | UI Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **C** | **Contrast** | Different elements must look different. | Primary vs secondary buttons, headings vs body text. |
| **R** | **Repetition** | Reuse visual styles for unity. | Same icon style across the app. |
| **A** | **Alignment** | Every element has a visual connection. | Grid-based layouts, baseline alignment of text. |
| **P** | **Proximity** | Related items are grouped. | Form labels sit close to their fields. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** These are the **four pillars of visual design** (often taught in graphic-design curricula). KTU examiners frequently test this acronym.

---

## 2.5 The Amount-of-Information Trade-off

### Miller's Law (1956)

The human working memory can hold approximately:

$$C = 7 \pm 2 \text{ chunks}$$

**Design Strategies for Overload:**

1. **Chunking:** Group digits as `425-09-8832` instead of `425098832`.
2. **Progressive Disclosure:** Show summary first; reveal details on demand.
3. **Defaults:** Pre-fill known values.
4. **Scaffolding:** Break complex tasks into sequential steps.

---

## 2.6 Focus and Emphasis Techniques

| Technique | Visual Mechanism | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Size** | Larger = more important | Headlines, primary buttons |
| **Color** | Saturated = more important | CTA buttons, error messages |
| **Whitespace** | Isolation = more important | Single CTA on a clean background |
| **Typography Weight** | Bold = more important | Key terms, totals |
| **Motion** | Animation = more important | New notifications, alerts |
| **Position** | Top-left = more important (Western) | Logo, primary nav |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre-attentive Processing:** The human visual system detects color, orientation, size, and motion in **< 200 ms**, *before* conscious attention. Leverage these channels for emphasis.

---

## 2.7 Meaningful Presentation — Tufte's Principles

Edward Tufte's **Data-Ink Ratio** states that the proportion of ink devoted to data versus decoration should be maximized:

$$\text{Data-Ink Ratio} = \frac{\text{Ink used to display data}}{\text{Total ink on the chart}}$$

**Design Rule:** Remove **chartjunk** (unnecessary 3D, gridlines, shadows) to maximize clarity.

---

## 2.8 Web Information Retrieval Models

### 2.8.1 Boolean Model

Documents are represented as sets of terms. Queries use **AND, OR, NOT**. Simple but no ranking.

### 2.8.2 Vector Space Model (Salton, 1975)

Documents and queries are represented as vectors in an $n$-dimensional term space. Relevance is measured by **cosine similarity**:

$$\text{sim}(q, d) = \cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d} \vert}$$

### 2.8.3 TF-IDF Weighting

A term's importance in a document is computed as:

$$w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$$

where:
- $\text{tf}(t,d)$ = frequency of term $t$ in document $d$
- $N$ = total number of documents
- $\text{df}(t)$ = number of documents containing term $t$

### 2.8.4 PageRank (Brin & Page, 1998)

A link-based ranking model where a page's score is recursively:

$$PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \in B(p)} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$$

where $B(p)$ is the set of pages linking to $p$, $L(q)$ is the out-degree of $q$, and $d$ is the **damping factor** (typically $0.85$).

---

## 2.9 Statistical Graphics — Cleveland-McGill Hierarchy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most Accurate → Least Accurate:**
> 1. Position along a common scale
> 2. Position along non-aligned scales
> 3. Length, direction, angle
> 4. Area
> 5. Volume, curvature
> 6. Shading, color saturation
> 7. Color hue

**Design Rule:** Encode the **most critical variable** on **position**; use color only for the least critical variable.

---

## 2.10 Technological Considerations — Concrete Constraints

| Constraint | Typical Value | Design Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Pixel Density (DPI)** | 96 (web), 326+ (retina) | Affects icon size, line weight. |
| **Frame Rate** | 60 Hz (16.67 ms/frame) | Animations must complete in 1 frame for "instant" feel. |
| **Latency Budget** | < 100 ms for "instant" | Local UI feedback; async for network. |
| **Accessibility** | WCAG 2.1 AA: contrast ratio $\geq 4.5{:}1$ for body text | Color palette selection. |
| **Bandwidth** | 3G = 400 kbps, 4G = 10 Mbps, 5G = 100+ Mbps | Image compression, lazy loading. |
| **Battery** | Mobile ~ 3000 mAh | Dark mode, reduced animation. |

> [!NOTE]
> **WCAG Contrast Ratio Formula:**
> $$\text{CR} = \frac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05}$$
> where $L_1$ is the relative luminance of the lighter color and $L_2$ of the darker.

---

## 2.11 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (One-Page Summary)

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Design Application |
|---|---|---|
| Miller's Law | $C = 7 \pm 2$ | Chunk information |
| Hick's Law | $T = a + b \log_2(n+1)$ | Limit visible choices |
| Fitts's Law | $T = a + b \log_2(1 + D/W)$ | Big, close targets |
| Information Foraging | $R = G / (T+C)$ | Place high-value info on low-cost paths |
| Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim} = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \vert \vec{d} \vert}$ | Rank search results |
| TF-IDF | $w = \text{tf} \cdot \log(N/\text{df})$ | Weight index terms |
| PageRank | $PR(p) = (1-d)/N + d \sum PR(q)/L(q)$ | Rank web pages |
| Data-Ink Ratio | Maximize | Remove chartjunk |
| C.R.A.P. | Contrast, Repetition, Alignment, Proximity | Visual composition |
| WCAG Contrast | $(L_1 + 0.05) / (L_2 + 0.05) \geq 4.5$ | Accessibility |
| Cleveland-McGill | Position > Length > Area > Color | Chart design |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fitts's Law in the Context of Button Design

Fitts's Law quantifies the time required to move a pointer (mouse, finger) to a target (button, link, icon). Let us derive the design implications step by step.

### Given

- Distance from pointer to target center: $D$
- Width of the target: $W$
- Empirical constants: $a$ (start/stop time), $b$ (slope of the learning factor)

### Step 1 — Write the Base Equation

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{D}{W}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Index of Difficulty (ID)

$$ID = \log_2\left(1 + \frac{D}{W}\right) \text{ (measured in bits)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "bits"?** Fitts modeled human motor control as an **information channel** transmitting "I am at target $X$" vs "I am not." The amount of information transmitted is $\log_2$ of the number of equiprobable target positions, which is $\log_2(2D/W) \approx \log_2(1 + D/W)$.

### Step 3 — Solve for a Design Scenario

**Scenario:** A "Buy Now" button is at 200 px from the cursor and is 80 px wide. Compute the ID.

$$ID = \log_2\left(1 + \frac{200}{80}\right)$$

$$ID = \log_2(1 + 2.5)$$

$$ID = \log_2(3.5)$$

$$ID \approx 1.807 \text{ bits}$$

**Interpretation:** The user must resolve $\approx 1.8$ bits of spatial uncertainty to acquire the target. This is *easy* — most adults can resolve 4–6 bits comfortably.

### Step 4 — Design Action

If we **double** the button width to 160 px:

$$ID_{\text{new}} = \log_2\left(1 + \frac{200}{160}\right) = \log_2(2.25) \approx 1.170 \text{ bits}$$

The **ID drops by 35%**, so movement time drops significantly. This justifies the design heuristic: **make primary CTAs as large as possible**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Cosine Similarity Ranking Step-by-Step

Suppose a user searches for the query "**green energy**" in a 3-document corpus. After TF-IDF weighting, we have the following vectors in a 2-D term space (`green`, `energy`):

- Query: $\vec{q} = (0.8,\ 0.6)$
- Doc 1 (about solar panels): $\vec{d_1} = (0.4,\ 0.9)$
- Doc 2 (about green salad): $\vec{d_2} = (0.9,\ 0.1)$
- Doc 3 (about nuclear energy): $\vec{d_3} = (0.1,\ 0.8)$

### Step 1 — Compute the Norm of the Query Vector

$$\vert \vec{q} \vert = \sqrt{0.8^2 + 0.6^2} = \sqrt{0.64 + 0.36} = \sqrt{1.00} = 1.000$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Norm of Each Document

$$\vert \vec{d_1} \vert = \sqrt{0.4^2 + 0.9^2} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.81} = \sqrt{0.97} \approx 0.985$$

$$\vert \vec{d_2} \vert = \sqrt{0.9^2 + 0.1^2} = \sqrt{0.81 + 0.01} = \sqrt{0.82} \approx 0.906$$

$$\vert \vec{d_3} \vert = \sqrt{0.1^2 + 0.8^2} = \sqrt{0.01 + 0.64} = \sqrt{0.65} \approx 0.806$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Dot Product with Each Document

$$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_1} = (0.8)(0.4) + (0.6)(0.9) = 0.32 + 0.54 = 0.86$$

$$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_2} = (0.8)(0.9) + (0.6)(0.1) = 0.72 + 0.06 = 0.78$$

$$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_3} = (0.8)(0.1) + (0.6)(0.8) = 0.08 + 0.48 = 0.56$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Cosine Similarity

$$\text{sim}(q, d_1) = \frac{0.86}{1.000 \times 0.985} \approx 0.873$$

$$\text{sim}(q, d_2) = \frac{0.78}{1.000 \times 0.906} \approx 0.861$$

$$\text{sim}(q, d_3) = \frac{0.56}{1.000 \times 0.806} \approx 0.695$$

### Step 5 — Rank the Results

| Rank | Document | Similarity | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Doc 1 (solar) | 0.873 | Best balance of "green" + "energy" |
| 2 | Doc 2 (salad) | 0.861 | Strong "green", weak "energy" |
| 3 | Doc 3 (nuclear) | 0.695 | Strong "energy", weak "green" |

**Design Insight:** The IR interface should present Doc 1 first, with a snippet highlighting *both* terms — this is called **query-biased summarization**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of WCAG Contrast Ratio for Accessible Color Pairs

**Scenario:** A designer wants white text on a blue button. Is the contrast sufficient?

### Step 1 — Compute Relative Luminance of White

For white, $R = G = B = 1.0$ (in sRGB linear terms).

$$L_{\text{white}} = 0.2126 \cdot 1.0 + 0.7152 \cdot 1.0 + 0.0722 \cdot 1.0 = 1.000$$

### Step 2 — Compute Relative Luminance of a Blue with sRGB $(0, 0, 0.5)$

First, convert to linear values. For sRGB channel $C_s$:

$$C_{\text{lin}} = \begin{cases} C_s / 12.92 & \text{if } C_s \le 0.03928 \\ ((C_s + 0.055)/1.055)^{2.4} & \text{if } C_s > 0.03928 \end{cases}$$

For $B_s = 0.5$:

$$B_{\text{lin}} = ((0.5 + 0.055)/1.055)^{2.4} = (0.5261)^{2.4} \approx 0.2140$$

So $L_{\text{blue}} = 0.2126(0) + 0.7152(0) + 0.0722(0.2140) \approx 0.01546$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Contrast Ratio

$$\text{CR} = \frac{L_{\text{white}} + 0.05}{L_{\text{blue}} + 0.05} = \frac{1.000 + 0.05}{0.01546 + 0.05} = \frac{1.05}{0.06546} \approx 16.04$$

### Step 4 — Compare to WCAG 2.1 Thresholds

| WCAG Level | Body Text | Large Text |
|---|---|---|
| AA | $\geq 4.5$ | $\geq 3.0$ |
| AAA | $\geq 7.0$ | $\geq 4.5$ |

A ratio of **16.04** exceeds even **AAA**, so the white-on-blue combination is **highly accessible**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Visual Encoding

For a data set of *sales figures per region per quarter*, which chart type should you choose?

| Goal | Best Chart | Why (Cleveland-McGill) | Pitfall to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compare values across categories | Bar chart (vertical) | Position along common scale is the most accurate | 3D bars distort perception |
| Show trend over time | Line chart | Position + slope are pre-attentive | Too many series clutter the line |
| Show part-to-whole | Stacked bar or treemap | Position within a common baseline | Pie charts > 5 slices are unreadable |
| Show correlation between two variables | Scatter plot | Position on $x$ and $y$ axes | Over-plotting without transparency |
| Show distribution | Histogram or box plot | Position + length convey quartile spread | Mis-binning the histogram |
| Show geographic data | Choropleth (color-filled map) | Position + color saturation | Color hue is least accurate — use a single hue gradient |
| Show many variables at once | Parallel coordinates or radar | Each axis is a position | Radar charts distort area perception |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever the question asks "which chart for which data," your answer must reference **Cleveland-McGill's hierarchy** to earn full marks.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Information Foraging Calculation

**Scenario:** A user is on a documentation portal. The home page has 5 top-level links; each link leads to a section with 10 sub-pages. The user wants the answer to a specific question.

### Step 1 — Estimate Patch Value

Each link promises $G = 20$ units of useful info (in expectation).

### Step 2 — Estimate Costs

- Time to scan home page: $T = 5$ s.
- Time to enter and scan one sub-page: $T = 15$ s.
- Cost of moving (click + load): $C = 2$ s per click.

### Step 3 — Compute the Foraging Rate if User Picks the First Link

$$R = \frac{G}{T + C} = \frac{20}{15 + 2} \approx 1.176 \text{ units/s}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Foraging Rate if User Goes Back and Tries the Second Link

$$R_{\text{multi}} = \frac{G}{(15 + 2 + 5 + 2)} = \frac{20}{24} \approx 0.833 \text{ units/s}$$

The **rate drops** as the user explores. This is the **patch-departure decision**: when $R$ falls below the expected rate of the next patch, the user should leave.

### Step 5 — Design Implication

Provide a **search box** so the user can bypass navigation. The search box offers a near-infinite-rate patch: $R_{\text{search}} \to \infty$ if the answer is in the top 3 results.

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Application of the C.R.A.P. Principles to a Login Form

Let us redesign a "cluttered" login form step by step.

**Before:** Username, Password, "Remember me" checkbox, "Forgot password" link, "Sign up" link, social-login buttons, captcha, terms checkbox — all in a single column with the same font size and color.

### Step 1 — Apply CONTRAST

- **Primary action** ("Sign In") → large, bold, brand color.
- **Secondary actions** ("Forgot password", "Sign up") → small, muted text.
- **Error messages** → red; **success** → green.

### Step 2 — Apply REPETITION

- All buttons use the same border-radius (e.g., 8 px).
- All labels use the same font weight (e.g., medium).
- All icons use the same stroke width.

### Step 3 — Apply ALIGNMENT

- Form fields and labels share a left edge (grid column).
- Buttons are right-aligned to a consistent column.
- Group related items (e.g., social login) in a sub-grid.

### Step 4 — Apply PROXIMITY

- Label sits **8 px** above its field.
- "Remember me" sits **16 px** below the password field.
- Helper text sits **4 px** below the field it describes.

### Result

The form is now **scannable in under 2 seconds** and passes a basic usability test with 90%+ first-attempt success.

## 4.1 Screen Navigation Flow — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Goal: Buy Product] --> B[Home Page]
    B --> C[Search Box]
    B --> D[Category Menu]
    C --> E[Search Results Page]
    D --> F[Category Listing Page]
    E --> G[Product Detail Page]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Add to Cart]
    H --> I[Cart Page]
    I --> J[Checkout Page]
    J --> K[Payment Page]
    K --> L[Confirmation Page]
    B -.breadcrumb.-> M[Breadcrumb: Home]

    G -.breadcrumb.-> O[Breadcrumb: Home / Electronics / Phones / iPhone]
    I -.undo.-> P[Undo: Remove item]
    J -.back.-> Q[Back: Edit cart]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = primary forward navigation. Dotted arrows = secondary / escape / orientation paths. This is the **hub-and-spoke** pattern with breadcrumbs as the orientation layer.

## 4.2 The Information Architecture Pyramid (Inverted)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Need] --> B[Goal Statement]
    B --> C[Primary Task]
    C --> D[Concrete Action]
    D --> E[Confirmation / Feedback]
    style A fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000

```

**Reading:** The pyramid descends from the **abstract user need** (top, broadest) to the **concrete action confirmation** (bottom, narrowest). Designers must align each UI screen with the corresponding layer.

## 4.3 The C.R.A.P. Composition Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Contrast] --> X[Visual Hierarchy]
    R[Repetition] --> Y[Unity / Cohesion]
    A[Alignment] --> Z[Order / Grid]
    P[Proximity] --> W[Grouping / Relation]
    X --> OUT[Pleasing Composition]
    Y --> OUT
    Z --> OUT
    W --> OUT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All four principles converge on a single outcome: a **visually pleasing, scannable, unified** interface. Omit any one, and the design feels *off* to the user, even if they cannot articulate why.

---

## 4.4 Information Retrieval Pipeline (Web Search)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Query] --> B[Query Parser]
    B --> C[Tokenizer and Stemmer]
    C --> D[Inverted Index Lookup]
    D --> E[Candidate Set of Documents]
    E --> F[TF-IDF Weighting]
    F --> G[Cosine Similarity Scoring]
    G --> H[PageRank Boosting]
    H --> I[Ranked Result List]
    I --> J[Snippet Generator]
    J --> K[Search Results Page UI]
    K --> L[User Clicks Result]
    L --> M[Click Log for Re-ranking]
    M --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This is a modern web search pipeline. Notice the **feedback loop** — click logs are fed back into the ranking model for continuous improvement. This is the basis of **Learning to Rank (LTR)** in modern IR systems.

---

```mermaid

    E -- Yes --> F[Apply Progressive Disclosure]

    G --> H[Final Scannable Interface]
    C --> H

    style H fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000
```

## 4.6 Focus and Emphasis — Visual Weight Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> C[Secondary Action 25 percent visual weight]
    A --> D[Tertiary Info 10 percent visual weight]
    A --> E[Background 5 percent visual weight]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **60-25-10-5 rule** (also called the *Pareto principle of visual weight*). Aim to allocate visual weight in this proportion for a balanced, prioritized screen.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define "Information Foraging Theory" and explain how it influences the placement of a search box on a web page. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Information Foraging Theory**, proposed by Pirolli and Card (1999), models user behavior on the web as analogous to animal foraging for food. Users act to maximize the **rate of valuable information gained per unit cost**.

The foraging rate is given by:

$$R = \frac{G}{T + C}$$

where $G$ is the information gained, $T$ is the time spent, and $C$ is the cost of moving between information patches (pages).

**Influence on search-box placement:** A search box reduces $C$ (the cost of finding information across many pages) to nearly zero. By placing the search box at the **top of every page** — within the user's F-pattern scan path — designers ensure that $R$ is maximized from the very first interaction. The search box becomes a *high-yield patch* with low entry cost, exactly as Foraging Theory predicts.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the C.R.A.P. principle of visual composition? List its four components with one example each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **C.R.A.P.** framework (Contrast, Repetition, Alignment, Proximity) is a foundational set of composition principles in visual design.

| Principle | Meaning | UI Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Contrast** | Different elements must look visually different. | Primary button in bright orange, secondary in grey. |
| **Repetition** | Reuse visual elements to create unity. | Same border-radius (8 px) on all buttons. |
| **Alignment** | Every element has a visual relationship with another. | Form labels and fields share a left-edge grid column. |
| **Proximity** | Related items are placed close together. | Label sits 8 px above its input field. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]` — 14 Marks

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of *Screen Navigation and Flow* in HCI. Discuss the three core questions of wayfinding with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe *Fitts's Law* and *Hick's Law*. For a screen where the user must choose between 8 menu items versus 4 menu items, compute the index of difficulty for each and state which design reduces cognitive load. Assume $a = 200$ ms, $b = 150$ ms. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (a) — 7 Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept of Screen Navigation and Flow (3 marks):**
> Screen navigation refers to the set of controls (menus, links, tabs, gestures) and pathways that allow a user to move through an application. Flow refers to the seamless, goal-directed trajectory the user experiences. Effective navigation reduces the **Gulf of Execution** (gap between user goal and available actions) and the **Gulf of Evaluation** (gap between system state and user perception).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Questions of Wayfinding (Arthur Passini, 1984) — 4 marks:**
> 1. **Where am I?** — *Location indicator.* Example: a breadcrumb trail "Home → Products → Laptops → MacBook Pro" tells the user their current position.
> 2. **Where am I going?** — *Destination cue.* Example: a clearly labeled "Add to Cart" button tells the user what will happen if they click.
> 3. **How do I get there?** — *Route information.* Example: a navigation menu with categories and sub-categories shows the path.

**Synthesis Example (1 mark):** In an e-commerce site, the breadcrumb answers "where," the "Checkout" button answers "where to," and the top navigation menu answers "how."

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (b) — 7 Marks

**Fitts's Law (2 marks):** The time to acquire a target depends on its distance and size.

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{D}{W}\right)$$

**Hick's Law (2 marks):** The time to make a choice increases logarithmically with the number of choices.

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$$

**Computation (3 marks):**

For **8 menu items** ($n = 8$):

$$T_8 = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(8+1) = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(9) = 200 + 150 \cdot 3.170 \approx 200 + 475.5 = 675.5 \text{ ms}$$

For **4 menu items** ($n = 4$):

$$T_4 = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(4+1) = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(5) = 200 + 150 \cdot 2.322 \approx 200 + 348.3 = 548.3 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** The design with **4 menu items** reduces decision time by approximately **127 ms (≈19%)**, which is cognitively significant. Therefore, **use progressive disclosure**: show 4 top-level items, expand the rest on hover/click.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q: (a)** Explain the **principles of ordering screen data and content**. Discuss F-pattern, Z-pattern, and the inverted-pyramid model with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **statistical graphics** in interface design. With reference to the **Cleveland-McGill hierarchy of perceptual tasks**, recommend the most appropriate chart for: (i) sales trend over 12 months, (ii) population density across 50 districts, (iii) market share of 6 companies. Justify each choice. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (a) — 7 Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **Principles of Ordering Screen Data (2 marks):**
> 1. **Hierarchy** — Rank elements by importance; place high-importance items at the top-left.
> 2. **Grouping (Gestalt)** — Cluster related items using proximity and similarity.
> 3. **Alignment** — Snap elements to a grid for a coherent flow.
> 4. **Density Balance** — Distribute content evenly to avoid visual heaviness on one side.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **F-Pattern (2 marks):** Eye-tracking research (Nielsen Norman Group, 2006) shows users on text-heavy pages scan in an **F-shape**: a long horizontal sweep at the top, a shorter horizontal sweep in the middle, and a vertical scan down the left margin. Place the most important content (headline, primary CTA) along the top horizontal bar and key navigation along the left vertical bar.

**Z-Pattern (1 mark):** On visually-rich landing pages, users scan in a **Z-shape**: top-left → top-right → bottom-left → bottom-right. Place the logo top-left, primary CTA top-right or bottom-right, and the value proposition along the diagonal.

**Inverted Pyramid (2 marks):** Borrowed from journalism — present the **conclusion first**, then the supporting evidence, then the background. Example: a news app shows the headline and key fact at the top, then a one-paragraph summary, then the full article. The user gets the answer before deciding to read further.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (b) — 7 Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **Statistical Graphics (1 mark):** Visual encodings that map quantitative data to perceptual primitives (position, length, angle, area, color) for rapid comprehension.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cleveland-McGill Hierarchy — Most Accurate to Least Accurate (1 mark):**
> Position along common scale > Position along non-aligned scales > Length / Direction / Angle > Area > Volume / Curvature > Shading / Color saturation > Color hue.

**Recommendations (5 marks):**

| Use Case | Recommended Chart | Channel Used | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Sales trend over 12 months** | **Line chart** | Position on $x$ (time) and $y$ (sales) | Time-series data is best read as a continuous position trajectory. The slope (rate of change) is pre-attentive. |
| **(ii) Population density across 50 districts** | **Choropleth map** (color-filled regions) | Position (geographic) + Color saturation | Position is the most accurate channel — preserves geographic truth. Color saturation is used only for the *density* variable, not critical categorical data. Use a **single-hue gradient** to avoid misleading hue interpretations. |
| **(iii) Market share of 6 companies** | **Horizontal bar chart** (sorted descending) | Position + Length | Bar charts use the two most accurate channels. Avoid pie charts because **angle/area** is less accurate and 6 slices approach the perceptual limit. Sort bars descending so the eye scans naturally from largest to smallest. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake:**
> Many students answer "(iii) pie chart" because "pie charts show parts of a whole." This is **wrong** for 6 categories. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks unless you justify the choice using **Cleveland-McGill**. Always justify chart choice with the hierarchy, not the textbook cliché.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Points in This Topic (across all KTU papers):**
> 1. **Memorizing without applying:** Writing down "Miller's Law $7 \pm 2$" without explaining *how* it influences the design of a specific screen earns 0 marks. Always **apply** the principle to a concrete UI element.
> 2. **Confusing C.R.A.P. with C.R.A.P.D.:** Some textbooks add **D for "Direction"** or **D for "Dominance."** KTU uses the **4-letter version** (Contrast, Repetition, Alignment, Proximity). Stick to 4.
> 3. **Confusing Fitts's Law and Hick's Law:** Fitts is about **movement** to a target; Hick is about **decision** between options. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the human in HCI:** A purely technical answer about "what is PageRank" without connecting it to "how it improves user experience" loses application marks. Always bridge to the user.
> 5. **Ignoring accessibility:** Any design discussion that omits WCAG contrast ratios, keyboard navigation, or screen-reader compatibility will lose at least 1 mark in the technology-constraints sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — KTU Module 2 — Screen Design**

- [x] **Ordering of Screen Data:** Hierarchy first, then grouping, then alignment. Apply the **inverted pyramid**.
- [x] **Screen Navigation:** Answer the **3 wayfinding questions** — *where am I, where to, how to get there*. Provide a **breadcrumb**, **menu**, and **escape route** on every screen.
- [x] **Visually Pleasing Composition:** Use **C.R.A.P.** — Contrast, Repetition, Alignment, Proximity. Apply the **60-25-10-5 visual-weight rule**.
- [x] **Amount of Information:** Respect **Miller's Law ($7 \pm 2$)**. Use **chunking, progressive disclosure, defaults, and scaffolding**.
- [x] **Focus & Emphasis:** Use **size, color, whitespace, weight, motion, position**. Pre-attentive cues (< 200 ms) are most powerful.
- [x] **Simple & Meaningful Presentation:** Maximize **Tufte's Data-Ink Ratio**; remove chartjunk; match encoding to mental model.
- [x] **Web Information Retrieval:** Know the **Boolean, Vector Space, TF-IDF, and PageRank** models. Cosine similarity ranks results.
- [x] **Statistical Graphics:** Apply the **Cleveland-McGill hierarchy** — position is king; color hue is last resort.
- [x] **Technological Considerations:** Respect **DPI, latency (100 ms rule), bandwidth, accessibility (WCAG 4.5:1), battery, and platform conventions**.
- [x] **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $T_{\text{Fitts}} = a + b \log_2(1 + D/W)$
  - $T_{\text{Hick}} = a + b \log_2(n+1)$
  - $R = G / (T + C)$ (Foraging)
  - $\text{sim} = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d} \vert}$ (Cosine)
  - $w = \text{tf} \cdot \log(N/\text{df})$ (TF-IDF)
  - $\text{CR} = (L_1 + 0.05) / (L_2 + 0.05) \geq 4.5$ (WCAG)
- [x] **Key Acronyms:** C.R.A.P., IA (Information Architecture), WCAG, F-pattern, Z-pattern, IR (Information Retrieval), LTR (Learning to Rank), Scent (Information Scent).
- [x] **Always End with a User-Centered Sentence:** "This design choice reduces cognitive load by $X\%$, helping the user achieve goal $Y$ in fewer steps."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation Schemes - Selection of Window
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Navigation Schemes - Selection of Window

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Navigation Schemes** in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) refer to the structured set of techniques, metaphors, and architectural patterns that enable a user to move through, locate, and traverse the information space or functional components of an interactive software system. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PECPT414 - Fundamentals of HCI), navigation schemes govern *how a user decides where to go next* and *how the system represents that movement*.

**Selection of a Window** is the specific sub-process within a Window-Manager-orchestrated Graphical User Interface (GUI) by which the operating system (or window manager) designates one particular window as the **active window** (also called the *focused window* or *foreground window*) such that subsequent keyboard, mouse, or pointer events are routed to that window's client area.

In standard ISO 9241 terminology adopted by KTU's HCI curriculum:
- **Navigation** = the cognitive and motor act of moving *between* destinations.
- **Selection** = the act of *choosing one* destination (object, window, widget) among many.
- **Window Selection** = the binding of input focus to a single rectangular screen region.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Post Office Counter**
>
> Imagine you walk into a large post office with **five service counters**, each enclosed in a glass booth (Booth 1: Parcels, Booth 2: Savings, Booth 3: Speed Post, etc.). When you arrive, **only one booth has its sliding glass window open and lit** — the rest are dimmed and closed. That *open, lit booth* is the **active/selected window**. All your queries, forms, and stamps are directed to the clerk inside *that specific booth*.
>
> Now suppose you want to pay a bill at Booth 3. You must **navigate** through the lobby (the navigation scheme — perhaps a queue-ropes metaphor, or numbered tokens) and then **select** Booth 3 by tapping on the glass, taking a token, or asking the supervisor. Once selected, Booth 3's window opens, the light turns on, and *your* interaction is now bound exclusively to that counter.
>
> Just like in a GUI, there are *rules of engagement*: only one counter can be active at a time, you can switch counters (Alt-Tab equivalents — looking at the lobby display board), and the system must remember your place in each queue (window state restoration).

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

This topic directly satisfies **Course Outcomes (CO)** for Module 3 of PECPT414:
- **CO3**: *Design effective navigation, menus, and window-based interaction schemes for user interfaces.*
- It maps to **Bloom's Taxonomy levels L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), and L4 (Analyze)** under NEP 2020's outcome-based education framework.

The standard metrics used to evaluate selection efficiency are:
- **Selection Time (T)** measured in **milliseconds (ms)**
- **Error Rate (E)** measured in **percentage (%)**
- **Index of Difficulty (ID)** measured in **bits**
- **Throughput (TP)** measured in **bits/second (bps)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Navigation Schemes

Navigation schemes are broadly classified into **five canonical categories** as defined in Shneiderman's "Designing the User Interface" (the KTU reference text):

| # | Navigation Scheme | Mechanism | Best Use Case |
|---|-------------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Linear / Sequential** | One-after-another, fixed order | Wizards, Installers, Checkout flows |
| 2 | **Hierarchical / Tree** | Parent → Child expansion | File systems, Org charts, Menus |
| 3 | **Direct (Spatial / Map-based)** | Pan/Zoom on 2D or 3D spatial map | Maps (Google Maps), CAD, Games |
| 4 | **Hypertext / Associative** | Click embedded links to jump non-linearly | Web browsers, Help systems, Wikis |
| 5 | **Tabular / Search-Driven** | Query → Filtered list → Select | Databases, E-commerce catalogs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The five schemes above are *the* expected answer for any "Classify navigation schemes" or "List types of navigation" question. Memorize them verbatim with examples.

### 2.2 Hierarchical Navigation — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

Hierarchical navigation is the most common scheme in windowed GUIs. Here is the operational logic in a structured bulleted form:

1. **Root Node Display**: The system presents the top-level container (e.g., the Desktop, the Start Menu, the Home Screen).
2. **User Initiates Traversal**: The user issues a *navigate* command — a click, a tap, a voice command, or a hotkey.
3. **Child Node Expansion**: The selected parent node reveals its children (e.g., clicking "Programs" reveals a list of installed applications).
4. **State Preservation**: The system retains the *breadcrumb trail* and the *back-stack* so the user can reverse the navigation.
5. **Leaf Node Selection**: When the user reaches a *leaf* (a node with no children), the system performs the **selection of window** action, opening or focusing the leaf's associated window.
6. **Focus Handoff**: Input focus is transferred from the previous window to the newly selected window.

### 2.3 Window Selection — The Four Canonical Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Trigger | Typical OS | KTU Weightage |
|-----------|---------|-----------|----------------|
| **Click-to-Select (Point-and-Click)** | Mouse press on window's title bar or client area | Windows, macOS, X11 | Very High |
| **Alt-Tab Cyclic Switcher** | Hold Alt + press Tab repeatedly | Windows | High |
| **Taskbar / Dock Click** | Click on a pinned or running app's icon | Windows, macOS | High |
| **Expose / Mission Control / Overview** | Hot-corner or hotkey shows all windows tiled | macOS, GNOME | Medium |
| **Hover-to-Focus (Focus-Follows-Mouse)** | Mouse pointer enters window region | X11, Linux DEs | Medium |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following mathematical models are **high-yield** for KTU board examinations on selection-based interaction.

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit | Formula / Law |
|--------|---------|------|---------------|
| $T$ | Mean selection time | seconds (s) or ms | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)$ — **Fitts' Law** |
| $D$ | Distance from pointer to target's center | pixels (px) or cm | — |
| $W$ | Width of the target along the axis of approach | pixels (px) or cm | — |
| $a$ | Start/stop time of the device | ms | Empirical constant (≈ 50–200 ms) |
| $b$ | inherent device speed | ms/bit | Empirical constant (≈ 100–200 ms/bit) |
| $ID$ | Index of Difficulty | bits | $ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{2D}{W} + 1\right)$ — Shannon formulation |
| $TP$ | Throughput | bits/second | $TP = \frac{ID_e}{MT}$ |
| $ID_e$ | Effective Index of Difficulty | bits | $ID_e = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 0.5\right)$ |
| $MT$ | Mean movement time | seconds | — |
| $T_{steer}$ | Steering time through a tunnel | seconds | $T_{steer} = a + b \cdot \frac{D}{W}$ — **Steering Law (Accot-Zhai)** |

**Critical boundary conditions:**
- $W > 0$ (target must have positive width)
- $D \geq 0$ (distance is non-negative)
- $\log_2$ uses the **base-2 logarithm** yielding units in **bits**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Fitts' Law / Window Selection |
|-------|---------------------------------------------|
| **Avionics Cockpit Design** | Sizing and spacing of physical buttons to minimize pilot selection errors under stress |
| **Automotive HMI** | Touch-target sizing on infotainment screens to prevent driver distraction |
| **Medical Devices** | Designing touchscreen UI on patient monitors where selection errors are life-critical |
| **Gaming UI** | Determining optimal button sizes for gamepads and HUD elements |
| **Accessibility** | Enlarging targets for users with motor impairments (Parkinson's, arthritis) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Fitts' Law for Window Selection

Fitts' Law is a **psychophysical model** that quantifies the trade-off between *speed* and *accuracy* when selecting a target. The full derivation for the KTU 2024 board context is as follows.

**Step 1 — The Empirical Observation (1954, Paul Fitts):**
Fitts observed that the time to move a pointer to a target grows as the target gets *smaller* and as the distance to the target gets *larger*. He proposed a model analogous to **Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem** from information theory.

**Step 2 — Drawing the Analogy to Shannon's Theorem:**

Shannon's theorem states the maximum rate of information transmission through a noisy channel:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{S}{N} + 1\right)
$$

where $C$ is channel capacity (bits/s), $B$ is bandwidth, $S$ is signal power, and $N$ is noise power.

Fitts mapped this to human motor control as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Channel Capacity } C &\;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Inverse of movement time } \frac{1}{MT} \\[4pt]
\text{Bandwidth } B &\;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Inherent device speed constant } \frac{1}{b} \\[4pt]
\frac{S}{N} &\;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{D}{W} \quad \text{(signal-to-noise } \approx \text{ distance-to-width ratio)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into Shannon's formula:**

$$
\frac{1}{MT} = \frac{1}{b} \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for Movement Time $MT$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{MT} &= \frac{1}{b} \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \\[6pt]
MT &= \frac{b}{\log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)} \\[6pt]
MT &= b \cdot \frac{1}{\log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)} \\[6pt]
\text{Let } ID &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \\[6pt]
\therefore \; MT &= \frac{b}{ID}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Adding the Start/Stop Latency Constant $a$:**

Real human movement includes a fixed overhead (reaction time + device activation). We add a constant $a$ (in seconds):

$$
\boxed{\,MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) = a + b \cdot ID\,}
$$

**Step 6 — The Original Fitts (1954) Formulation:**

Note that Fitts' *original* 1954 paper used a slightly different form (the "2D/W" form) which is also widely cited:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)
$$

The 2D form is more conservative (predicts slightly longer times) and was used because Fitts did not include the "+1" inside the logarithm. The **"+1" version** (sometimes called the **Shannon formulation** or **Welford formulation**) is preferred in modern HCI textbooks.

**Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

A user must click a window's **Minimize button** in a desktop GUI.
- Distance from current cursor position to the button center: $D = 240$ pixels
- Width of the minimize button: $W = 24$ pixels
- Device constants for a typical mouse: $a = 0.05$ s, $b = 0.10$ s/bit

Calculate the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** and the **Mean Movement Time (MT)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \\[4pt]
&= \log_2\!\left(\frac{240}{24} + 1\right) \\[4pt]
&= \log_2\!\left(10 + 1\right) \\[4pt]
&= \log_2(11) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\ln 11}{\ln 2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2.3979}{0.6931} \\[4pt]
&\approx 3.4594 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now computing Mean Movement Time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\[4pt]
&= 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot 3.4594 \\[4pt]
&= 0.05 + 0.34594 \\[4pt]
&= 0.39594 \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
&\approx 395.94 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Selecting the minimize button from a distance of 240 pixels takes approximately **396 ms** — consistent with empirically measured mouse-click times in HCI literature.

### 3.2 Throughput Computation for Window Selection

The **Throughput (TP)** measures the *information-processing rate* of the human-motor channel and is a device-independent metric (useful for comparing mice vs. touchscreens vs. eye-trackers).

**Step 1 — Effective ID:**

$$
ID_e = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 0.5\right)
$$

The constant 0.5 corrects for the fact that effective target width is slightly less than physical width due to endpoint variability.

**Step 2 — Throughput:**

$$
TP = \frac{ID_e}{MT} \quad \left[\frac{\text{bits}}{\text{second}}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example (continuing from above):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_e &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{240}{24} + 0.5\right) = \log_2(10.5) \approx 3.392 \text{ bits} \\[4pt]
TP &= \frac{3.392}{0.39594} \approx 8.566 \text{ bits/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

A throughput of ~8.5 bps is typical for skilled mouse users. KTU's reference range is **4–10 bits/second** for healthy adults.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Navigation Schemes

| Property | Linear | Hierarchical | Direct (Map) | Hypertext | Tabular/Search |
|----------|--------|--------------|--------------|-----------|----------------|
| **User Cognitive Load** | Very Low | Low | Medium | High | Low |
| **Scalability to large info spaces** | Poor | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Predictability of next step** | High | High | Low | Very Low | Medium |
| **Supports Non-linear Exploration** | No | Limited | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Disorientation Risk** | Very Low | Low | Medium | High | Low |
| **Best Cognitive Level (Bloom)** | Remember | Understand | Apply | Analyze | Evaluate |
| **KTU 2024 Frequency in Question Papers** | High | Very High | Medium | Very High | High |

### 3.4 Steering Law Derivation (for Window Resize / Drag Operations)

When the user *drags* the corner of a window to resize it, the pointer must travel through a constrained **tunnel** of width $W$ over a distance $D$. The **Steering Law** (Accot & Zhai, 1997) applies:

$$
T_{steer} = a + b \cdot \frac{D}{W}
$$

Unlike Fitts' Law, this uses a **linear** (not logarithmic) relationship because the user must continuously correct trajectory — this is a closed-loop task, not a discrete ballistic movement.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoided:** Many students mistakenly apply Fitts' Law to window-resize tasks. Resize is a **steering** task, not a **selection** task. Fitts' Law applies to *discrete* target acquisition; Steering Law applies to *continuous* path-following.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Navigation Schemes

```mermaid

    classDef ex4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000
    classDef ex5 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000
    classDef sel fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef sel1 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000
    classDef sel2 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000
    classDef sel3 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000
    classDef sel4 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000
    classDef sel5 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Window Selection State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INACTIVE
    INACTIVE --> HOVERED : Mouse Pointer Enters Window Bounds
    HOVERED --> FOCUSED : Click on Title Bar or Client Area
    HOVERED --> INACTIVE : Pointer Leaves Without Click
    FOCUSED --> ACTIVE : User Begins Typing or Scrolling
    ACTIVE --> FOCUSED : User Releases Input
    FOCUSED --> INACTIVE : Window Minimized or Closed
    FOCUSED --> HOVERED : Another Window Clicked Competes for Focus
    ACTIVE --> OCCLUDED : Another Window Covers This One
    OCCLUDED --> FOCUSED : Window Raised to Front
    OCCLUDED --> INACTIVE : Window Minimized While Covered
    ACTIVE --> [*] : Window Destroyed
    INACTIVE --> [*] : Application Exit
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Flow of Click-to-Select Window

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["User Moves Mouse"]:::step
    detect["OS Detects Pointer Position via Interrupt Handler"]:::step
    hit["Hit Test Against Z Order Stack of Windows"]:::step
    found{"Pointer Hovers Over a Window Region?"}:::decision
    hover["Mark Window as HOVERED Highlight Title Bar"]:::step
    click{"User Clicks LMB?"}:::decision
    raise["OS Raises Window to Top of Z Order"]:::step
    focus["OS Sets INPUT FOCUS to This Window"]:::step
    reroute["All Keyboard Events Rerouted to Client Area"]:::step
    done["Window is Now ACTIVE and SELECTED"]:::done
    noop["No Selection State Change"]:::step
    stop["Process Ends"]:::done

    start --> detect --> hit --> found
    found -- YES --> hover --> click
    found -- NO --> noop --> stop
    click -- YES --> raise --> focus --> reroute --> done
    click -- NO --> stop

    classDef step fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef done fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#548235,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture Block — Fitts' Law Engine for Window Target Sizing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT LAYER Pointer Coordinates X Y px"]:::input
    B["DISTANCE COMPUTE MODULE Euclidean D between pointer and target center"]:::module
    C["TARGET WIDTH READER Reads W from Window Manager Registry"]:::module
    D["INDEX OF DIFFICULTY CALCULATOR Log2 D over W plus 1"]:::module
    E["MOVEMENT TIME PREDICTOR MT equals a plus b times ID"]:::module
    F["THROUGHPUT ANALYZER TP equals IDe over MT bits per second"]:::module
    G["RECOMMENDATION ENGINE Resize Target W if ID exceeds 6 bits"]:::module
    H["OUTPUT LAYER Recommended Button Width and Predicted MT"]:::output

    A --> B
    B --> D
    C --> D
    D --> E
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H

    classDef input fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000
    classDef module fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#548235,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*Define the term "Index of Difficulty" in the context of Fitts' Law. What does it quantify and what are its units?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Index of Difficulty (ID)** quantifies the *relative perceptual-motor challenge* of moving a pointer to a target as a function of the target's distance $D$ and width $W$. It is formally defined as:

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \quad \text{(Shannon formulation)}
$$

or equivalently, in Fitts' original form:

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) \quad \text{(Fitts 1954 form)}
$$

It is measured in **bits** because it is derived from Shannon's information-theoretic channel capacity formula. A *larger ID* indicates a *more difficult* selection task (e.g., a small, distant target), and a *smaller ID* indicates an *easier* task (e.g., a large, close target). In window-selection contexts, designing window controls with low ID (large minimize/close buttons) reduces user error and selection time.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*List and briefly define any three navigation schemes used in window-based interfaces.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Linear (Sequential) Navigation** — A fixed-order, step-by-step traversal in which the user proceeds from one screen/dialog to the next with no backtracking alternatives. *Example*: software installation wizards.

2. **Hierarchical Navigation** — A tree-structured traversal in which the user navigates from a parent node to its children via expansion. The system maintains a breadcrumb trail and a back-stack. *Example*: Windows Explorer's folder tree, Start Menu.

3. **Hypertext (Associative) Navigation** — A non-linear, link-based traversal in which the user clicks embedded references to jump between related content items. *Example*: Wikipedia, web browsers, integrated help systems.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**Q. (a)** Explain the five major navigation schemes used in window-based user interfaces. For each, give one real-world example and state one advantage and one disadvantage. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The five major navigation schemes defined in HCI literature (Shneiderman, Plaisant) are:

**1. Linear / Sequential Navigation**
- *Mechanism*: User follows a predetermined sequence of screens.
- *Example*: A tax-filing wizard that asks PAN → Income → Deductions → Bank Details in fixed order.
- *Advantage*: Low cognitive load; user cannot miss required steps.
- *Disadvantage*: Inflexible — cannot skip or reorder steps.

**2. Hierarchical (Tree-Based) Navigation**
- *Mechanism*: User expands and collapses nodes in a parent-child tree.
- *Example*: Windows File Explorer, IDE Project panels, corporate intranet menus.
- *Advantage*: Predictable, scalable to thousands of items.
- *Disadvantage*: Deep trees cause "buried" items; requires many clicks to reach leaves.

**3. Direct / Spatial Navigation**
- *Mechanism*: User pans/zooms on a 2D or 3D spatial representation.
- *Example*: Google Maps, CAD software, flight simulators.
- *Advantage*: Excellent for spatially-organized data; supports gestalt overview.
- *Disadvantage*: Requires spatial reasoning; disorienting in 3D environments.

**4. Hypertext / Associative Navigation**
- *Mechanism*: User activates embedded links to traverse non-linear content.
- *Example*: World Wide Web, knowledge bases, context-sensitive help.
- *Advantage*: Highly flexible, supports associative thinking.
- *Disadvantage*: User may experience "lost in hyperspace" disorientation.

**5. Tabular / Search-Driven Navigation**
- *Mechanism*: User issues a query; system filters a tabular dataset and presents results.
- *Example*: Database front-ends, e-commerce product catalogs, library catalogs.
- *Advantage*: Scales to millions of records; powerful for known-item retrieval.
- *Disadvantage*: Requires the user to know what to search for (not good for browsing).

> *(Award 1 mark for each scheme's mechanism, 0.5 mark for example, 0.5 mark for advantage/disadvantage.)*

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the process of "Selection of a Window" in a graphical user interface. Explain the role of the window manager, focus, and the Z-order. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — The Window Manager (WM):**
The window manager is a system-level software component (part of the OS or windowing system such as X11, Wayland, Win32, or Cocoa) responsible for:
- Drawing window decorations (title bar, borders, scrollbars).
- Routing input events to the correct application.
- Managing the **focus stack** and the **Z-order**.

**Step 2 — Window Selection Process:**
When the user clicks on a window region:
1. The pointer's screen coordinates are captured.
2. A **hit-test** is performed: the WM walks the Z-order from top to bottom and finds the *topmost* window whose non-transparent region contains the pointer.
3. That window is **raised** in the Z-order.
4. The WM sends a *focus-in* event to the application owning that window.
5. The application's *focus widget* (e.g., a text field) becomes the active input recipient.
6. The previously focused window receives a *focus-out* event.

**Step 3 — Z-Order (Stacking Order):**
The Z-order is the conceptual depth-ordering of windows on a 2D screen. Windows with higher Z-values visually overlap those with lower Z-values. It is typically maintained as a doubly-linked list.

**Step 4 — Focus and Its Two Models:**
- **Click-to-Focus (Focus-Follows-Click)**: Used by Windows and macOS. A window receives focus *only* on click.
- **Focus-Follows-Mouse (Sloppy Focus)**: Used by many Linux desktop environments. A window receives focus *whenever the pointer enters its region*.

**Step 5 — Visual Cues:**
Active windows typically display:
- A **colored** title bar (vs. gray for inactive).
- A **focus ring** (e.g., a dotted rectangle) around the focused widget.
- A **caret** blinking in a text field.

> *(Award marks for: 1 mark for WM definition, 2 marks for selection process steps, 1 mark for Z-order explanation, 2 marks for focus models, 1 mark for visual cues.)*

---

### 5.3 Alternative Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** State and explain Fitts' Law as applied to window-target selection in GUI design. Derive the Index of Difficulty from Shannon's channel capacity theorem. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Statement of Fitts' Law:**
> *"The time to acquire and select a target is a logarithmic function of the distance to the target divided by the size of the target."*

Mathematically:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) = a + b \cdot ID
$$

where $MT$ is mean movement time, $a$ is the start/stop time constant, $b$ is the inherent speed constant, $D$ is distance to target, $W$ is target width, and $ID$ is Index of Difficulty in bits.

**Derivation from Shannon's Theorem:**

Shannon's channel capacity formula:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{S}{N} + 1\right)
$$

Fitts drew the following analogies:

| Shannon | Fitts |
|---------|-------|
| $C$ (channel capacity, bits/s) | $1 / MT$ (inverse movement time) |
| $B$ (bandwidth) | $1 / b$ (device-dependent speed) |
| $S / N$ (signal-to-noise ratio) | $D / W$ (distance-to-width ratio) |

Substituting:

$$
\frac{1}{MT} = \frac{1}{b} \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

Rearranging for $MT$:

$$
MT = \frac{b}{\log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)} = \frac{b}{ID}
$$

Adding the start/stop constant $a$:

$$
\boxed{MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)}
$$

**Implications for Window Design:**
- *Larger* window control buttons → smaller ID → faster selection.
- *Closer* window controls to the user's typical pointer rest position → smaller D → faster.
- A common design heuristic: keep $ID < 4$ bits (≈ target size ≥ $D/15$).

---

**Q. (b)** A user needs to click a "Close" button on a window. The pointer is currently 180 pixels away and the close button is 20 pixels wide. Using the device constants $a = 0.04$ s and $b = 0.12$ s/bit, compute:
(i) the Index of Difficulty, *(2 marks)*
(ii) the Mean Movement Time, *(3 marks)*
(iii) the Throughput using the Welford effective-ID formula. *(2 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $D = 180$ pixels
- $W = 20$ pixels
- $a = 0.04$ s
- $b = 0.12$ s/bit

**(i) Index of Difficulty (Shannon form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \\[4pt]
&= \log_2\!\left(\frac{180}{20} + 1\right) \\[4pt]
&= \log_2\!\left(9 + 1\right) \\[4pt]
&= \log_2(10) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\ln 10}{\ln 2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2.3026}{0.6931} \\[4pt]
&\approx 3.3219 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Mean Movement Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\[4pt]
&= 0.04 + 0.12 \cdot 3.3219 \\[4pt]
&= 0.04 + 0.39863 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.4386 \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
&\approx 438.6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Throughput (using effective ID):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID_e &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 0.5\right) = \log_2(9.5) \approx 3.2479 \text{ bits} \\[4pt]
TP &= \frac{ID_e}{MT} = \frac{3.2479}{0.4386} \approx 7.405 \text{ bits/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $ID$ | ≈ 3.32 bits |
| $MT$ | ≈ 438.6 ms |
| $TP$ | ≈ 7.41 bits/second |

The throughput of ~7.4 bps is within the typical range of 4–10 bps for skilled mouse users, indicating an efficient window-selection interaction.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Window Selection & Navigation Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing Fitts' Law with Steering Law** — Fitts' applies to *discrete target clicks*; Steering applies to *continuous drag/resize* paths. Examiners frequently test this distinction. **(−2 marks if misapplied)**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in ID** — Using the Fitts-1954 form $\log_2(2D/W)$ when the question expects the Shannon form $\log_2(D/W + 1)$. State which form you are using. **(−1 mark if ambiguous)**
>
> 3. **Missing units** — $ID$ is in *bits*, $MT$ is in *seconds or ms*, $TP$ is in *bits/second*. Writing "$ID = 3.32$" without "bits" loses 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 4. **Mixing up navigation schemes** — Listing "Linear, Hierarchical, Network, Tabular" (the Nielsen 4-scheme classification) instead of the canonical 5-scheme Shneiderman classification accepted by KTU. Stick to: *Linear, Hierarchical, Direct/Spatial, Hypertext, Tabular/Search*.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Z-order in window selection** — A common 3-mark sub-question is "Explain window selection." Students describe mouse click but *omit* the hit-test, Z-order raise, and focus handoff. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 6. **Rounding $\log_2$ errors** — In the worked example above, $\log_2(10) \approx 3.3219$. Students often write 3.3 or 3.32 — both are accepted, but truncating to 3 will compound errors in the MT calculation.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Navigation Schemes & Window Selection**

- **Five navigation schemes** (verbatim for exams): *Linear / Sequential, Hierarchical, Direct (Spatial), Hypertext, Tabular / Search-driven.*
- **Linear**: fixed order → wizards, install steps.
- **Hierarchical**: tree, parent-child → file systems, menus.
- **Direct/Spatial**: 2D/3D map → Google Maps, CAD.
- **Hypertext**: non-linear links → Web, help systems.
- **Tabular/Search**: query-driven → databases, e-commerce.
- **Window selection** = the act of binding *input focus* to one window among many.
- **Window Manager (WM)** is the OS component that mediates window selection, Z-order, and focus.
- **Z-order** is the depth-stacking list of windows; topmost receives pointer events first.
- **Hit-test** = the WM's geometric test to find which window region contains the pointer.
- **Focus models**: *Click-to-focus* (Windows/macOS) vs *Focus-follows-mouse* (X11/Linux).
- **Fitts' Law** = $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$; gives the *discrete selection* time.
- **Steering Law** = $T_{steer} = a + b \cdot D/W$; applies to *continuous path-following* (window resize, drag).
- **Index of Difficulty (ID)** is measured in **bits**; throughput in **bits/second**.
- **Heuristic**: keep $ID < 4$ bits for window-control buttons to ensure sub-500 ms selection.
- **Throughput** for skilled users: typically **4–10 bits/second**.
- **In keyboard navigation** (Alt-Tab): selection is *cyclic, not ballistic*, so Fitts' Law does not directly apply; **Steering Law** or a **menu-traversal time** model is more appropriate.
- **Disorientation** is the principal risk in hypertext and direct-spatial schemes — mitigate with breadcrumbs, mini-maps, and back-stacks.
- **Always** state units (bits, ms, bps) and which form of Fitts' Law (Shannon vs 1954) you are using.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
