**Interpretation:** A 16% error rate is **too high** for a financial application. Reliability should ideally be **> 0.95** for mission-critical systems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Evaluation Technique

| Evaluation Technique | Stage of Design | Number of Users | Cost | Time | Type of Data |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Heuristic Evaluation** | Early (Low-fi) | 3–5 experts | Low | 2–4 hours | Qualitative |
| **Cognitive Walkthrough** | Early–Mid (Lo-fi) | 1–3 experts | Low | 1–2 hours | Qualitative |
| **Think-Aloud Usability Test** | Mid–High-fi | 5–10 users | Medium | 1–2 days | Qualitative + Quantitative |
| **A/B Testing** | High-fi / Live | Large sample (100s) | High | Weeks | Quantitative |
| **Questionnaires (SUS, QUIS)** | Any stage | 20+ users | Low | Hours | Quantitative |
| **Eye-Tracking** | Mid–High-fi | 10–20 users | High | Days | Quantitative (gaze data) |
| **Field Study / Contextual Inquiry** | Early (Pre-design) | 5–15 users | High | Weeks | Qualitative (rich) |
| **Automated Tools (e.g., Lighthouse)** | High-fi / Live | N/A | Free–Low | Minutes | Quantitative (WCAG) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Which evaluation method to use when?" apply the **3-question rule:**
> 1. *What stage is the design in?* (early → heuristic; late → user testing)
> 2. *What type of data is needed?* (qualitative → think-aloud; quantitative → A/B)
> 3. *What is the budget and timeline?* (low → heuristic; high → field study)

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Design Rules to Principles

| Principle | Concrete Design Rule | Real-world Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Visibility** | Show all available options on screen | Google Docs toolbar always visible |
| **Feedback** | Every click produces a visible response | Button ripple effect on Android |
| **Consistency** | Use the same color for the same action across screens | Red always = "delete" in Gmail |
| **Affordance** | Use skeuomorphic cues to suggest action | Folder icon looks like a folder |
| **Constraint** | Disable inapplicable options dynamically | "Reply All" greyed out in a single-recipient email |
| **Error Prevention** | Confirm before destructive action | "Are you sure you want to delete?" dialog |
| **Recognition over Recall** | Show recently opened files | File menu lists recent documents |
| **Mapping** | Arrange controls in spatial relation to results | Car headlights on left light up the left side |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Iterative Design Cycle — Star Lifecycle Model (Hix & Hartson)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["REQUIREMENTS GATHERING"] --> nodeB["CONCEPTUAL DESIGN"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["PROTOTYPING"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["EVALUATION"]
    nodeD -->|Feedback Loop| nodeB
    nodeD -->|Major Issues Found| nodeA
    nodeB -->|Alternative Concept| nodeC
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- The cycle starts with **Requirements Gathering** (understanding users).
- Moves to **Conceptual Design** (sketches, scenarios).
- **Prototyping** builds a tangible artifact.
- **Evaluation** tests it with users/experts.
- Two feedback paths exist: a **minor feedback loop** back to Design (for refinements) and a **major feedback loop** back to Requirements (if fundamental issues are found).

---

## 4.2 Prototyping Fidelity Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START: Need a Prototype] --> nodeB{What is the Goal?}
    nodeB -->|Explore Ideas| nodeC{Resources Available?}
    nodeB -->|Validate Design| nodeF{User Testing Required?}
    nodeC -->|Low Budget| nodeD[LOW-FIDELITY: Paper Wireframes]
    nodeC -->|Moderate Budget| nodeE[MID-FIDELITY: Figma Wireframes]
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[HIGH-FIDELITY: Interactive Mockup]
    nodeF -->|Stakeholder Demo| nodeH[CLICKABLE PROTOTYPE: Proto.io]
```

---

## 4.3 Nielsen's 10 Heuristics — Categorical Grouping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Nielsen 10 Heuristics"] --> nodeB["VISIBILITY GROUP"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["CONTROL GROUP"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["CONSISTENCY GROUP"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["COGNITION GROUP"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["AESTHETICS GROUP"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["ERROR GROUP"]
    nodeA --> nodeH["HELP GROUP"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["H1: System Status"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["H2: Match Real World"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["H3: User Control"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["H7: Flexibility"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["H4: Consistency"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["H6: Recognition over Recall"]
    nodeF --> nodeF1["H8: Minimalist Design"]
    nodeG --> nodeG1["H5: Error Prevention"]
    nodeG --> nodeG2["H9: Error Recovery"]
    nodeH --> nodeH1["H10: Help and Docs"]
```

---

## 4.4 Evaluation Technique Selection Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Choose Evaluation Method] --> nodeB{Design Stage?}
    nodeB -->|Pre-Design| nodeC[Contextual Inquiry]
    nodeB -->|Low-Fi Prototype| nodeD[Heuristic Evaluation]
    nodeB -->|Mid-Fi Prototype| nodeE[Cognitive Walkthrough]
    nodeB -->|High-Fi Prototype| nodeF[Think-Aloud Testing]
    nodeB -->|Live System| nodeG[A/B Testing]
    nodeC --> nodeH{Data Type Needed?}
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH -->|Qualitative| nodeI[Interviews, Think-Aloud]
    nodeH -->|Quantitative| nodeJ[SUS, NASA-TLX, Eye-Tracking]
    nodeH -->|Both| nodeK[Mixed Methods: User Testing + Questionnaire]
```

---

## 4.5 Design Rationale Capture (IBIS Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[ISSUE] --> nodeB[POSITION A]
    nodeA --> nodeC[POSITION B]
    nodeA --> nodeD[POSITION C]
    nodeB --> nodeE[ARGUMENT Pro A]
    nodeB --> nodeF[ARGUMENT Con A]
    nodeC --> nodeG[ARGUMENT Pro B]
    nodeC --> nodeH[ARGUMENT Con B]
    nodeE --> nodeI[DECISION: Adopt A]
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

**Explanation:** IBIS (Issue-Based Information System) starts with an **Issue** (the design question). Multiple **Positions** (proposed solutions) are raised. Each position has **Arguments** (pros and cons). The final **Decision** is the rationale for the chosen design.

---

## 4.6 User-Centered Design Process (ISO 9241-210)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Use Context Analysis"] --> node2["User Requirements"]
    node2 --> node3["Design Solutions"]
    node3 --> node4["Prototyping"]
    node4 --> node5["Evaluation Against Requirements"]
    node5 -->|Iterate| node3
    node5 -->|Major Issues| node1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (SHORT ANSWERS)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**List and briefly explain any two of Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules of interface design.**

**Model Answer:**

**Rule 1: Strive for Consistency.**
Consistency in actions, terminology, layout, and color should be maintained throughout the interface. For example, the "Save" icon and its keyboard shortcut (Ctrl+S) should work the same way across all screens. This reduces user learning time and minimizes errors.

**Rule 2: Offer Informative Feedback.**
For every user action, the system should provide a visible, timely response. For example, after clicking "Submit," a confirmation message ("Form submitted successfully") should appear. Feedback confirms that the action was registered and informs the user of the result.

*(Any two of the 8 rules with explanations are acceptable.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between low-fidelity and high-fidelity prototypes.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Low-Fidelity Prototype | High-Fidelity Prototype |
|---|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Rough sketches, paper-based | Polished, pixel-perfect |
| **Interactivity** | Static or limited | Fully interactive |
| **Cost** | Very low | High |
| **Time to Build** | Hours | Days to weeks |
| **Purpose** | Early ideation, concept exploration | Final validation, stakeholder demos |
| **Tools** | Paper, Balsamiq, whiteboard | Figma, Adobe XD, Axure |
| **User Reaction** | Reacts to concept | Reacts to full experience |

Low-fidelity prototypes are used in the **early stages** to explore ideas quickly, while high-fidelity prototypes are used in the **later stages** to validate designs with realistic user interactions.

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Adapted from Past Papers]**

**(a)** Explain the **iterative design process** in detail, with a neat block diagram. Discuss how evaluation feeds back into design refinement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics** with one example for each heuristic. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Iterative Design Process (7 Marks)

**Definition:**
Iterative design is a user-centered design methodology in which the design is progressively refined through repeated cycles of **Analysis → Design → Prototype → Evaluate**. Each cycle (iteration) improves the design based on feedback from the previous one.

**Phases of Iterative Design:**

1. **Requirements Analysis:** Identify user needs, tasks, and goals. Methods: interviews, observation, contextual inquiry.
2. **Design:** Generate multiple design alternatives using sketches, scenarios, and personas.
3. **Prototyping:** Build a tangible artifact (low-fidelity or high-fidelity).
4. **Evaluation:** Test the prototype with users or experts to identify usability problems.
5. **Refinement / Iteration:** Incorporate feedback into the next design cycle.

**Role of Evaluation in Iteration:**
Evaluation is the **feedback mechanism** that drives iteration. Without evaluation, iteration is meaningless. Evaluation produces:
- **Usability problems** (e.g., users can't find the search button).
- **Quantitative metrics** (e.g., task success rate = 60% — too low).
- **User satisfaction scores** (e.g., SUS = 50 — poor).

These findings are then mapped to design changes, and the next prototype addresses them. This forms a **closed feedback loop**, ensuring continuous improvement until usability goals are met.

**Block Diagram:**
*(See Section 4.1 of these notes for the Star Lifecycle Model diagram.)*

**Conclusion:** Iterative design ensures that the final product is **user-validated**, not just designer-assumed.

---

#### Part (b) — Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics (7 Marks)

| # | Heuristic | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Visibility of system status** | A progress bar appears during file upload. |
| 2 | **Match between system and real world** | A trash icon for "delete" (familiar metaphor). |
| 3 | **User control and freedom** | "Undo" button in word processors. |
| 4 | **Consistency and standards** | All links are blue and underlined across the website. |
| 5 | **Error prevention** | Confirming before deleting a file. |
| 6 | **Recognition rather than recall** | A drop-down menu lists options instead of asking the user to type. |
| 7 | **Flexibility and efficiency of use** | Keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+C) for expert users. |
| 8 | **Aesthetic and minimalist design** | A clean home screen with only essential icons. |
| 9 | **Help users recognize, diagnose, recover from errors** | "Invalid password. Try again or click 'Forgot Password'." |
| 10 | **Help and documentation** | A searchable help center in the app. |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **various techniques used for prototyping in practice**, with examples and tool recommendations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Design Rationale**. Discuss the **IBIS (Issue-Based Information System)** method of capturing design rationale with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Prototyping Techniques (7 Marks)

**1. Paper Prototyping:**
- Hand-drawn sketches on paper, sticky notes, whiteboards.
- **Tools:** Paper, pen, sticky notes.
- **Use:** Early brainstorming, exploring layouts.
- **Advantage:** Fastest and cheapest; encourages rapid iteration.

**2. Wireframing (Low-Fidelity Digital):**
- Simple digital mockups showing structure and content.
- **Tools:** Balsamiq, Wireframe.cc, Figma (low-fi mode).
- **Use:** Information architecture testing.

**3. Storyboarding:**
- Visual narrative showing the user journey through the system.
- **Use:** Communicating flow to stakeholders.

**4. High-Fidelity Interactive Prototyping:**
- Pixel-perfect, clickable prototypes simulating the final product.
- **Tools:** Figma, Adobe XD, Sketch, Axure, Proto.io, InVision.
- **Use:** Realistic user testing and stakeholder presentations.

**5. HTML/CSS/JS Prototyping:**
- Functional web prototypes built with code.
- **Use:** Web application design; allows for realistic data interactions.

**6. Wizard of Oz Prototyping:**
- A human simulates the system's response behind the scenes.
- **Use:** Testing AI or voice interfaces before full implementation.

**7. Video Prototyping:**
- A video simulates the user experience for an app that doesn't yet exist.
- **Use:** Concept communication, securing funding.

**Conclusion:** The choice of prototype depends on the **stage of design**, **fidelity required**, and **resources available**. Iterative design uses a progression from low-fidelity (exploration) to high-fidelity (validation).

---

#### Part (b) — Design Rationale & IBIS (7 Marks)

**Definition:**
*Design Rationale* is the documentation of the reasoning behind a design decision — capturing **why** a particular design was chosen over alternatives, what trade-offs were made, and what assumptions held.

**Importance of Design Rationale:**
- **Knowledge preservation** for future team members.
- **Avoids repeated discussions** of the same issues.
- **Supports design evolution** — when requirements change, rationale helps assess impact.
- **Legal and compliance evidence** in safety-critical systems.

**IBIS (Issue-Based Information System) — by Horst Rittel:**
IBIS structures design rationale into three main elements:

1. **Issue:** The design question or problem to be solved.
2. **Position:** A proposed answer or solution to the issue.
3. **Argument:** A justification (pro or con) for a position, often supported by evidence.

**Worked Example:**

**Issue:** *Which navigation pattern should we use for the e-commerce site — hamburger menu, top-bar, or sidebar?*

- **Position A: Hamburger Menu**
  - Pro: Saves screen space, common in mobile.
  - Con: Low discoverability, hides options.

- **Position B: Top Bar Navigation**
  - Pro: High visibility, familiar to users.
  - Con: Limited space for many categories.

- **Position C: Sidebar Navigation**
  - Pro: Visible at all times, easy access.
  - Con: Takes screen real estate.

**Decision:** Based on user research showing that 80% of our users shop on mobile, we **adopt Position A (Hamburger Menu)** with a clearly visible icon and add a search bar to improve discoverability.

This rationale is now documented and can be revisited if user feedback changes.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and How to Avoid Them):**
>
> 1. **Confusing heuristics with principles.** Heuristics are *evaluation* rules (Nielsen); principles are *design* rules (Norman). Do not interchange.
> 2. **Listing Shneiderman's 8 rules without explanation.** KTU expects a *brief* explanation or example for each.
> 3. **Forgetting the feedback loop** in iterative design. The diagram must show evaluation **feeding back** into design.
> 4. **Writing formulas without units or interpretation.** Always state what the result *means* (e.g., "85% effectiveness = good").
> 5. **Not relating to real-world examples.** Examiners reward **applied** answers, not just textbook definitions.
> 6. **Mixing up HCI Patterns with Design Patterns.** HCI patterns are for *usability*; software design patterns are for *code architecture*.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Iterative Design** = cyclic process of design, prototype, evaluate, refine.
- ✅ **Prototyping Fidelity** ranges from **low (paper)** to **high (interactive mockups)**.
- ✅ **Design Rationale** captures the **why** behind decisions; use **IBIS** (Issue, Position, Argument) or **QOC** (Question, Option, Criteria).
- ✅ **Norman's Principles:** Affordance, Signifier, Mapping, Feedback, Visibility, Constraint, Consistency.
- ✅ **Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules:** Consistency, Universal Usability, Feedback, Closure, Error Prevention, Easy Reversal, User Control, Reduce Memory Load.
- ✅ **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics:** System Status, Real World Match, User Control, Consistency, Error Prevention, Recognition over Recall, Flexibility, Minimalist Design, Error Recovery, Help & Docs.
- ✅ **HCI Patterns** = reusable UI solutions (breadcrumbs, modals, autocomplete, wizards).
- ✅ **Evaluation Techniques** are classified into **User-based, Expert-based, Model-based, Automated**.
- ✅ **Heuristic Evaluation** = 3–5 experts; **Think-Aloud** = 5–10 users; **A/B Testing** = large sample.
- ✅ **Usability Metrics:** Effectiveness = $T_{\text{completed}} \div T_{\text{attempted}} \times 100\%$; SUS Score is out of 100; >68 is acceptable, >80.3 is excellent.
- ✅ **Standards:** ISO 9241 (ergonomics), ISO 9241-11 (usability), ISO 9241-210 (HCD process), WCAG (accessibility).
- ✅ **Golden Number to Remember:** **SUS > 68 = Above Average**; **Effectiveness > 95% for safety-critical systems**; **Error Rate < 5% for high-reliability systems**.
- ✅ **Evaluation always comes before iteration** — you cannot iterate without feedback.
- ✅ **KTU expects:** Block diagrams, real-world examples, formula derivations, and applied reasoning — not just definitions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Goals of Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERACTION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Goals of Evaluation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)**, **Evaluation** is the systematic process of collecting data about a system's **usability**, **functionality**, and **user experience** to determine whether the design meets its intended objectives. The **Goals of Evaluation** define *what* we aim to measure, *why* we measure it, and *how* the results inform iterative design decisions. According to the standard HCI framework (popularized by Jakob Nielsen in *Usability Engineering*, 1993), the goals of evaluation are rooted in a single overarching aim: > **"To improve the usability of a system by identifying specific problems in the user interface before deployment."** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 (Evaluation)** > The module emphasizes three hierarchical tiers of evaluation goals: > 1. **Usability Goals (User Performance)** — *Learnability, Efficiency, Memorability, Low Error Rate, Satisfaction* > 2. **User Experience Goals (User Reaction)** — *Enjoyable, Fun, Engaging, Aesthetically Pleasing, Supportive of Creativity* > 3. **Organizational / Practical Goals** — *Increased productivity, Reduced training costs, Improved sales, Reduced errors at work* ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are the **owner of a newly opened restaurant**: - You wouldn't just cook food and hope customers love it. You would set **goals** to *evaluate* success: - *"Can a first-time customer understand the menu easily?"* → **Learnability** - *"How fast can the waiter serve the table?"* → **Efficiency** - *"Will the customer remember the ordering process next time?"* → **Memorability** - *"How often do orders go wrong?"* → **Errors** - *"Are customers smiling when they leave?"* → **Satisfaction** Just like the restaurant, every interactive software system (a banking app, a learning portal, a medical dashboard) has **measurable goals of evaluation**. Without these goals, a designer is essentially *flying blind*. > [!NOTE] > **Why Goals Matter:** Goals of evaluation act as a *compass* — they convert subjective feelings ("I think the app is hard to use") into **objective, measurable, and comparable design criteria**. ## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for KTU Examiner Recognition) - **Usability** — The extent to which a product can be used by specified users to achieve specified goals with effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction in a specified context of use (ISO 9241-11 standard). - **Formative Evaluation** — Evaluation conducted *during* the design cycle to *form* and refine the design. - **Summative Evaluation** — Evaluation conducted *after* design completion to *summarize* and validate the product. - **Heuristic Evaluation** — A usability inspection method where experts compare the interface against a set of recognized usability principles. - **User Experience (UX)** — A person's entire perception and response resulting from the use or anticipated use of a product, system, or service. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Nielsen Usability Framework — Five Classical Goals Jakob Nielsen (1993) consolidated the discipline of usability evaluation by defining **five attribute-level goals**. Every usability test, questionnaire, or metric in industry traces back to these pillars. ### 2.1.1 Learnability - **Definition:** How easy is it for *first-time / novice users* to accomplish basic tasks? - **Why it matters:** Determines the *learning curve* and *time-to-first-success*. - **Measured by:** Time taken to complete a task on first exposure; number of errors during initial use. - **Real-world utility:** Critical for *consumer-facing* apps (e.g., Zomato, WhatsApp), where users have no training. ### 2.1.2 Efficiency - **Definition:** Once users have learned the system, how *fast* can they perform tasks? - **Why it matters:** Determines *productivity gains* in professional / industrial contexts. - **Measured by:** Time per task after the learning period; *tasks per hour*. - **Real-world utility:** Vital for *enterprise software* (e.g., SAP, Tally, AutoCAD) where expert users are paid by the hour. ### 2.1.3 Memorability - **Definition:** When users return after a *period of non-use* (days, weeks), how easily can they re-establish proficiency? - **Why it matters:** Determines the *retention cost* of casual / intermittent users. - **Measured by:** Time to regain prior performance level after an idle interval. - **Real-world utility:** Critical for *infrequently-used* systems (e.g., tax-filing portals, ticket-booking apps). ### 2.1.4 Low Error Rate (Errors) - **Definition:** How many *mistakes* do users make, how severe are they, and how easily do they recover? - **Why it matters:** Errors in *safety-critical* systems (aviation, medical, nuclear) can cost lives. - **Measured by:** Error count per task; error severity rating; recovery time. - **Real-world utility:** Non-negotiable for *medical infusion pumps*, *aircraft cockpits*, *industrial control systems*. ### 2.1.5 Satisfaction - **Definition:** How *pleasant* and *acceptable* is the design to use? - **Why it matters:** Drives *brand loyalty*, *word-of-mouth*, and *repeat engagement*. - **Measured by:** Subjective rating scales (e.g., **System Usability Scale (SUS)** scoring 0–100; **Net Promoter Score (NPS)**). - **Real-world utility:** Key differentiator in *competitive consumer markets* (smartphones, streaming platforms). > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for KTU Exams — "L-MEES"** > **L**earnability · **M**emorability · **E**fficiency · **E**rrors (low) · **S**atisfaction ## 2.2 Shneiderman's Eight Golden Rules of Interface Design Prof. Ben Shneiderman (University of Maryland) framed design principles that *operationalize* the goals of evaluation. They are widely used in **KTU 2024 Scheme** HCI curricula as evaluation heuristics: 1. **Strive for consistency** — Identical terminology, actions, and situations should be presented identically. 2. **Enable frequent users to use shortcuts** — Tailor the system for both novices and experts. 3. **Offer informative feedback** — For every user action, provide visible system response. 4. **Design dialogs to yield closure** — Group actions into sequences with a clear beginning, middle, and end. 5. **Offer simple error handling** — Errors should be prevented where possible; if they occur, recovery should be easy. 6. **Permit easy reversal of actions** — "Undo" reduces anxiety and encourages exploration. 7. **Support internal locus of control** — User should feel they are in command, not the system. 8. **Reduce short-term memory load** — Keep displays simple; recognition over recall. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **No pipes inside table cells — uses `\mid` and `\vert` for absolute value notation.** | **Goal / Metric** | **Standard Symbol** | **Definition / Formula** | **Typical Unit / Range** | |---|---|---|---| | Task Completion Rate | $TCR$ | $TCR = \dfrac{\text{Tasks completed successfully}}{\text{Total tasks attempted}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (0% to 100%) | | Time-on-Task | $T_{task}$ | Mean time to complete a given task | Seconds $\vert$ Minutes | | Error Rate | $E_r$ | $E_r = \dfrac{\text{Errors observed}}{\text{Tasks completed}}$ | Errors per task | | Efficiency | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Tasks completed}}{\text{Time spent (sec)}}$ | Tasks per second | | Learning Curve Slope | $S_{learn}$ | $S_{learn} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{task}}{\Delta \text{Sessions}}$ (negative = improving) | Seconds per session | | System Usability Scale | $SUS$ | Likert 10-item questionnaire; score $\in [0, 100]$ | Dimensionless; > 68 = above average | | Net Promoter Score | $NPS$ | $NPS = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$ | $\in [-100, +100]$ | | Mean Time to Recovery | $MTTR$ | Average time taken to recover from an error | Seconds | | Memorability Index | $M_{idx}$ | $M_{idx} = 1 - \dfrac{\vert T_{relearn} - T_{initial} \vert}{T_{initial}}$ | Dimensionless, $\in [0, 1]$ | ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility | **Application Domain** | **Primary Evaluation Goal** | **Engineering Justification** | |---|---|---| | **Medical Devices** (Pacemakers, Infusion Pumps) | Low Error Rate | Patient safety; regulatory compliance (FDA IEC 62366) | | **E-commerce Portals** (Amazon, Flipkart) | Efficiency, Satisfaction | Conversion rate, customer lifetime value | | **Aviation Cockpits** | Learnability, Errors, Memorability | Mission-critical tasks under stress | | **Educational Software** (MOOCs, e-learning) | Learnability, Satisfaction | Engagement, retention, learning outcomes | | **Industrial Control (SCADA)** | Efficiency, Errors | Real-time process safety; reduced downtime | | **Government e-Services** (DigiLocker, IRCTC) | Memorability, Satisfaction | Inclusivity for diverse literacy levels | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Derivations > [!NOTE] > Since *Goals of Evaluation* is a *conceptual + quantitative hybrid* topic (per KTU 2024 HCI syllabus), this section derives the **decision logic** for choosing goals and **computes example metrics** for each goal using Nielsen's framework. ## 3.1 Analytical Framework: Mapping Goals to Evaluation Phases The **Goal-Question-Metric (GQM)** paradigm (Basili, Caldiera, Rombach) is the standard method used to *operationalize* Nielsen's goals. The derivation proceeds as follows: **Step 1 — Define the Goal (G)** A goal is expressed at three levels: *Object* (what is being evaluated), *Purpose* (why), *Focus* (which attribute), *Viewpoint* (whose perspective), *Context*. Example Goal: **Analyze** the *checkout process of an e-commerce mobile app* for the purpose of *evaluating* its *Learnability* with respect to *novice users* from the *viewpoint of* *end-customers* in a *rural Indian context*. **Step 2 — Refine into Questions (Q)** Each goal is refined into measurable questions: - Q1: How long does a first-time user take to complete checkout? - Q2: How many errors occur during the first attempt? - Q3: How many users abandon the flow? **Step 3 — Define Metrics (M)** Each question maps to a metric: - Q1 → $T_{task}$ in seconds - Q2 → $E_r$ in errors per session - Q3 → $TCR$ in percentage ## 3.2 Quantitative Derivation — Worked Example **Scenario:** 30 first-time users test a new **Kerala State Lottery Mobile App**. They are asked to (1) register, (2) buy a ticket, (3) check results. Test observations recorded: | **User** | **Task 1 Time (s)** | **Task 1 Errors** | **Task 2 Completed?** | **Task 3 Time (s)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 45 | 0 | Yes | 20 | | 2 | 60 | 1 | Yes | 18 | | 3 | 90 | 3 | No (abandoned) | – | | 4 | 50 | 0 | Yes | 22 | | 5 | 80 | 2 | Yes | 25 | | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ### 3.2.1 Derivation of Task Completion Rate (TCR) for Task 2 $$ \begin{aligned} TCR_{T2} &= \frac{\text{Users who completed Task 2}}{\text{Total users who attempted Task 2}} \times 100\% \\[4pt] &= \frac{4}{5} \times 100\% \\[4pt] &= 80\% \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** 80% completion falls into the *acceptable* range for consumer apps. The 20% failure (User 3 abandoned) triggers a *heuristic inspection* of the payment flow. ### 3.2.2 Derivation of Mean Time-on-Task for Task 1 $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{T}_{T1} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{T1,i}}{n} \\[4pt] &= \frac{45 + 60 + 90 + 50 + 80}{5} \\[4pt] &= \frac{325}{5} \\[4pt] &= 65 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Mean of 65 s suggests an *acceptable learnability* profile for a first-time task, but the high *variance* indicates inconsistent user experiences. ### 3.2.3 Derivation of Standard Deviation (Consistency Indicator) $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{T1} &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_i - \bar{T}_{T1})^{2}} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{5} \left[ (45-65)^{2} + (60-65)^{2} + (90-65)^{2} + (50-65)^{2} + (80-65)^{2} \right]} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{5} \left[ 400 + 25 + 625 + 225 + 225 \right]} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{\frac{1500}{5}} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{300} \\[4pt] &\approx 17.32 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** $\sigma \approx 17.32$ s relative to $\bar{T} = 65$ s gives a **Coefficient of Variation (CoV) = 26.6%**, indicating moderate inconsistency. KTU examiners expect students to discuss *why high variance signals poor learnability*. ### 3.2.4 Derivation of Error Rate $E_r$ for Task 1 $$ \begin{aligned} E_r &= \frac{\sum \text{Errors}}{\text{Total attempts}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{0 + 1 + 3 + 0 + 2}{5} \\[4pt] &= \frac{6}{5} \\[4pt] &= 1.2 \text{ errors per attempt} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** An error rate of 1.2 per attempt is **high** for a registration flow. This violates the *Low Error Rate* goal and demands a redesign of input validation. ### 3.2.5 Derivation of Efficiency $\eta$ for Task 3 $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{T3} &= \frac{\text{Tasks completed}}{\text{Total time spent (seconds)}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{4}{20 + 18 + 22 + 25} \\[4pt] &= \frac{4}{85} \\[4pt] &\approx 0.047 \text{ tasks per second} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Equivalent to ~2.82 tasks per minute, suggesting a streamlined *result-check* flow (the app likely places this feature on the home screen). ## 3.3 Decision Matrix: Which Goal to Prioritize? The KTU 2024 examiner often frames a 14-mark question: *"Given context X, which goal of evaluation is most important and why?"* The decision matrix is: | **User Profile** | **Usage Frequency** | **Risk Level** | **Primary Goal** | |---|---|---|---| | Novice | Daily | Low | **Learnability** | | Novice | Weekly | High | **Learnability + Low Errors** | | Expert | Daily | High | **Efficiency + Low Errors** | | Casual | Yearly | Low | **Memorability** | | Any | Any | High (medical/aviation) | **Low Errors** > all | | Consumer | Daily | Low | **Satisfaction** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Map — The Five Nielsen Usability Goals ```mermaid flowchart TD A[USABILITY EVALUATION GOALS] --> B[USER PERFORMANCE GOALS] A --> C[USER EXPERIENCE GOALS] A --> D[ORGANIZATIONAL GOALS] B --> B1[LEARNABILITY] B --> B2[EFFICIENCY] B --> B3[MEMORABILITY] B --> B4[LOW ERROR RATE] B --> B5[SATISFACTION] B1 --> M1[Time on first task] B2 --> M2[Tasks per minute] B3 --> M3[Relearning time] B4 --> M4[Errors per task] B5 --> M5[SUS Score] C --> C1[Enjoyable] C --> C2[Engaging] C --> C3[Aesthetically Pleasing] D --> D1[Reduced Training Cost] D --> D2[Increased Productivity] D --> D3[Improved Sales] D --> D4[Reduced Support Calls] ``` ## 4.2 GQM (Goal-Question-Metric) Operationalization Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR G1[DEFINE GOAL
What to evaluate and why] --> Q1[REFINE QUESTIONS
Ask measurable questions] Q1 --> M1[DEFINE METRICS
Numerical indicators] M1 --> C1[COLLECT DATA
User testing, logs, surveys] C1 --> A1[ANALYSE
Compare against benchmark] A1 --> R1[REPORT
Actionable redesign insights] R1 --> D1[REDESIGN
Iterate the interface] D1 --> G1 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Evaluation Goal ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[START: New UI to Evaluate] --> S1{Is user a first-timer?} S1 -- YES --> S2{Priority: Speed of learning?} S1 -- NO --> S3{Is task repeated often?} S2 -- YES --> S4[PRIORITIZE: LEARNABILITY] S2 -- NO --> S5[PRIORITIZE: SATISFACTION] S3 -- YES --> S6{Is safety critical?} S3 -- NO --> S7[PRIORITIZE: MEMORABILITY] S6 -- YES --> S8[PRIORITIZE: LOW ERROR RATE] S6 -- NO --> S9[PRIORITIZE: EFFICIENCY] S4 --> S10[Methods: Think-aloud, SUS, Heuristic Eval] S5 --> S10 S7 --> S10 S8 --> S10 S9 --> S10 ``` ## 4.4 Layered Architecture: From Goal to Practice ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[LEVEL 1: PHILOSOPHY
User-Centred Design] --> L2[LEVEL 2: GOALS
Learnability, Efficiency, Memorability, Errors, Satisfaction] L2 --> L3[LEVEL 3: HEURISTICS
Shneiderman Rules, Nielsens 10] L3 --> L4[LEVEL 4: METHODS
User Testing, Inspection, Analytics] L4 --> L5[LEVEL 5: METRICS
Time, Errors, SUS, Completion Rate] L5 --> L6[LEVEL 6: DECISIONS
Redesign, Ship, Reject] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Mistake 1:** Conflating *Goals of Evaluation* with *Evaluation Methods*. *Goals* = *what to measure*; *Methods* = *how to measure*. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this confusion. > 2. **Mistake 2:** Listing Nielsen's goals without giving a *contextual justification* (who, what, where). Always tie the goal to the *user profile* and *context of use*. > 3. **Mistake 3:** Writing only the *definition* of SUS without giving the *score range* (0–100) and *benchmark* (>68 = above average). A 1–2 mark deduction is common. > 4. **Mistake 4:** Ignoring *User Experience (UX) goals*. KTU 2024 scheme explicitly includes UX goals (enjoyable, engaging) alongside usability goals. --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme] **"Differentiate between usability goals and user experience goals in HCI."** **Model Answer (Structured for 3 Marks):** | **Aspect** | **Usability Goals** | **User Experience (UX) Goals** | |---|---|---| | **Focus** | Task effectiveness, efficiency, learnability | Emotional response, enjoyment, engagement | | **Nature** | Objective, measurable | Subjective, affective | | **Metrics** | Time, errors, completion rate | Satisfaction surveys, smiley ratings, NPS | | **Examples** | *Learnability, Efficiency, Memorability* | *Fun, Aesthetically Pleasing, Motivating* | | **Question Asked** | "Can the user complete the task?" | "Did the user enjoy doing the task?" | *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for example metrics.)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme] **"List Nielsen's five usability attributes and explain any two briefly."** **Model Answer (3-Mark Structure):** 1. **Learnability** (1⁄2 mark) — Ease with which new users can begin productive work. 2. **Efficiency** (1⁄2 mark) — Speed of task execution once learned. 3. **Memorability** (1⁄2 mark) — Ease of re-establishing proficiency after a break. 4. **Low Error Rate** (1⁄2 mark) — Frequency and severity of errors. 5. **Satisfaction** (1⁄2 mark) — Subjective pleasantness of the design. **Brief explanations (1 mark total):** - *Learnability:* A user opening the IRCTC app for the first time should be able to book a ticket in under 5 minutes with no training. - *Low Error Rate:* In a hospital infusion pump, an error rate above zero is unacceptable; the system must *prevent* incorrect dosages. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme] **Q: (a)** Explain in detail Nielsen's five usability goals. For each goal, provide one *real-world example* and the *metric* used to measure it. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss how Shneiderman's eight golden rules of interface design support the goals of evaluation. Illustrate with at least three examples of mobile applications. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Nielsen's Five Usability Goals:** 1. **Learnability (1 Mark + Example 1⁄2 Mark)** - *Definition:* Ease with which novice users can accomplish basic tasks on first exposure. - *Example:* WhatsApp allows a first-time user to send a message within 60 seconds of opening the app, thanks to its minimalist three-tab navigation. - *Metric:* Time to first successful task completion; tutorial drop-off rate. 2. **Efficiency (1 Mark + Example 1⁄2 Mark)** - *Definition:* Speed at which experienced users can perform tasks. - *Example:* Power users of Microsoft Excel use keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+C, Ctrl+V, Alt+=) to perform operations 3–5× faster than mouse-only users. - *Metric:* Tasks per minute; time per task after learning plateau. 3. **Memorability (1 Mark + Example 1⁄2 Mark)** - *Definition:* Ability of casual users to remember how to use the system after a period of non-use. - *Example:* Google Search's single text box has remained unchanged for 20+ years — a deliberate memorability strategy. - *Metric:* Time to regain prior performance after a 1-week, 1-month break. 4. **Low Error Rate (1 Mark + Example 1⁄2 Mark)** - *Definition:* Number, severity, and recoverability of user errors. - *Example:* Gmail's "Undo Send" feature prevents the catastrophic error of sending an email to the wrong recipient. - *Metric:* Errors per task; error recovery time; catastrophic-to-minor error ratio. 5. **Satisfaction (1 Mark + Example 1⁄2 Mark)** - *Definition:* Subjective pleasantness of using the system. - *Example:* Spotify's personalized playlists create an emotionally engaging experience, driving high NPS scores. - *Metric:* System Usability Scale (SUS) score (0–100); Net Promoter Score (NPS). > [!TIP] > **Examiner's insight:** Award **1⁄2 mark** for definition, **1⁄2 mark** for example, and **1⁄2 mark** for metric. Ensure 5 goals × ~1.4 marks = 7 marks. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules mapped to Evaluation Goals:** | **Rule** | **Supported Goal** | **Mobile App Example** | |---|---|---| | 1. Strive for consistency | Learnability | In Instagram, the heart icon always means "like" across feed, stories, and reels. | | 2. Enable shortcuts | Efficiency | Swiping down in Gmail refreshes the inbox — a fast expert gesture. | | 3. Informative feedback | Low Error Rate, Satisfaction | "Message sent" checkmark in WhatsApp confirms delivery. | | 4. Design dialogs to yield closure | Satisfaction | UPI payment apps show a clear success/failure screen with a tick or cross. | | 5. Offer simple error handling | Low Error Rate | Auto-correct in keyboard apps underlines misspelt words in red. | | 6. Permit easy reversal | Low Error Rate, Satisfaction | Gmail's "Undo Send" within 30 seconds. | | 7. Support internal locus of control | Satisfaction, Efficiency | Volume buttons on YouTube give immediate, predictable response. | | 8. Reduce short-term memory load | Learnability, Memorability | Icons + labels in Apple Control Center use recognition, not recall. | **Conclusion (1 Mark):** Shneiderman's rules act as *operational heuristics* that translate the abstract goals of evaluation (Nielsen) into *concrete, testable design choices*. Each rule is independently verifiable through a heuristic evaluation, and the eight rules collectively support all five usability goals. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often *list* the 8 rules without *mapping* them to evaluation goals. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for missing the linkage. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme] **Q: (a)** Describe the Goal-Question-Metric (GQM) approach. Apply it to evaluate the *learnability* of a university online examination portal. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast *formative* and *summative* evaluation. State which type best supports the goals of evaluation in an iterative design cycle, with justification. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **1. GQM Approach (3 Marks)** The GQM model, developed by Basili, Caldiera, and Rombach, is a top-down measurement framework. It has three layers: - **Goal (G):** Define *what* to evaluate, *why*, for *whom*, in *which context*. - **Question (Q):** Refine the goal into measurable questions. - **Metric (M):** Define data elements to answer each question. **2. Application: Learnability of University Online Exam Portal (4 Marks)** | **G/Q/M Level** | **Content** | |---|---| | **Goal** | Evaluate the *learnability* of the KTU online exam portal for *first-time* B.Tech students during the *end-semester* exam context. | | **Question 1** | How long does a first-time student take to log in, navigate to the exam, and submit answers? | | **Question 2** | How many students encounter errors (wrong password, broken navigation, time-out) on the first attempt? | | **Question 3** | What percentage of students require external help (chat, helpline) to complete the first exam? | | **Metric 1** | $T_{first-task}$ in minutes | | **Metric 2** | $E_r$ = errors per first session | | **Metric 3** | Help-seeking rate = % of students calling support | | **Benchmark** | $T_{first-task} \leq 10$ min, $E_r \leq 0.5$, help rate $\leq 10\%$ | --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Comparison Table (4 Marks):** | **Aspect** | **Formative Evaluation** | **Summative Evaluation** | |---|---|---| | **When** | During design & development | After design completion | | **Purpose** | Identify problems to *form* the design | Validate and *summarize* overall quality | | **Frequency** | Continuous, iterative | Once, at the end | | **Methods** | Think-aloud, heuristic walkthroughs, paper prototypes | A/B testing, benchmark tests, field deployment | | **Audience** | Design team | Stakeholders, management, end-users | | **Output** | Specific bug list, redesign suggestions | Final usability score, certification, ROI | **Best Type for Iterative Design (3 Marks):** *Formative evaluation* is the most aligned with the goals of evaluation in an iterative design cycle. Justification: 1. It provides *early feedback* before costly implementation. 2. It *informs* redesign — directly feeding into the *Iterative Design* loop (Design → Prototype → Evaluate → Redesign). 3. It supports *all five* of Nielsen's goals incrementally. 4. *Summative* evaluation is *terminal* — it can only confirm or refute, not improve the product mid-cycle. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** A common error is to claim *summative* is "best" because it is "final." Examiners explicitly look for *justification linking* formative evaluation to the *iterative design cycle*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Goal vs Method:** Goals = *what* to measure (Learnability, Efficiency, Memorability, Errors, Satisfaction). Methods = *how* (think-aloud, heuristic, SUS, A/B test). - **Nielsen's Mnemonic:** **L-MEES** (Learnability, Memorability, Efficiency, Errors, Satisfaction). - **Three Hierarchical Tiers:** (1) Usability goals → (2) UX goals → (3) Organizational goals. - **GQM Paradigm:** Goal → Question → Metric — a *top-down* measurement approach. - **Formative vs Summative:** Formative = *during* (drives redesign); Summative = *after* (validates product). - **SUS Score Benchmark:** > 68 = above average; < 50 = severe usability issues. - **Shneiderman's Rules:** Consistency, Shortcuts, Feedback, Closure, Error handling, Reversal, Locus of control, Reduce memory load. - **Context-Dependence:** The *priority* of each goal depends on *user expertise*, *usage frequency*, and *risk severity*. - **Safety-Critical Systems:** Errors > Efficiency > Satisfaction (priority order in medical/aviation contexts). - **Quantitative vs Qualitative:** Nielsen's goals are operationalized *quantitatively* (time, errors, scores); UX goals are *qualitative* (enjoyment, aesthetics). - **Iterative Loop:** Goals of evaluation *drive* the design cycle — evaluation is not a one-time terminal event. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always structure 14-mark answers as: (i) Definition (2 marks), (ii) Detailed explanation with examples (3 marks), (iii) Diagram / Table (1 mark), (iv) Justification / Application (1 mark). This guarantees full marks.

topics.title: Evaluation Through Expert Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evaluation Through Expert Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Evaluation Through Expert Analysis?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Evaluation Through Expert Analysis* refers to a family of **discount usability inspection methods** in which trained specialists (not end users) systematically examine, judge, and diagnose the usability of a user interface by applying recognized principles, heuristics, structured walkthroughs, and analytical frameworks — *without* involving representative users.

In simple terms, it is a **"simulated usage"** approach: instead of asking real users to perform tasks on the interface, we ask domain experts to play the role of the user and predict what would go wrong, based on their knowledge of **human cognition, design guidelines, and usability principles**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Building Inspector**
> Imagine an old apartment building. Before tenants move in, the city sends a *building inspector* — not the future residents. The inspector doesn't *live* in the building, but walks through every room with a checklist (electrical wiring, plumbing, fire exits, ventilation). 
> 
> Similarly, an **HCI expert evaluator** walks through every screen of the software with a structured set of usability rules (heuristics) and reports violations — *before real users ever touch the product*.
> 
> The inspector is **cheap, fast, and predictable**; tenants (users) testing it would be **slower, costlier, and inconsistent**.

## 1.3 Classification of Expert Analysis Methods

The four principal methods covered in the KTU Module 4 syllabus are:

| # | Method | Core Idea | Evaluator Role |
|---|--------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Heuristic Evaluation** | Apply Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics | Judge against rules |
| 2 | **Cognitive Walkthrough** | Simulate a user's problem-solving steps | Predict learnability |
| 3 | **Pluralistic Walkthrough** | Group walkthrough with mixed stakeholders | Discuss collaboratively |
| 4 | **Feature/Consistency Inspection** | Compare features with prior versions/standards | Detect inconsistencies |

## 1.4 Why We Use Expert Analysis (Key Advantages)

> [!TIP]
> - **Low cost** — no participant recruitment, no lab setup
> - **Speed** — a single evaluator can complete an inspection in a few hours
> - **Early detection** — usable during design phase, before any code is written
> - **Diagnostic depth** — experts can explain *why* a problem is a problem
> - **Ethical safety** — no risk of exposing users to broken/bad designs

## 1.5 When to Use (and Not Use)

> [!WARNING]
> **Use Expert Analysis When:**
> - The design is in its early/medium stages
> - Time, budget, or participants are limited
> - You need quick, broad coverage of usability issues
> 
> **Do NOT Rely Solely On It When:**
> - The task is novel, complex, or unfamiliar to the evaluator
> - Real-world context, workflow, or domain knowledge is critical
> - The interface is highly innovative (no heuristics apply)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1: Heuristic Evaluation (Jakob Nielsen, 1990)

### 2.1.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heuristic Evaluation** is a systematic inspection method in which a small set of evaluators (typically **3 to 5**) examines the interface and judges its compliance against a list of recognized **usability heuristics** (rules of thumb). Each evaluator works **independently**, then findings are aggregated.

### 2.1.2 Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics (High-Yield — KTU Examiner Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> The following 10 heuristics are **mandatory** for KTU answers. Memorize them by name and meaning.

1. **Visibility of System Status** — The system should always keep users informed about what is going on, through appropriate feedback within reasonable time. *(e.g., progress bars, loading icons)*
2. **Match Between System and the Real World** — Speak the users' language; follow real-world conventions. *(e.g., shopping cart icon, not "SKU-147 container")*
3. **User Control and Freedom** — Support undo/redo and clear "emergency exits." *(e.g., Cancel button, Back arrow)*
4. **Consistency and Standards** — Same words, situations, actions should mean the same thing. Follow platform conventions.
5. **Error Prevention** — Prevent problems from occurring in the first place. *(e.g., confirmation dialogs before destructive actions)*
6. **Recognition Rather than Recall** — Minimize the user's memory load. Make objects, actions, and options visible.
7. **Flexibility and Efficiency of Use** — Accelerators (shortcuts) for expert users; allow customization.
8. **Aesthetic and Minimalist Design** — Interfaces should not contain irrelevant or rarely-needed information.
9. **Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors** — Error messages in plain language, indicate the problem, suggest a solution.
10. **Help and Documentation** — Provide easy-to-search, task-focused help.

### 2.1.3 Severity Rating Scale (Nielsen)

Each found problem is rated on a **5-point Severity Scale**:

| Rating | Severity Level | Description |
|:------:|:--------------:|:-----------|
| 0 | **Not a usability problem** | Cosmetic only |
| 1 | **Cosmetic** | Need not be fixed unless extra time available |
| 2 | **Minor** | Low priority fix |
| 3 | **Major** | High priority — must be fixed |
| 4 | **Catastrophe** | Imperative to fix before release |

### 2.1.4 Procedure (Step-by-Step Logical Flow)

1. **Brief the evaluators** on the heuristics and the system.
2. **Each evaluator independently** performs 2–3 passes through the interface.
3. **First pass** — get a feel for the system and its scope.
4. **Second pass** — focus on specific interface elements.
5. **Third pass (optional)** — verify earlier findings.
6. **List violations** with reference to the violated heuristic.
7. **Rate severity** of each violation.
8. **Aggregate findings** from all evaluators.
9. **Debrief** to discuss and consolidate.

---

## 2.2 Method 2: Cognitive Walkthrough (Lewis & Wharton, 1997)

### 2.2.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Cognitive Walkthrough** is an expert-based inspection method that focuses on **learnability** — it simulates a user's cognitive process as they attempt to perform a task *for the first time*, evaluating whether the user can successfully *discover*, *interpret*, and *act* on the interface.

### 2.2.2 The Four Core Questions (Asked at Every Step)

For each action the user takes, the evaluator asks:

1. **Will the user try to achieve the right effect?** *(Goal formation)*
2. **Will the user notice that the correct action is available?** *(Action discovery)*
3. **Will the user associate the correct action with the effect?** *(Action interpretation)*
4. **Will the user see that they are making progress toward the solution?** *(Feedback / progress)*

> [!TIP]
> If the answer to **any** of these is *No*, then a usability problem exists at that step.

### 2.2.3 Required Inputs for a Cognitive Walkthrough

- A **prototype or description** of the interface
- A **description of the typical users** (knowledge, experience)
- A **set of representative tasks** (scenarios)
- A **list of action sequences** required to complete each task

### 2.2.4 Strengths & Weaknesses

| Strengths | Weaknesses |
|:----------|:-----------|
| Excellent for **learnability** | Time-consuming to set up |
| Predicts first-time use problems | Requires detailed task descriptions |
| No users needed | Assumes evaluator can simulate cognition accurately |
| Useful early in design | Not good for experienced/expert users |

---

## 2.3 Method 3: Pluralistic Walkthrough (Bias, 1994)

### 2.3.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Pluralistic Walkthrough** is a group-based evaluation method that brings together **representatives from multiple stakeholder groups** — developers, users, usability experts, product managers — and walks them through representative tasks *together*, step by step, discussing usability issues openly.

### 2.3.2 Key Features

- **Group session**, typically **1–2 hours**
- **3 or more stakeholder groups** represented
- **Step-by-step discussion** of each action in a task
- **Written record** of issues raised
- Heavily **participatory** — surfaces mismatches in mental models

### 2.3.3 When to Use

- Early in design when **consensus** is needed
- When **stakeholder alignment** is critical
- When domain knowledge is complex

---

## 2.4 Method 4: Feature & Consistency Inspection

### 2.4.1 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Feature Inspection** checks that the application's features support the *core tasks* identified during requirements analysis. **Consistency Inspection** compares the current interface to other parts of the same product, prior versions, or industry standards.

### 2.4.2 The Two Sub-Questions

1. *Does the feature exist that supports this task?*
2. *Is the feature used consistently across the product and platform?*

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Expert Analysis Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following compact summary covers the high-yield **quantitative metrics** used in expert-based evaluation (KTU Module 4 weightage).

| Metric | Formula | Purpose | Typical Range |
|:-------|:--------|:--------|:--------------|
| **Number of Evaluators (N)** | $N \in [3, 5]$ | Optimal range to catch most issues | 3–5 |
| **Problem Detection Rate (PDR)** | $PDR = \dfrac{N_{\text{seen by at least 1}}}{N_{\text{total problems}}}$ | Proportion of issues any evaluator can spot | Single: $\approx 35\%$ |
| **Discount Factor** | N/A | Expert methods sacrifice realism for cost | High |
| **Cost Multiplier vs User Test** | $\approx 0.1 \times$ | Expert eval is ~10× cheaper | 5–10% of user test |
| **Inter-rater Reliability (Cohen's $\kappa$)** | $\kappa = \dfrac{P_o - P_e}{1 - P_e}$ | Agreement between evaluators | $\geq 0.6$ acceptable |
| **Severity Index (SI)** | $SI = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Severity}_i \times N_i)}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Aggregate severity score | 0–4 |
| **Cost-Benefit Ratio (CBR)** | $CBR = \dfrac{N_{\text{issues found}}}{\text{Cost} \times \text{Time}}$ | Efficiency of the method | Higher = better |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, vertical bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5.1 Nielsen's Empirical Law of Evaluators

> [!TIP]
> A single evaluator finds only ~35% of total usability problems. With **5 evaluators**, the detection rate approaches **~85%**, after which diminishing returns set in. This is the **classic justification** for using **3–5 evaluators** in Heuristic Evaluation.

This can be approximated as:

$$P(N) \;=\; 1 - (1 - p_1)^{N}$$

Where:
- $P(N)$ = cumulative probability of finding a problem with $N$ evaluators
- $p_1$ = probability that a *single* evaluator finds the problem ($\approx 0.35$)
- $N$ = number of evaluators

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Industry | Application of Expert Analysis |
|:---------|:-------------------------------|
| **Avionics / Automotive** | Pre-flight HMI inspection of cockpit / dashboard displays |
| **Medical Devices** | IEC 62366 compliance — formative evaluation of infusion pump UIs |
| **Banking / FinTech** | Audit of mobile banking apps for ISO 9241 compliance |
| **Government / Defence** | Inspection of mission-critical operator consoles |
| **Gaming** | Pre-launch UX/UI audit before playtesting |
| **IoT / Embedded** | Inspection of appliance/control panel interfaces |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Nielsen's Problem Detection Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show *why* **3–5 evaluators** is the optimal team size for Heuristic Evaluation.

### Step 1: State the Assumption

Assume that each evaluator has an **independent** probability $p_1$ of detecting a given usability problem. From Nielsen's 1993 empirical study, the single-evaluator detection rate is:

$$p_1 = 0.35$$

### Step 2: Probability a Single Evaluator MISSES a Problem

The probability that *one* evaluator fails to find the problem is:

$$q_1 = 1 - p_1 = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$$

### Step 3: Probability ALL N Evaluators MISS the Problem

Since the evaluators are **independent**, the probability that *all* $N$ evaluators miss a particular problem is the product of individual miss probabilities:

$$q_{\text{all}}(N) = (q_1)^{N} = (0.65)^{N}$$

### Step 4: Probability That AT LEAST ONE Evaluator Detects It

Therefore, the probability that the problem is detected by **at least one** evaluator is:

$$P(N) = 1 - q_{\text{all}}(N) = 1 - (0.65)^{N}$$

### Step 5: Substitute $q_1$ Generically

Writing it with the symbolic miss probability $q_1$:

$$\boxed{P(N) = 1 - (1 - p_1)^{N}}$$

### Step 6: Evaluate for Increasing N

Plug in $p_1 = 0.35$ and compute the cumulative detection rate:

| $N$ (Evaluators) | $P(N) = 1 - (0.65)^{N}$ | Approx. % |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $1 - 0.65$ | 35.00% |
| 2 | $1 - 0.4225$ | 57.75% |
| 3 | $1 - 0.2746$ | 72.54% |
| 4 | $1 - 0.1785$ | 82.15% |
| 5 | $1 - 0.1160$ | 88.40% |
| 6 | $1 - 0.0754$ | 92.46% |
| 7 | $1 - 0.0490$ | 95.10% |
| 10 | $1 - 0.0135$ | 98.65% |

### Step 7: Marginal Benefit Analysis

The **marginal gain** from the $(N+1)$-th evaluator is:

$$\Delta P(N) = P(N+1) - P(N) = (0.65)^{N} - (0.65)^{N+1} = (0.65)^{N} \cdot (0.35)$$

| $N$ | $\Delta P(N)$ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 1 → 2 | 22.75% |
| 2 → 3 | 14.79% |
| 3 → 4 | 9.61% |
| 4 → 5 | 6.25% |
| 5 → 6 | 4.06% |

### Step 8: Conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marginal gains drop sharply after the 3rd evaluator**, and become marginal beyond the 5th. Hence, the **cost-effective optimal team size is 3–5 evaluators** — a KTU-board-approved answer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Inter-Rater Reliability (Cohen's $\kappa$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Quantify agreement between two evaluators on whether a usability problem exists.

### Step 1: Define Variables

Let:
- $P_o$ = observed agreement (proportion of cases both evaluators agree)
- $P_e$ = expected agreement by chance alone

### Step 2: Build the 2×2 Contingency Table (Conceptual)

| | Evaluator B: Yes | Evaluator B: No | Row Total |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Evaluator A: Yes** | $a$ | $b$ | $a+b$ |
| **Evaluator A: No** | $c$ | $d$ | $c+d$ |
| **Col Total** | $a+c$ | $b+d$ | $N = a+b+c+d$ |

### Step 3: Compute Observed Agreement

$$P_o = \frac{a + d}{N}$$

### Step 4: Compute Expected Agreement

$$P_e = \frac{(a+b)(a+c) + (c+d)(b+d)}{N^{2}}$$

### Step 5: Compute $\kappa$

$$\boxed{\kappa = \frac{P_o - P_e}{1 - P_e}}$$

### Step 6: Interpretation Scale (Landis & Koch)

| $\kappa$ Value | Strength of Agreement |
|:---:|:---|
| $< 0$ | Poor |
| 0.00 – 0.20 | Slight |
| 0.21 – 0.40 | Fair |
| 0.41 – 0.60 | Moderate |
| 0.61 – 0.80 | **Substantial (KTU target)** |
| 0.81 – 1.00 | Almost perfect |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Heuristic Eval vs. Cognitive Walkthrough

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is a **favourite KTU Part-B question** (12–14 marks).

| Dimension | Heuristic Evaluation | Cognitive Walkthrough |
|:----------|:-------------------|:---------------------|
| **Focus** | General usability violations | Learnability / first-time use |
| **Input Required** | Heuristics + Interface | Tasks + User profile + Interface |
| **Time Required** | Low (a few hours) | High (hours per task) |
| **Expertise Needed** | High (usability expert) | Moderate (cognitive psychology familiarity) |
| **Number of Evaluators** | 3–5 | 1–3 |
| **Output** | List of heuristic violations with severity | List of step-by-step cognitive failures |
| **Best for** | Polished prototypes, finished UIs | Early design, paper prototypes |
| **Cost** | Very low | Moderate |
| **Quantitative Metric** | Severity Index, Detection Rate | # of failed steps / total steps |
| **Founders** | Nielsen (1990) | Lewis & Wharton (1997) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Choosing Between Heuristic Eval and Cognitive Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A startup has built an MVP mobile banking app for first-time users (rural population with low digital literacy). They have **2 weeks** and **2 usability experts** available.

**Step 1 — Identify the primary concern:** First-time *learnability* for a novice user population.

**Step 2 — Map to method strengths:**
- *Heuristic Eval* → broad general usability check
- *Cognitive Walkthrough* → **explicitly designed for first-time use / learnability**

**Step 3 — Decision rule:**

$$\text{If focus} = \text{learnability} \Rightarrow \text{Cognitive Walkthrough}$$

$$\text{If focus} = \text{general usability audit} \Rightarrow \text{Heuristic Evaluation}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** Since the focus is first-time use, **run a Cognitive Walkthrough** *supplemented* by a quick Heuristic Evaluation (combined method).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Expert Evaluation Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block illustrates the **end-to-end flow** of an expert analysis evaluation, from preparation to final reporting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Preparation] --> B[Stage 2: Briefing]

    A2[Select 3-5 expert evaluators] -.-> A
    A3[Prepare prototype or screenshots] -.-> A

    B2[Walk through the system] -.-> B

    C3[Pass 2: Element-wise review] -.-> C
    C4[Pass 3: Verification] -.-> C

    E1[Rate 0 to 4 severity] -.-> E

    F3[Remove duplicates] -.-> F
    F4[Sort by severity] -.-> F

    G1[Discuss disagreements] -.-> G
    G2[Consolidate final list] -.-> G
    G3[Prioritize fixes] -.-> G

    A --- A2

```

## 4.2 Comparison Diagram: User-Based vs. Expert-Based Evaluation

```mermaid

    subgraph USER_BASED["USER-BASED METHODS"]
        U1[Think Aloud]
        U2[Field Observation]

    EXPERT_BASED -->|Low Cost, Fast| RESULT
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Expert Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Need Expert Evaluation] --> Q1{What is the primary goal?}
    Q1 -->|General usability audit| Q2{Is the design polished?}
    Q1 -->|Learnability check| CW[Cognitive Walkthrough]
    Q1 -->|Stakeholder alignment| PW[Pluralistic Walkthrough]
    Q1 -->|Check feature completeness| FI[Feature Inspection]
```
